title: Peer Institution Comparison
text:
After this lesson, you will be able to:

Construct a peer institution framework using objective criteria (AAU, Big Ten, Carnegie classification)
Generate and structure simulated peer salary data for benchmarking
Apply cost-of-living adjustments using regional price parities
Calculate salary percentile rankings relative to peers
Build radar/spider charts comparing multiple compensation dimensions
Identify fields where competitive position is eroding
Create bump charts and Cleveland dot plots for peer rankings
Locate real-world data sources for institutional salary comparison

title: Peer Institution Comparison
text:
Introduction
Salary data at a single institution only tells part of the story. To understand whether UW-Madison compensation is competitive, you need to benchmark against peer institutions—schools that compete for the same talent. This lesson builds a complete peer comparison workflow, from defining the peer group through visualization of competitive position.

What Makes a Good Peer Group?
Peer groups should be constructed from institutions that genuinely compete for the same faculty and staff. Common criteria include:

AAU Membership: The Association of American Universities includes 71 leading research institutions. UW-Madison is a founding member.
Big Ten Conference: Athletic conference peers share geographic and cultural similarities, and frequently recruit from the same talent pool (Illinois, Michigan, Ohio State, Penn State, Minnesota, etc.)
Carnegie Classification: R1 (Very High Research Activity) institutions share similar research missions and faculty expectations.
Research Expenditure: NSF HERD survey rankings identify schools with comparable research portfolios (typically $800M+ for UW-Madison peers).
State Flagship Status: Other flagship state universities face similar public-sector compensation constraints.

A good peer group balances aspirational peers (where you want to be) with competitive peers (who you actually lose candidates to). Most institutions use 10–15 peers for salary benchmarking.


The Politics and Strategy of Peer Selection
Peer groups are as much political as they are analytical. Understanding the dynamics helps you build more effective analyses:
How peer groups are chosen (and contested):

At the UW System level, peer groups are established by the Board of Regents and reviewed periodically. UW-Madison’s peers include other Big Ten and AAU flagship institutions.
Aspirational peers (e.g., Michigan, Berkeley) make the salary gap look larger, supporting arguments for more funding. Regional peers (e.g., Minnesota, Iowa) may show a smaller gap but represent the institutions you most directly compete with for talent.
Departments and colleges sometimes contest the institutional peer list, arguing that their discipline-specific labor market doesn’t match the overall peers. A Computer Science department competes with Stanford and CMU for faculty regardless of the institutional peer list.

Key data sources for real peer comparisons:

AAUP Faculty Compensation Survey: Annual data on faculty salaries by rank and institution type. The gold standard for faculty benchmarking.
CUPA-HR Surveys: Comprehensive data on staff and administrator compensation, with job-level matching.
IPEDS (Integrated Postsecondary Education Data System): Free, publicly available data on faculty salaries, but less granular than AAUP or CUPA-HR.
NSF HERD Survey: Research expenditure data useful for identifying research-comparable peers.

Building a competitiveness narrative: The most effective peer analyses tell a story: “UW-Madison faculty salaries ranked 8th among Big Ten peers in 2015 and have fallen to 12th by 2024. In the three fields where we’ve lost the most faculty (CS, Statistics, Finance), our salary percentile rank has dropped below the 25th percentile of peers.” This narrative connects data to the decision: “We request a $3M targeted investment to restore competitiveness in our highest-attrition fields.”
When presenting peer data to the Board of Regents or legislative committees, always include cost-of-living adjustments (as taught in this lesson). Raw comparisons to California or New York peers are immediately discounted by decision-makers who know about cost differences. Adjusted comparisons are credible comparisons.

title: Peer Institution Comparison
section: Setting Up and Generating Peer Data
text:
Setting Up and Generating Peer Data
In practice you would pull from IPEDS, AAUP, or CUPA-HR. For this lesson we simulate realistic salary data for 10 Big Ten and peer AAU institutions.

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots

# Wong colorblind-safe palette
WONG = ["#E69F00", "#56B4E9", "#009E73", "#F0E442",
        "#0072B2", "#D55E00", "#CC79A7"]

np.random.seed(42)

# Peer institutions with realistic salary multipliers relative to UW-Madison
# Multiplier reflects overall faculty salary competitiveness
peers = {
    "UW-Madison":      {"multiplier": 1.00, "col_index": 0.95, "region": "Midwest"},
    "U of Michigan":   {"multiplier": 1.12, "col_index": 0.97, "region": "Midwest"},
    "Ohio State":      {"multiplier": 1.01, "col_index": 0.88, "region": "Midwest"},
    "Penn State":      {"multiplier": 1.03, "col_index": 0.93, "region": "Northeast"},
    "U of Minnesota":  {"multiplier": 0.98, "col_index": 0.96, "region": "Midwest"},
    "U of Illinois":   {"multiplier": 1.05, "col_index": 0.91, "region": "Midwest"},
    "Purdue":          {"multiplier": 0.96, "col_index": 0.87, "region": "Midwest"},
    "Indiana":         {"multiplier": 0.93, "col_index": 0.86, "region": "Midwest"},
    "U of Iowa":       {"multiplier": 0.94, "col_index": 0.88, "region": "Midwest"},
    "Michigan State":  {"multiplier": 0.97, "col_index": 0.92, "region": "Midwest"},
    "Northwestern":    {"multiplier": 1.22, "col_index": 1.05, "region": "Midwest"},
}

# UW-Madison baseline salaries by field (approximate medians)
fields = {
    "Business":         135000,
    "Engineering":      125000,
    "Computer Science": 140000,
    "Medicine":         160000,
    "Law":              145000,
    "Biological Sci":   90000,
    "Physical Sci":     95000,
    "Social Sciences":  85000,
    "Humanities":       75000,
    "Education":        78000,
}

# Generate salary records for each institution and field
records = []
for inst, params in peers.items():
    for field, base_salary in fields.items():
        # Apply institution multiplier with field-specific noise
        field_noise = np.random.normal(1.0, 0.04)
        median_salary = base_salary * params["multiplier"] * field_noise

        # Generate individual faculty salaries (n=30 per field per school)
        n_faculty = 30
        salaries = np.random.lognormal(
            mean=np.log(median_salary) - 0.5 * 0.15**2,
            sigma=0.15,
            size=n_faculty
        )
        salaries = np.clip(salaries, 40000, 500000)

        for sal in salaries:
            records.append({
                "institution": inst,
                "field": field,
                "salary": round(sal, 0),
                "region": params["region"],
                "col_index": params["col_index"],
            })

df = pd.DataFrame(records)
print(f"Total records: {len(df):,}")
print(f"Institutions: {df['institution'].nunique()}")
print(f"Fields: {df['field'].nunique()}")
print(f"\nMedian salary by institution:")
print(df.groupby("institution")["salary"].median().sort_values(ascending=False).to_string())







TipIn R





In R you might generate this data with tidyverse:
library(tidyverse)

peers <- tribble(
  ~institution,     ~multiplier, ~col_index,
  "UW-Madison",     1.00,        0.95,
  "U of Michigan",  1.12,        0.97,
  # ...
)

salary_data <- peers %>%
  crossing(tibble(field = names(fields), base = unlist(fields))) %>%
  rowwise() %>%
  mutate(salary = rlnorm(1, log(base * multiplier), 0.15)) %>%
  ungroup()
The crossing() function creates the full grid, similar to our nested Python loops.

title: Peer Institution Comparison
text:
Cost-of-Living Normalization
Raw salary comparisons are misleading because $100,000 in Madison, WI buys more than $100,000 in Ann Arbor, MI or Evanston, IL. We adjust using Regional Price Parities (RPPs) or metro-area CPI-U indices.

Regional Price Parities (RPPs)
The Bureau of Economic Analysis publishes RPPs that measure price-level differences across states and metro areas. An RPP of 95 means prices are 5% below the national average. Key facts:

National average = 100
Madison, WI metro area RPP is roughly 95 (cost of living 5% below national average)
The col_index in our data simulates RPPs divided by 100
Adjusted salary = Nominal salary / COL index normalizes to a common purchasing power basis
BEA publishes RPPs annually with a ~2 year lag

Alternative sources: C2ER Cost of Living Index (quarterly, metro-level), ACCRA data.


# Adjust salaries for cost of living
# col_index < 1.0 means cheaper than national average
# Dividing by col_index makes cheaper-area salaries look LARGER (more purchasing power)
df["salary_adj"] = df["salary"] / df["col_index"]

# Compare nominal vs adjusted medians
comparison = df.groupby("institution").agg(
    nominal_median=("salary", "median"),
    adjusted_median=("salary_adj", "median"),
    col_index=("col_index", "first")
).sort_values("adjusted_median", ascending=False)

comparison["rank_nominal"] = comparison["nominal_median"].rank(ascending=False).astype(int)
comparison["rank_adjusted"] = comparison["adjusted_median"].rank(ascending=False).astype(int)
comparison["rank_change"] = comparison["rank_nominal"] - comparison["rank_adjusted"]

print("Nominal vs COL-Adjusted Median Salaries:")
print(comparison[["nominal_median", "adjusted_median", "col_index",
                   "rank_nominal", "rank_adjusted", "rank_change"]]
      .round(0).to_string())


# Visualize the impact of COL adjustment
fig = go.Figure()

institutions = comparison.index.tolist()

fig.add_trace(go.Bar(
    x=institutions,
    y=comparison["nominal_median"],
    name="Nominal",
    marker_color=WONG[4],
    offsetgroup=0
))

fig.add_trace(go.Bar(
    x=institutions,
    y=comparison["adjusted_median"],
    name="COL-Adjusted",
    marker_color=WONG[2],
    offsetgroup=1
))

fig.update_layout(
    title="Nominal vs COL-Adjusted Median Faculty Salary",
    yaxis_title="Median Salary ($)",
    barmode="group",
    xaxis_tickangle=-30,
    legend=dict(x=0.75, y=0.95)
)

fig.show()







TipIn R





In R, mutate() with grouped operations makes this straightforward:
salary_data <- salary_data %>%
  mutate(salary_adj = salary / col_index)

comparison <- salary_data %>%
  group_by(institution) %>%
  summarise(
    nominal = median(salary),
    adjusted = median(salary_adj)
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    rank_nom = rank(-nominal),
    rank_adj = rank(-adjusted)
  )

title: Peer Institution Comparison
text:
Salary Percentile Benchmarking
Where does UW-Madison fall in the peer distribution for each field? Percentile ranking gives a direct answer.

# Calculate UW-Madison percentile rank by field
def percentile_rank(group, target="UW-Madison"):
    """Where does the target institution's median fall among peers?"""
    medians = group.groupby("institution")["salary_adj"].median()
    target_median = medians.get(target, np.nan)
    if pd.isna(target_median):
        return np.nan
    # Percentile: share of peers at or below target
    rank = (medians <= target_median).sum() / len(medians) * 100
    return round(rank, 1)

field_percentiles = (
    df.groupby("field")
    .apply(percentile_rank, include_groups=False)
    .reset_index()
)
field_percentiles.columns = ["field", "uw_percentile"]
field_percentiles = field_percentiles.sort_values("uw_percentile")

print("UW-Madison COL-Adjusted Salary Percentile Rank by Field:")
print(field_percentiles.to_string(index=False))


# Cleveland dot plot: UW-Madison percentile by field
fig = go.Figure()

colors = [WONG[5] if p < 30 else WONG[0] if p < 50 else WONG[2]
          for p in field_percentiles["uw_percentile"]]

fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=field_percentiles["uw_percentile"],
    y=field_percentiles["field"],
    mode="markers",
    marker=dict(size=14, color=colors, line=dict(width=1, color="black")),
    text=[f"{p}th percentile" for p in field_percentiles["uw_percentile"]],
    hovertemplate="%{y}: %{text}<extra></extra>"
))

# Add reference lines
fig.add_vline(x=50, line_dash="dash", line_color="gray",
              annotation_text="Median", annotation_position="top")
fig.add_vline(x=25, line_dash="dot", line_color=WONG[5],
              annotation_text="25th", annotation_position="bottom")

fig.update_layout(
    title="UW-Madison Salary Percentile Among Peers (COL-Adjusted)",
    xaxis_title="Percentile Rank",
    xaxis=dict(range=[0, 105]),
    yaxis_title="",
    height=450,
    showlegend=False
)

fig.show()


When building percentile calculations interactively in PyCharm, use the Python Console (Alt+F12) to test your groupby().apply() chains one step at a time. You can inspect intermediate DataFrames by selecting a variable and pressing Ctrl+Shift+Enter to evaluate it in the console.

title: Peer Institution Comparison
text:
Radar Charts: Multi-Dimensional Comparison
Salary is not the only dimension. We can compare institutions across multiple metrics using radar (spider) charts.

# Multi-dimensional institutional profiles (simulated index scores, 0-100)
np.random.seed(123)

dimensions = [
    "Median Salary", "Benefits Value", "Research Funding",
    "Teaching Load", "Cost of Living", "Quality of Life"
]

# Generate scores for a subset of peers
radar_institutions = ["UW-Madison", "U of Michigan", "Ohio State",
                      "U of Illinois", "Northwestern", "U of Minnesota"]

profiles = {}
base_scores = {
    "UW-Madison":     [55, 70, 75, 65, 78, 82],
    "U of Michigan":  [78, 72, 85, 60, 68, 70],
    "Ohio State":     [60, 65, 70, 62, 80, 72],
    "U of Illinois":  [65, 60, 72, 64, 82, 68],
    "Northwestern":   [88, 75, 82, 55, 52, 65],
    "U of Minnesota": [58, 68, 68, 66, 74, 75],
}

fig = go.Figure()

for i, (inst, scores) in enumerate(base_scores.items()):
    color = WONG[i % len(WONG)]
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatterpolar(
        r=scores + [scores[0]],  # Close the polygon
        theta=dimensions + [dimensions[0]],
        name=inst,
        line=dict(color=color, width=2),
        fill="toself" if inst == "UW-Madison" else "none",
        opacity=0.7 if inst == "UW-Madison" else 0.9,
    ))

fig.update_layout(
    polar=dict(
        radialaxis=dict(visible=True, range=[0, 100]),
    ),
    title="Multi-Dimensional Institutional Comparison",
    height=550,
    legend=dict(x=1.05, y=1.0)
)

fig.show()







TipIn R





R’s fmsb package creates radar charts, or you can use ggradar:
library(ggradar)

profiles_df <- tibble(
  group = names(base_scores),
  `Median Salary` = c(55, 78, 60, 65, 88, 58) / 100,
  `Benefits Value` = c(70, 72, 65, 60, 75, 68) / 100,
  # ... normalize to 0-1
)

ggradar(profiles_df, grid.max = 1, grid.mid = 0.5)
Plotly’s Scatterpolar is more interactive and handles the polygon closing automatically when you repeat the first point.

title: Peer Institution Comparison
text:
Competitive Position Analysis
Which fields are we losing ground in? We can identify vulnerability by comparing UW-Madison’s position to peers in specific disciplines.

# Field-level comparison: UW-Madison vs peer medians
uw_medians = (df[df["institution"] == "UW-Madison"]
              .groupby("field")["salary_adj"].median())
peer_medians = (df[df["institution"] != "UW-Madison"]
                .groupby("field")["salary_adj"].median())

competitive_gap = pd.DataFrame({
    "uw_median": uw_medians,
    "peer_median": peer_medians,
}).dropna()

competitive_gap["gap_pct"] = (
    (competitive_gap["uw_median"] - competitive_gap["peer_median"])
    / competitive_gap["peer_median"] * 100
)
competitive_gap = competitive_gap.sort_values("gap_pct")

print("UW-Madison Salary Gap vs Peer Median (COL-Adjusted):")
print("Negative = UW below peers\n")
for field, row in competitive_gap.iterrows():
    direction = "BELOW" if row["gap_pct"] < 0 else "ABOVE"
    print(f"  {field:18s}: {row['gap_pct']:+.1f}% ({direction} peers)")


# Diverging bar chart of competitive gaps
fig = go.Figure()

colors = [WONG[5] if g < 0 else WONG[2] for g in competitive_gap["gap_pct"]]

fig.add_trace(go.Bar(
    x=competitive_gap["gap_pct"],
    y=competitive_gap.index,
    orientation="h",
    marker_color=colors,
    text=[f"{g:+.1f}%" for g in competitive_gap["gap_pct"]],
    textposition="outside"
))

fig.add_vline(x=0, line_color="black", line_width=2)

fig.update_layout(
    title="UW-Madison Salary Gap vs Peer Median by Field (COL-Adjusted)",
    xaxis_title="% Difference from Peer Median",
    yaxis_title="",
    height=450,
    showlegend=False
)

fig.show()


Interpreting Competitive Gaps

Gaps below -5%: High-risk fields where UW-Madison may struggle to recruit and retain. These warrant immediate attention in salary-setting.
Gaps between -5% and +5%: Roughly competitive. Monitor for trends.
Gaps above +5%: Areas of strength, but verify this is not driven by a few highly-paid outliers (check the full distribution, not just medians).
Trend matters more than level: A field that was at +3% last year and is now at -2% is more concerning than a field that has been stable at -4%.

title: Peer Institution Comparison
section: Bump Charts and Peer Rankings Over Time
text:
Bump Charts and Peer Rankings Over Time
Bump charts show how institutional rankings shift across years. We simulate a 5-year history to demonstrate the technique.

# Simulate 5 years of median salary data for bump chart
np.random.seed(99)
years = [2020, 2021, 2022, 2023, 2024]
bump_institutions = ["UW-Madison", "U of Michigan", "Ohio State", "Penn State",
                     "U of Illinois", "Purdue", "Northwestern", "U of Minnesota"]

# Starting medians (approximate, in thousands)
starting_medians = {
    "UW-Madison": 97, "U of Michigan": 110, "Ohio State": 99,
    "Penn State": 100, "U of Illinois": 103, "Purdue": 94,
    "Northwestern": 120, "U of Minnesota": 96
}

bump_records = []
for inst in bump_institutions:
    salary = starting_medians[inst]
    for year in years:
        # Annual growth with some randomness
        growth = np.random.normal(0.025, 0.012)
        salary = salary * (1 + growth)
        bump_records.append({
            "institution": inst,
            "year": year,
            "median_salary": round(salary * 1000, 0)
        })

bump_df = pd.DataFrame(bump_records)

# Calculate ranks each year (1 = highest salary)
bump_df["rank"] = (
    bump_df.groupby("year")["median_salary"]
    .rank(ascending=False, method="min")
    .astype(int)
)

print("Salary Rankings by Year:")
pivot = bump_df.pivot(index="institution", columns="year", values="rank")
print(pivot.to_string())


# Bump chart using plotly
fig = go.Figure()

for i, inst in enumerate(bump_institutions):
    inst_data = bump_df[bump_df["institution"] == inst]
    color = WONG[i % len(WONG)]

    is_uw = inst == "UW-Madison"

    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=inst_data["year"],
        y=inst_data["rank"],
        mode="lines+markers+text",
        name=inst,
        line=dict(color=color, width=4 if is_uw else 2),
        marker=dict(size=10 if is_uw else 7),
        text=[inst if yr == 2024 else "" for yr in inst_data["year"]],
        textposition="middle right",
        textfont=dict(size=10),
    ))

fig.update_layout(
    title="Faculty Salary Rankings Among Big Ten Peers (2020-2024)",
    xaxis_title="Year",
    yaxis_title="Rank (1 = Highest)",
    yaxis=dict(autorange="reversed", dtick=1),
    height=500,
    showlegend=True,
    legend=dict(x=0.01, y=0.99)
)

fig.show()

title: Peer Institution Comparison
section: Cleveland Dot Plots for Field-Level Rankings
text:
Cleveland Dot Plots for Field-Level Rankings
Cleveland dot plots effectively show where each institution stands in a particular field.

# Cleveland dot plot: Compare all institutions in a single field
target_field = "Computer Science"

field_data = (df[df["field"] == target_field]
              .groupby("institution")["salary_adj"]
              .median()
              .sort_values()
              .reset_index())
field_data.columns = ["institution", "median_adj"]

fig = go.Figure()

colors = [WONG[5] if inst == "UW-Madison" else WONG[4]
          for inst in field_data["institution"]]
sizes = [16 if inst == "UW-Madison" else 10
         for inst in field_data["institution"]]

fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=field_data["median_adj"],
    y=field_data["institution"],
    mode="markers",
    marker=dict(size=sizes, color=colors,
                line=dict(width=1, color="black")),
    hovertemplate="%{y}: $%{x:,.0f}<extra></extra>"
))

# Add a vertical line for the peer median
peer_med = field_data["median_adj"].median()
fig.add_vline(x=peer_med, line_dash="dash", line_color="gray",
              annotation_text=f"Peer Median: ${peer_med:,.0f}",
              annotation_position="top")

fig.update_layout(
    title=f"{target_field}: COL-Adjusted Median Salary by Institution",
    xaxis_title="COL-Adjusted Median Salary ($)",
    xaxis_tickformat="$,.0f",
    yaxis_title="",
    height=450,
    showlegend=False
)

fig.show()


# Multi-field Cleveland dot plot: UW-Madison vs top peer in each field
top_peer_comparison = []

for field in fields:
    field_medians = (df[df["field"] == field]
                     .groupby("institution")["salary_adj"].median()
                     .sort_values(ascending=False))

    uw_val = field_medians.get("UW-Madison", np.nan)
    top_inst = field_medians.index[0]
    top_val = field_medians.iloc[0]

    # Also get the peer average (excluding UW)
    peer_avg = field_medians.drop("UW-Madison", errors="ignore").mean()

    top_peer_comparison.append({
        "field": field,
        "uw_median": uw_val,
        "top_institution": top_inst,
        "top_median": top_val,
        "peer_avg": peer_avg,
    })

tpc = pd.DataFrame(top_peer_comparison).sort_values("field")

fig = go.Figure()

# Peer average line segments
for _, row in tpc.iterrows():
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=[row["uw_median"], row["peer_avg"]],
        y=[row["field"], row["field"]],
        mode="lines",
        line=dict(color="lightgray", width=2),
        showlegend=False,
        hoverinfo="skip"
    ))

# UW-Madison dots
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=tpc["uw_median"],
    y=tpc["field"],
    mode="markers",
    name="UW-Madison",
    marker=dict(size=12, color=WONG[5],
                line=dict(width=1, color="black")),
    hovertemplate="%{y}: $%{x:,.0f}<extra>UW-Madison</extra>"
))

# Peer average dots
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=tpc["peer_avg"],
    y=tpc["field"],
    mode="markers",
    name="Peer Average",
    marker=dict(size=12, color=WONG[4], symbol="diamond",
                line=dict(width=1, color="black")),
    hovertemplate="%{y}: $%{x:,.0f}<extra>Peer Average</extra>"
))

fig.update_layout(
    title="UW-Madison vs Peer Average by Field (COL-Adjusted)",
    xaxis_title="COL-Adjusted Median Salary ($)",
    xaxis_tickformat="$,.0f",
    yaxis_title="",
    height=500,
    legend=dict(x=0.7, y=0.05)
)

fig.show()







TipIn R





Cleveland dot plots are a strength of ggplot2:
library(ggplot2)

ggplot(tpc_long, aes(x = median_salary, y = field, color = source)) +
  geom_point(size = 4) +
  geom_line(aes(group = field), color = "gray80") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("UW-Madison" = "#D55E00", "Peer Avg" = "#0072B2")) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "UW-Madison vs Peer Average by Field")
The ggplot2 version handles the connected dot plot with geom_line(aes(group = field)), which is slightly more concise than the Plotly loop approach.

title: Peer Institution Comparison
section: Data Sources for Real Peer Comparison
text:
Data Sources for Real Peer Comparison

Real-World Data Sources
When you move beyond simulated data, these sources provide actual peer salary information:









Source
Coverage
Access
Granularity




AAUP Faculty Compensation Survey
~900 institutions
Public summary, full via membership
Rank (Asst/Assoc/Full), institution


IPEDS (Integrated Postsecondary Education Data System)
All Title IV institutions
Free (NCES website)
Faculty rank, 9/12-month, gender


CUPA-HR (College & University Professional Association)
~1,300 institutions
Membership required
Discipline, rank, admin roles


NSF HERD Survey
~900 institutions
Free (NCSES website)
Research expenditures by field


BLS Occupational Employment Statistics
National/metro
Free (BLS website)
Occupation, not institution-specific


Chronicle of Higher Education
Varies
Subscription
Faculty salary database



Tips for working with real data:

IPEDS is your best free starting point: download via the Data Center or use the ipeds Python package
AAUP publishes an annual report each April; full data tables are available to member institutions
CUPA-HR is the gold standard for discipline-level comparisons but requires institutional membership
Always check whether figures are 9-month or 12-month contracts (common source of error)
Watch for differences in benefit valuation methods across surveys



# Example: Loading real IPEDS data (structure for reference)
# Download from https://nces.ed.gov/ipeds/datacenter/

# IPEDS salary data typically comes in this structure:
ipeds_example = pd.DataFrame({
    "unitid": [240444, 170976, 204796],          # IPEDS institution ID
    "institution": ["UW-Madison", "U of Michigan", "Ohio State"],
    "rank": ["Professor", "Professor", "Professor"],
    "contract_length": ["9-month", "9-month", "9-month"],
    "avg_salary": [132000, 155000, 128000],       # Average, not median
    "num_faculty": [1250, 1100, 980],
    "year": [2023, 2023, 2023],
})

# Convert 9-month to 12-month equivalent (common adjustment)
ipeds_example["salary_12mo"] = ipeds_example["avg_salary"] * (12 / 9)

# Merge with cost-of-living data
# BEA Regional Price Parities: https://www.bea.gov/data/prices-inflation
col_lookup = pd.DataFrame({
    "institution": ["UW-Madison", "U of Michigan", "Ohio State"],
    "rpp_index": [95.2, 97.1, 88.4],  # Metro-area RPP (national avg = 100)
})

merged = ipeds_example.merge(col_lookup, on="institution")
merged["salary_adj"] = merged["avg_salary"] / (merged["rpp_index"] / 100)

print(merged[["institution", "avg_salary", "rpp_index", "salary_adj"]])


When working with IPEDS downloads, the files often come as CSV with complex headers. Use PyCharm’s Database tool window (View > Tool Windows > Database) to preview CSV files before loading them. You can also use pd.read_csv(..., skiprows=N) to skip metadata rows that IPEDS includes at the top of their files.

title: Peer Institution Comparison
text:
Exercise

Exercise 25: Peer Benchmarking Report
Using the simulated peer data created in this lesson:

Calculate the overall rank of UW-Madison (by COL-adjusted median salary) among the 11 institutions
Identify the three fields where UW-Madison is furthest below the peer average (largest negative gap)
Create a grouped bar chart showing UW-Madison vs the peer average for every field, sorted by gap size
Build a radar chart comparing UW-Madison, the top-ranked peer, and the bottom-ranked peer across all 10 fields (use percentile scores scaled 0–100)
Produce a summary table with columns: Field, UW Median, Peer Median, Gap %, UW Rank (out of 11)







NoteSolution






# 1. Overall rank
overall_ranks = (df.groupby("institution")["salary_adj"]
                 .median()
                 .sort_values(ascending=False))
uw_rank = (overall_ranks.rank(ascending=False)
           .loc["UW-Madison"]
           .astype(int))
print(f"1. UW-Madison overall rank: {uw_rank} of {len(overall_ranks)}")
print(f"   COL-adjusted median: ${overall_ranks['UW-Madison']:,.0f}\n")

# 2. Three fields with largest negative gap
field_gaps = []
for field in fields:
    field_subset = df[df["field"] == field]
    uw_med = (field_subset[field_subset["institution"] == "UW-Madison"]
              ["salary_adj"].median())
    peer_med = (field_subset[field_subset["institution"] != "UW-Madison"]
                ["salary_adj"].median())
    gap = (uw_med - peer_med) / peer_med * 100

    # Calculate UW rank within this field
    field_ranks = (field_subset.groupby("institution")["salary_adj"]
                   .median()
                   .rank(ascending=False))
    uw_field_rank = int(field_ranks.get("UW-Madison", np.nan))

    field_gaps.append({
        "field": field,
        "uw_median": uw_med,
        "peer_median": peer_med,
        "gap_pct": gap,
        "uw_rank": uw_field_rank,
    })

gaps_df = pd.DataFrame(field_gaps).sort_values("gap_pct")

print("2. Three fields where UW-Madison is furthest below peers:")
for _, row in gaps_df.head(3).iterrows():
    print(f"   {row['field']}: {row['gap_pct']:+.1f}% (rank {row['uw_rank']} of 11)")

# 3. Grouped bar chart: UW vs peer average by field
gaps_sorted = gaps_df.sort_values("gap_pct")

fig = go.Figure()

fig.add_trace(go.Bar(
    x=gaps_sorted["field"],
    y=gaps_sorted["uw_median"],
    name="UW-Madison",
    marker_color=WONG[5]
))

fig.add_trace(go.Bar(
    x=gaps_sorted["field"],
    y=gaps_sorted["peer_median"],
    name="Peer Average",
    marker_color=WONG[4]
))

fig.update_layout(
    title="UW-Madison vs Peer Average Salary by Field (COL-Adjusted)",
    yaxis_title="COL-Adjusted Median Salary ($)",
    yaxis_tickformat="$,.0f",
    barmode="group",
    xaxis_tickangle=-30,
    height=500,
    legend=dict(x=0.75, y=0.95)
)

fig.show()

# 4. Radar chart: UW vs top and bottom peers
# Convert field medians to 0-100 percentile scores
all_field_medians = (df.groupby(["institution", "field"])["salary_adj"]
                     .median()
                     .unstack())

# Scale each field to 0-100 across institutions
scaled = all_field_medians.apply(
    lambda col: (col - col.min()) / (col.max() - col.min()) * 100, axis=0
)

# Identify top and bottom institutions by overall rank
top_inst = overall_ranks.index[0]
bottom_inst = overall_ranks.index[-1]
radar_fields = list(fields.keys())

fig = go.Figure()

for inst, color, width in [
    ("UW-Madison", WONG[5], 3),
    (top_inst, WONG[2], 2),
    (bottom_inst, WONG[0], 2)
]:
    scores = [scaled.loc[inst, f] for f in radar_fields]
    scores.append(scores[0])  # Close polygon

    fig.add_trace(go.Scatterpolar(
        r=scores,
        theta=radar_fields + [radar_fields[0]],
        name=inst,
        line=dict(color=color, width=width),
        fill="toself" if inst == "UW-Madison" else "none",
        opacity=0.6 if inst == "UW-Madison" else 0.9,
    ))

fig.update_layout(
    polar=dict(radialaxis=dict(visible=True, range=[0, 100])),
    title=f"Field-Level Comparison: UW-Madison vs {top_inst} vs {bottom_inst}",
    height=550,
)

fig.show()

# 5. Summary table
print("\n5. Summary Table:")
print(f"{'Field':18s} {'UW Median':>12s} {'Peer Median':>12s} {'Gap %':>8s} {'UW Rank':>8s}")
print("-" * 62)
for _, row in gaps_df.sort_values("field").iterrows():
    print(f"{row['field']:18s} ${row['uw_median']:>10,.0f} ${row['peer_median']:>10,.0f} "
          f"{row['gap_pct']:>+7.1f}% {row['uw_rank']:>6d}/11")

title: Peer Institution Comparison
text:
Summary
In this lesson you built a complete peer institution comparison framework:

Peer group selection: Objective criteria (AAU, Big Ten, Carnegie, research expenditure) define meaningful comparison sets
Simulated peer data: numpy random generation creates realistic multi-institution datasets for development and testing
Cost-of-living adjustment: Regional Price Parities normalize salaries to purchasing-power equivalence, often reshuffling rankings
Percentile benchmarking: Quantifies exactly where UW-Madison falls in the peer distribution for each field
Radar charts: Compare institutions across multiple dimensions simultaneously (salary, benefits, research, quality of life)
Competitive gap analysis: Diverging bar charts highlight fields where UW-Madison is above or below peer norms
Bump charts: Track ranking changes over time to identify concerning trends
Cleveland dot plots: Clean comparison of institutions within a single field, or UW vs peer average across all fields
Real data sources: AAUP, IPEDS, and CUPA-HR provide actual peer salary data for production analyses

Key takeaways:

Always adjust for cost of living before comparing—nominal rankings can be misleading
Percentile rank is more informative than simple averages for competitive positioning
Track trends over multiple years; a single year’s snapshot misses trajectory
Fields with large negative gaps and downward trends represent the greatest retention risk

title: Cost Modeling and Budget Impact
text:
After this lesson, you will be able to:

Calculate total compensation costs including fringe benefit loading factors
Model across-the-board (ATB), merit-based, and targeted equity raise scenarios
Estimate workforce-wide cost impacts of each raise strategy
Incorporate vacancy savings and turnover costs into budget models
Perform sensitivity analysis on key assumptions
Build a multi-year budget projection model
Visualize cost scenarios using plotly comparison charts

title: Cost Modeling and Budget Impact
text:
Introduction

Why Cost Modeling Matters
Budget analysts need more than descriptive salary statistics. Decision-makers ask questions like:

“What does a 2% across-the-board raise cost us next year?”
“How much do we save from unfilled positions?”
“If we shift from ATB raises to targeted equity adjustments, what is the net impact?”
“What does our salary budget look like over the next five years under different scenarios?”

Cost modeling translates compensation data into budget impact projections that inform real decisions. The key is building models flexible enough to test multiple assumptions quickly.


Fund Accounting in Cost Models
A cost model that ignores fund source will produce misleading totals. In a public university, not all salary dollars are equal:
Fund sources and their constraints:

GPR (General Purpose Revenue): State-appropriated funds. These are the most constrained — the legislature controls the total, and the pay plan determines allowable raises. A cost model showing “we need $5M for raises” must specify how much comes from GPR vs. other sources.
Tuition/Fee Revenue (PR 131): More flexible than GPR but subject to tuition policy. During Wisconsin’s tuition freeze (2013-2023), this source was effectively capped.
Federal Grants (Fund 144): Salary on grants must comply with OMB Uniform Guidance (2 CFR 200). Raises must be consistent with institutional policy applied uniformly — you can’t give a 5% raise to a grant-funded employee if the institutional standard is 2%.
Gift Funds: Donor intent may restrict how funds are used. An endowed chair might have ample funds for salary, but the gift terms may not allow using it for other positions.

Fringe benefit rates differ by fund: This is a critical detail many models miss. At UW, the fringe rate for GPR-funded employees differs from grant-funded employees because federal negotiated rates include different components (e.g., tuition remission for graduate assistants is charged to grants but not GPR). A $100K salary might cost $135K on GPR but $142K on a federal grant.
Why this matters for your models: When you model a 3% raise pool, the total cost depends on the fund mix of the affected population. A division that’s 80% grant-funded will have different budget implications than one that’s 80% GPR-funded. Building fund source into your cost models from the start prevents the “where does the money come from?” question that will inevitably arise when you present to leadership.


import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots

# Wong color-blind-safe palette
WONG = ["#E69F00", "#56B4E9", "#009E73", "#F0E442", "#0072B2", "#D55E00", "#CC79A7"]

title: Cost Modeling and Budget Impact
text:
Generating a Realistic Workforce Dataset
We will build a synthetic workforce loosely modeled on a large university division. Each employee has a salary, FTE status, division, and title.

np.random.seed(2024)

divisions = [
    "College of Letters & Science",
    "School of Engineering",
    "School of Business",
    "School of Education",
    "Division of Information Technology",
    "Facilities Planning & Management",
    "University Health Services",
]

titles = {
    "Professor":           {"min": 95000, "max": 200000, "n": 120},
    "Associate Professor": {"min": 80000, "max": 140000, "n": 150},
    "Assistant Professor":  {"min": 70000, "max": 110000, "n": 130},
    "Research Scientist":   {"min": 55000, "max": 95000,  "n": 100},
    "Academic Staff":       {"min": 42000, "max": 75000,  "n": 200},
    "Admin Staff":          {"min": 38000, "max": 62000,  "n": 180},
    "IT Professional":      {"min": 58000, "max": 105000, "n": 70},
}

records = []
emp_id = 1000

for title, params in titles.items():
    for _ in range(params["n"]):
        salary = np.random.uniform(params["min"], params["max"])
        fte = np.random.choice([1.0, 0.8, 0.5], p=[0.80, 0.12, 0.08])
        division = np.random.choice(divisions, p=[0.25, 0.18, 0.15, 0.12, 0.12, 0.10, 0.08])
        years = np.clip(np.random.exponential(8), 0, 35)

        records.append({
            "emp_id": emp_id,
            "title": title,
            "division": division,
            "salary": round(salary, 2),
            "fte": fte,
            "years_service": round(years, 1),
        })
        emp_id += 1

df = pd.DataFrame(records)
print(f"Workforce size: {len(df)} employees")
print(f"Total base salary: ${df['salary'].sum():,.0f}")
print(f"\nBy title:")
print(df.groupby("title")["salary"].agg(["count", "mean", "sum"]).round(0))

title: Cost Modeling and Budget Impact
text:
1. Total Compensation Cost Calculation
Base salary is only part of what an employer pays. Fringe benefits — health insurance, retirement contributions, FICA, disability, and other benefits — add a loading factor on top of base pay.

Fringe Benefit Loading Factors
Typical loading rates for a public university:



Component
Rate




FICA (Social Security + Medicare)
7.65%


Retirement (WRS employer share)
~6.75%


Health insurance
~15-20% of salary


Disability, life, other
~2-3%


Total fringe rate
~32-38%



We often model this as a single “fringe rate” multiplied by base salary. The rate may vary by employee category (faculty vs. classified staff).


# Define fringe rates by category
fringe_rates = {
    "Professor": 0.36,
    "Associate Professor": 0.36,
    "Assistant Professor": 0.36,
    "Research Scientist": 0.34,
    "Academic Staff": 0.34,
    "Admin Staff": 0.38,
    "IT Professional": 0.35,
}

df["fringe_rate"] = df["title"].map(fringe_rates)
df["fringe_cost"] = df["salary"] * df["fringe_rate"]
df["total_comp"] = df["salary"] + df["fringe_cost"]

print("Total Compensation Summary")
print(f"{'':>25} {'Base Salary':>15} {'Fringe':>15} {'Total Comp':>15}")
print("-" * 72)

for title in titles.keys():
    subset = df[df["title"] == title]
    print(f"{title:>25} ${subset['salary'].sum():>13,.0f} ${subset['fringe_cost'].sum():>13,.0f} ${subset['total_comp'].sum():>13,.0f}")

total_base = df["salary"].sum()
total_fringe = df["fringe_cost"].sum()
total_comp = df["total_comp"].sum()
print("-" * 72)
print(f"{'TOTAL':>25} ${total_base:>13,.0f} ${total_fringe:>13,.0f} ${total_comp:>13,.0f}")
print(f"\nEffective overall fringe rate: {total_fringe / total_base:.1%}")







TipIn R





In R you would use dplyr::mutate() with a join or case_when() to apply fringe rates:
fringe_lookup <- tibble(
  title = c("Professor", "Associate Professor", ...),
  fringe_rate = c(0.36, 0.36, ...)
)

df <- df %>%
  left_join(fringe_lookup, by = "title") %>%
  mutate(
    fringe_cost = salary * fringe_rate,
    total_comp = salary + fringe_cost
  )
The pandas .map() method works like a named-vector lookup in R.

title: Cost Modeling and Budget Impact
text:
2. Raise Scenario Modeling
Budget analysts typically evaluate three kinds of salary increases:

Raise Strategies

Across-the-Board (ATB): Every employee gets the same percentage increase. Simple and transparent, but does not address compression or equity.
Merit-based: Raises allocated based on performance ratings. Higher performers get larger increases, but the total pool is the same.
Targeted equity adjustments: Raises directed at specific groups — for example, staff below the market midpoint, or titles with documented compression. More targeted, but harder to communicate.



def model_atb_raise(df, pct):
    """Model an across-the-board raise."""
    result = df.copy()
    result["raise_amt"] = result["salary"] * pct
    result["new_salary"] = result["salary"] + result["raise_amt"]
    result["new_total_comp"] = result["new_salary"] * (1 + result["fringe_rate"])
    return result

def model_merit_raise(df, pool_pct, performance_weights=None):
    """Model merit-based raises with a fixed total pool.

    performance_weights: dict mapping performance tier to weight.
    Employees are randomly assigned to tiers.
    """
    if performance_weights is None:
        performance_weights = {
            "Exceeds": (0.20, 1.8),   # 20% of employees, 1.8x share
            "Meets+":  (0.30, 1.2),   # 30%, 1.2x share
            "Meets":   (0.40, 0.7),   # 40%, 0.7x share
            "Below":   (0.10, 0.0),   # 10%, no raise
        }

    result = df.copy()
    total_pool = result["salary"].sum() * pool_pct

    # Assign performance tiers
    tiers = []
    probs = []
    weights = []
    for tier, (prob, weight) in performance_weights.items():
        tiers.append(tier)
        probs.append(prob)
        weights.append(weight)

    result["perf_tier"] = np.random.choice(tiers, size=len(result), p=probs)
    result["perf_weight"] = result["perf_tier"].map(dict(zip(tiers, weights)))

    # Distribute pool proportionally to salary * weight
    weighted_salary = result["salary"] * result["perf_weight"]
    result["raise_amt"] = (weighted_salary / weighted_salary.sum()) * total_pool
    result["new_salary"] = result["salary"] + result["raise_amt"]
    result["new_total_comp"] = result["new_salary"] * (1 + result["fringe_rate"])
    return result

def model_equity_raise(df, pool_pct, target_col="title", target_values=None):
    """Model targeted equity adjustments for specific groups.

    Employees in target_values get raises; others get nothing.
    Within the target group, lower-paid employees get proportionally more.
    """
    result = df.copy()
    total_pool = result["salary"].sum() * pool_pct

    if target_values is None:
        target_values = ["Admin Staff", "Academic Staff"]

    is_target = result[target_col].isin(target_values)

    # Weight inversely by salary within the target group (equity-focused)
    target_salaries = result.loc[is_target, "salary"]
    inv_weight = 1.0 / target_salaries
    result["raise_amt"] = 0.0
    result.loc[is_target, "raise_amt"] = (inv_weight / inv_weight.sum()) * total_pool
    result["new_salary"] = result["salary"] + result["raise_amt"]
    result["new_total_comp"] = result["new_salary"] * (1 + result["fringe_rate"])
    return result


Comparing the Three Scenarios

np.random.seed(42)
raise_pct = 0.02  # 2% pool in each scenario

atb = model_atb_raise(df, raise_pct)
merit = model_merit_raise(df, raise_pct)
equity = model_equity_raise(df, raise_pct)

scenarios = {"ATB (2%)": atb, "Merit (2% pool)": merit, "Equity Targeted (2% pool)": equity}

print(f"{'Scenario':<25} {'Total Raise $':>15} {'Avg Raise %':>12} {'Total New Comp':>16}")
print("-" * 70)
for name, s in scenarios.items():
    total_raise = s["raise_amt"].sum()
    avg_pct = (s["raise_amt"] / s["salary"]).mean() * 100
    new_comp = s["new_total_comp"].sum()
    print(f"{name:<25} ${total_raise:>13,.0f} {avg_pct:>10.2f}% ${new_comp:>14,.0f}")


# Distribution of raises by title under each scenario
fig = make_subplots(rows=1, cols=3, subplot_titles=list(scenarios.keys()))

for i, (name, s) in enumerate(scenarios.items(), 1):
    raise_by_title = s.groupby("title")["raise_amt"].mean().sort_values()
    fig.add_trace(
        go.Bar(
            x=raise_by_title.values,
            y=raise_by_title.index,
            orientation="h",
            marker_color=WONG[i - 1],
            name=name,
            showlegend=False,
        ),
        row=1,
        col=i,
    )

fig.update_layout(
    title="Average Raise Amount by Title Under Each Scenario",
    height=400,
    width=950,
)
fig.update_xaxes(title_text="Avg Raise ($)", row=1, col=2)
fig.show()







TipIn R





In R, you might use purrr::map() or lapply() to run the same function across scenario parameter lists and bind the results:
scenarios <- list(
  "ATB" = model_atb(df, 0.02),
  "Merit" = model_merit(df, 0.02),
  "Equity" = model_equity(df, 0.02)
)

results <- bind_rows(scenarios, .id = "scenario")
The Python pattern of storing DataFrames in a dictionary and iterating with .items() is the direct equivalent.

title: Cost Modeling and Budget Impact
text:
3. Vacancy Savings and Turnover Costs


Vacancy Savings
When a position is vacant, the employer does not pay the salary or most fringe costs. A position vacant for 3 months saves roughly 25% of its annual total compensation. Budget models often include an assumed vacancy rate (e.g., 3-5%) applied across the workforce.


Turnover Costs
Conversely, turnover is expensive. Recruitment, onboarding, and lost productivity typically cost 50-200% of the departing employee’s annual salary, depending on the role. For highly specialized positions, costs can be even higher.



# Assume an average vacancy duration and turnover rate by title
turnover_params = {
    "Professor":           {"turnover_rate": 0.03, "vacancy_months": 6, "replacement_cost_pct": 1.5},
    "Associate Professor": {"turnover_rate": 0.04, "vacancy_months": 5, "replacement_cost_pct": 1.2},
    "Assistant Professor":  {"turnover_rate": 0.06, "vacancy_months": 4, "replacement_cost_pct": 1.0},
    "Research Scientist":   {"turnover_rate": 0.08, "vacancy_months": 3, "replacement_cost_pct": 0.8},
    "Academic Staff":       {"turnover_rate": 0.10, "vacancy_months": 2, "replacement_cost_pct": 0.5},
    "Admin Staff":          {"turnover_rate": 0.12, "vacancy_months": 2, "replacement_cost_pct": 0.5},
    "IT Professional":      {"turnover_rate": 0.09, "vacancy_months": 3, "replacement_cost_pct": 1.0},
}

turnover_df = pd.DataFrame(turnover_params).T.reset_index()
turnover_df.columns = ["title", "turnover_rate", "vacancy_months", "replacement_cost_pct"]

title_summary = df.groupby("title").agg(
    headcount=("emp_id", "count"),
    avg_salary=("salary", "mean"),
    avg_total_comp=("total_comp", "mean"),
).reset_index()

turnover_analysis = title_summary.merge(turnover_df, on="title")

# Expected separations per year
turnover_analysis["expected_separations"] = (
    turnover_analysis["headcount"] * turnover_analysis["turnover_rate"]
).round(1)

# Vacancy savings = separations * avg_total_comp * (vacancy_months / 12)
turnover_analysis["vacancy_savings"] = (
    turnover_analysis["expected_separations"]
    * turnover_analysis["avg_total_comp"]
    * (turnover_analysis["vacancy_months"] / 12)
)

# Replacement cost = separations * avg_salary * replacement_cost_pct
turnover_analysis["replacement_cost"] = (
    turnover_analysis["expected_separations"]
    * turnover_analysis["avg_salary"]
    * turnover_analysis["replacement_cost_pct"]
)

turnover_analysis["net_turnover_impact"] = (
    turnover_analysis["vacancy_savings"] - turnover_analysis["replacement_cost"]
)

print("Turnover Cost Analysis by Title")
print(turnover_analysis[["title", "expected_separations", "vacancy_savings",
                          "replacement_cost", "net_turnover_impact"]].to_string(index=False))

print(f"\nTotal vacancy savings:     ${turnover_analysis['vacancy_savings'].sum():>12,.0f}")
print(f"Total replacement costs:   ${turnover_analysis['replacement_cost'].sum():>12,.0f}")
print(f"Net turnover impact:       ${turnover_analysis['net_turnover_impact'].sum():>12,.0f}")


# Visualize vacancy savings vs replacement costs
fig = go.Figure()

fig.add_trace(go.Bar(
    x=turnover_analysis["title"],
    y=turnover_analysis["vacancy_savings"],
    name="Vacancy Savings",
    marker_color=WONG[2],
))

fig.add_trace(go.Bar(
    x=turnover_analysis["title"],
    y=-turnover_analysis["replacement_cost"],
    name="Replacement Cost",
    marker_color=WONG[5],
))

fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=turnover_analysis["title"],
    y=turnover_analysis["net_turnover_impact"],
    name="Net Impact",
    mode="markers+lines",
    marker=dict(size=10, color=WONG[4]),
    line=dict(color=WONG[4], dash="dash"),
))

fig.update_layout(
    title="Vacancy Savings vs Replacement Costs by Title",
    yaxis_title="Dollars ($)",
    barmode="relative",
    height=450,
)
fig.show()

title: Cost Modeling and Budget Impact
text:
4. Sensitivity Analysis
What happens if our assumptions are wrong? Sensitivity analysis tests how outputs change when we vary input parameters.

PyCharm Tip: Parameter Sweeps
When running sensitivity analyses, use PyCharm’s Scientific Mode to view DataFrames interactively. You can set breakpoints inside your loop to inspect intermediate results. The variable explorer makes it easy to compare values across iterations.


def compute_annual_cost(df, raise_pct, fringe_adj, vacancy_rate):
    """Compute total annual compensation cost with adjustable parameters."""
    new_salary = df["salary"] * (1 + raise_pct)
    effective_fringe = df["fringe_rate"] + fringe_adj
    total_comp = new_salary * (1 + effective_fringe)
    # Apply vacancy rate as a discount
    net_cost = total_comp.sum() * (1 - vacancy_rate)
    return net_cost

# Parameter ranges for sensitivity analysis
raise_range = np.arange(0.00, 0.06, 0.005)     # 0% to 5.5% raises
fringe_adj_range = np.arange(-0.03, 0.04, 0.01) # fringe rate +/- 3%
vacancy_range = np.arange(0.01, 0.08, 0.01)      # 1% to 7% vacancy rate

# Sensitivity to raise percentage (holding others fixed)
costs_by_raise = []
for r in raise_range:
    cost = compute_annual_cost(df, raise_pct=r, fringe_adj=0, vacancy_rate=0.04)
    costs_by_raise.append({"raise_pct": r * 100, "total_cost": cost})

raise_sensitivity = pd.DataFrame(costs_by_raise)

# Sensitivity to vacancy rate (holding raise at 2%)
costs_by_vacancy = []
for v in vacancy_range:
    cost = compute_annual_cost(df, raise_pct=0.02, fringe_adj=0, vacancy_rate=v)
    costs_by_vacancy.append({"vacancy_rate": v * 100, "total_cost": cost})

vacancy_sensitivity = pd.DataFrame(costs_by_vacancy)


fig = make_subplots(rows=1, cols=2, subplot_titles=[
    "Cost Sensitivity to Raise %",
    "Cost Sensitivity to Vacancy Rate",
])

fig.add_trace(
    go.Scatter(
        x=raise_sensitivity["raise_pct"],
        y=raise_sensitivity["total_cost"],
        mode="lines+markers",
        marker=dict(color=WONG[0]),
        line=dict(color=WONG[0]),
        name="Raise %",
    ),
    row=1, col=1,
)

fig.add_trace(
    go.Scatter(
        x=vacancy_sensitivity["vacancy_rate"],
        y=vacancy_sensitivity["total_cost"],
        mode="lines+markers",
        marker=dict(color=WONG[1]),
        line=dict(color=WONG[1]),
        name="Vacancy Rate",
    ),
    row=1, col=2,
)

fig.update_xaxes(title_text="Raise Percentage (%)", row=1, col=1)
fig.update_xaxes(title_text="Vacancy Rate (%)", row=1, col=2)
fig.update_yaxes(title_text="Total Annual Cost ($)", row=1, col=1)
fig.update_yaxes(title_text="Total Annual Cost ($)", row=1, col=2)
fig.update_layout(title="Sensitivity Analysis", height=400, width=900, showlegend=False)
fig.show()







TipIn R





Sensitivity sweeps in R often use purrr::map_dfr():
raise_sensitivity <- map_dfr(seq(0, 0.055, 0.005), function(r) {
  tibble(
    raise_pct = r * 100,
    total_cost = compute_annual_cost(df, raise_pct = r, fringe_adj = 0, vacancy_rate = 0.04)
  )
})
The Python pattern of building a list of dicts in a for-loop and converting to a DataFrame is straightforward and readable, though list comprehensions work too.

title: Cost Modeling and Budget Impact
text:
5. Multi-Year Budget Projection
Now we combine raise scenarios, fringe trends, and turnover into a multi-year projection.

def project_budget(df, years=5, annual_raise=0.02, fringe_growth=0.005,
                   vacancy_rate=0.04, turnover_savings_pct=0.01):
    """Project total compensation budget over multiple years.

    Parameters
    ----------
    df : DataFrame with salary, fringe_rate columns
    years : number of projection years
    annual_raise : annual salary increase rate
    fringe_growth : annual increase in fringe rate (absolute, e.g., 0.5% per year)
    vacancy_rate : assumed steady-state vacancy rate
    turnover_savings_pct : net savings from turnover as pct of total comp
    """
    projections = []
    current_salaries = df["salary"].copy()
    current_fringe_rates = df["fringe_rate"].copy()

    for year in range(years + 1):
        base_total = current_salaries.sum()
        fringe_total = (current_salaries * current_fringe_rates).sum()
        gross_comp = base_total + fringe_total
        vacancy_discount = gross_comp * vacancy_rate
        turnover_offset = gross_comp * turnover_savings_pct
        net_cost = gross_comp - vacancy_discount - turnover_offset

        projections.append({
            "year": year,
            "base_salary_total": base_total,
            "fringe_total": fringe_total,
            "gross_comp": gross_comp,
            "vacancy_savings": vacancy_discount,
            "net_cost": net_cost,
        })

        # Advance to next year
        current_salaries = current_salaries * (1 + annual_raise)
        current_fringe_rates = current_fringe_rates + fringe_growth

    return pd.DataFrame(projections)


# Compare three budget scenarios over 5 years
scenario_params = {
    "Conservative (1.5% raise)": {"annual_raise": 0.015, "fringe_growth": 0.003},
    "Moderate (2.5% raise)":     {"annual_raise": 0.025, "fringe_growth": 0.005},
    "Aggressive (4% raise)":     {"annual_raise": 0.040, "fringe_growth": 0.007},
}

all_projections = []
for name, params in scenario_params.items():
    proj = project_budget(df, years=5, **params)
    proj["scenario"] = name
    all_projections.append(proj)

projections = pd.concat(all_projections, ignore_index=True)

# Show year 5 comparison
year5 = projections[projections["year"] == 5][["scenario", "base_salary_total", "fringe_total", "net_cost"]]
year5.columns = ["Scenario", "Base Salary", "Fringe", "Net Cost"]
print("Year 5 Budget Comparison")
print(year5.to_string(index=False, float_format="${:,.0f}".format))


# Cumulative cost line chart
fig = go.Figure()

for i, (name, _) in enumerate(scenario_params.items()):
    scenario_data = projections[projections["scenario"] == name]
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=scenario_data["year"],
        y=scenario_data["net_cost"],
        mode="lines+markers",
        name=name,
        line=dict(color=WONG[i], width=3),
        marker=dict(size=8),
    ))

fig.update_layout(
    title="5-Year Budget Projection by Scenario",
    xaxis_title="Year",
    yaxis_title="Net Annual Cost ($)",
    yaxis_tickformat="$,.0f",
    height=450,
    legend=dict(x=0.02, y=0.98),
)
fig.show()


Cumulative Total Cost

fig = go.Figure()

for i, (name, _) in enumerate(scenario_params.items()):
    scenario_data = projections[projections["scenario"] == name].copy()
    scenario_data["cumulative_cost"] = scenario_data["net_cost"].cumsum()

    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=scenario_data["year"],
        y=scenario_data["cumulative_cost"],
        mode="lines+markers",
        name=name,
        line=dict(color=WONG[i], width=3),
        marker=dict(size=8),
        fill="tonexty" if i > 0 else None,
    ))

fig.update_layout(
    title="Cumulative Total Compensation Cost Over 5 Years",
    xaxis_title="Year",
    yaxis_title="Cumulative Cost ($)",
    yaxis_tickformat="$,.0f",
    height=450,
)
fig.show()







TipIn R





Multi-year projections in R typically use accumulate() from purrr or a manual loop with bind_rows():
project_budget <- function(df, years = 5, annual_raise = 0.02) {
  results <- vector("list", years + 1)
  current <- df

  for (yr in 0:years) {
    results[[yr + 1]] <- tibble(year = yr, net_cost = sum(current$total_comp))
    current <- current %>% mutate(salary = salary * (1 + annual_raise))
  }

  bind_rows(results)
}
The logic is identical. Python’s approach of appending dicts to a list and converting to a DataFrame mirrors the R pattern of collecting tibbles and calling bind_rows().

title: Cost Modeling and Budget Impact
text:
6. Cost Comparison Dashboard
Let us build a side-by-side visualization comparing all raise strategies with their budget impact.

# Bar chart: Year 0 vs Year 5 cost by scenario
year0 = projections[projections["year"] == 0][["scenario", "net_cost"]].rename(
    columns={"net_cost": "year_0_cost"}
)
year5_data = projections[projections["year"] == 5][["scenario", "net_cost"]].rename(
    columns={"net_cost": "year_5_cost"}
)
comparison = year0.merge(year5_data, on="scenario")
comparison["increase"] = comparison["year_5_cost"] - comparison["year_0_cost"]
comparison["increase_pct"] = (comparison["increase"] / comparison["year_0_cost"]) * 100

fig = go.Figure()

fig.add_trace(go.Bar(
    x=comparison["scenario"],
    y=comparison["year_0_cost"],
    name="Year 0",
    marker_color=WONG[1],
))

fig.add_trace(go.Bar(
    x=comparison["scenario"],
    y=comparison["year_5_cost"],
    name="Year 5",
    marker_color=WONG[4],
))

# Add percentage annotations
for idx, row in comparison.iterrows():
    fig.add_annotation(
        x=row["scenario"],
        y=row["year_5_cost"],
        text=f"+{row['increase_pct']:.1f}%",
        showarrow=False,
        yshift=15,
        font=dict(size=13, color=WONG[5]),
    )

fig.update_layout(
    title="Budget Impact: Year 0 vs Year 5",
    yaxis_title="Net Annual Cost ($)",
    yaxis_tickformat="$,.0f",
    barmode="group",
    height=450,
)
fig.show()


PyCharm Tip: Plotly in PyCharm
If plotly charts do not render inline in the PyCharm console, use fig.write_html("output.html") and open the file in a browser. Alternatively, enable the Scientific Mode plots pane, or run your script as a Jupyter notebook cell within PyCharm Professional.

title: Cost Modeling and Budget Impact
text:
Summary
In this lesson you learned to:

Calculate total compensation using fringe benefit loading factors applied by employee category
Model three raise strategies (ATB, merit, equity) and compare their workforce-wide cost impact
Estimate vacancy savings and turnover replacement costs to get a realistic net budget figure
Run sensitivity analyses to understand how results change with different assumptions
Build multi-year projections that compound raises, fringe growth, and offsets over time
Visualize budget scenarios with plotly bar charts, line charts, and annotated comparisons

The key takeaway: a well-structured cost model lets analysts answer “what if?” questions quickly. By parameterizing assumptions (raise rates, fringe growth, vacancy rates), you can re-run the model with new inputs in seconds rather than rebuilding spreadsheets from scratch.

title: Cost Modeling and Budget Impact
text:
Exercises

Exercise: Build a Division-Level Budget Model
Create a function that projects 5-year costs by division, with the following requirements:

Each division gets a different raise rate based on strategic priority:

“School of Engineering” and “Division of Information Technology”: 3.5% (retention)
“School of Business”: 3.0%
All other divisions: 2.0%

Apply a uniform fringe growth of 0.4% per year
Use a 4% vacancy rate
Return a DataFrame with columns: year, division, base_salary, fringe, net_cost
Create a plotly line chart showing net cost by division over the 5 years, with each division as a separate colored line
Add a summary table showing which division has the largest absolute and percentage increase from Year 0 to Year 5








NoteSolution






def project_by_division(df, years=5, raise_map=None, fringe_growth=0.004,
                        vacancy_rate=0.04):
    """Project budget by division with division-specific raise rates."""
    if raise_map is None:
        raise_map = {
            "School of Engineering": 0.035,
            "Division of Information Technology": 0.035,
            "School of Business": 0.030,
        }
    default_raise = 0.02

    projections = []

    for division in df["division"].unique():
        div_df = df[df["division"] == division].copy()
        annual_raise = raise_map.get(division, default_raise)
        current_salaries = div_df["salary"].copy()
        current_fringe_rates = div_df["fringe_rate"].copy()

        for year in range(years + 1):
            base = current_salaries.sum()
            fringe = (current_salaries * current_fringe_rates).sum()
            gross = base + fringe
            net = gross * (1 - vacancy_rate)

            projections.append({
                "year": year,
                "division": division,
                "base_salary": base,
                "fringe": fringe,
                "net_cost": net,
            })

            current_salaries = current_salaries * (1 + annual_raise)
            current_fringe_rates = current_fringe_rates + fringe_growth

    return pd.DataFrame(projections)


# Run the projection
div_projections = project_by_division(df)

# Line chart
fig = go.Figure()
for i, division in enumerate(sorted(df["division"].unique())):
    div_data = div_projections[div_projections["division"] == division]
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=div_data["year"],
        y=div_data["net_cost"],
        mode="lines+markers",
        name=division,
        line=dict(color=WONG[i % len(WONG)], width=2),
    ))

fig.update_layout(
    title="5-Year Net Cost Projection by Division",
    xaxis_title="Year",
    yaxis_title="Net Annual Cost ($)",
    yaxis_tickformat="$,.0f",
    height=500,
    legend=dict(font=dict(size=10)),
)
fig.show()

# Summary table: Year 0 vs Year 5
y0 = div_projections[div_projections["year"] == 0][["division", "net_cost"]].rename(
    columns={"net_cost": "year_0"}
)
y5 = div_projections[div_projections["year"] == 5][["division", "net_cost"]].rename(
    columns={"net_cost": "year_5"}
)
summary = y0.merge(y5, on="division")
summary["increase"] = summary["year_5"] - summary["year_0"]
summary["increase_pct"] = (summary["increase"] / summary["year_0"]) * 100
summary = summary.sort_values("increase", ascending=False)

print("Division Budget Growth: Year 0 to Year 5")
print(summary.to_string(index=False, float_format="${:,.0f}".format))
print(f"\nLargest absolute increase: {summary.iloc[0]['division']}"
      f" (${summary.iloc[0]['increase']:,.0f})")
print(f"Largest percentage increase: {summary.sort_values('increase_pct', ascending=False).iloc[0]['division']}"
      f" ({summary.sort_values('increase_pct', ascending=False).iloc[0]['increase_pct']:.1f}%)")
