title: Working with APIs
text:
After this lesson, you will be able to:

Understand REST API concepts and HTTP methods
Use the requests library to fetch data from APIs
Parse JSON responses into pandas DataFrames
Handle API authentication and rate limiting
Implement caching to avoid repeated API calls

title: Working with APIs
text:
Introduction

What is an API?
An API (Application Programming Interface) allows programs to communicate with web services. For data analysis, we often use APIs to:

Fetch economic data (FRED, BLS, Census)
Access financial data (stock prices, exchange rates)
Retrieve metadata from institutional systems

APIs are preferable to web scraping because they provide structured data in a predictable format.


import pandas as pd
import requests
import json
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
import os

title: Working with APIs
text:
REST API Basics
REST (Representational State Transfer) APIs use standard HTTP methods:



Method
Purpose
Example




GET
Retrieve data
Fetch CPI values


POST
Send data
Submit a query


PUT
Update data
Modify a record


DELETE
Remove data
Delete a record



For data analysis, we primarily use GET requests.

Anatomy of an API Request

# A typical API endpoint structure
base_url = "https://api.stlouisfed.org/fred/series/observations"

# Query parameters specify what data we want
params = {
    "series_id": "CPIAUCSL",      # CPI for All Urban Consumers
    "api_key": "YOUR_API_KEY",     # Authentication
    "file_type": "json",           # Response format
    "observation_start": "2020-01-01",
    "observation_end": "2024-01-01"
}

# The full URL would be:
# https://api.stlouisfed.org/fred/series/observations?series_id=CPIAUCSL&api_key=...

title: Working with APIs
text:
The requests Library
The requests library is Python’s standard for HTTP communication.

# Install if needed
# pip install requests

import requests

# Basic GET request
response = requests.get("https://httpbin.org/get")

# Check if successful (status code 200)
print(f"Status code: {response.status_code}")
print(f"Success: {response.ok}")

# Get response content
data = response.json()  # Parse JSON response
print(data)







TipIn R





# httr2 approach
library(httr2)
response <- request("https://api.example.com/data") %>%
  req_headers("Authorization" = paste("Bearer", api_key)) %>%
  req_perform()

data <- response %>% resp_body_json()




Making Requests with Parameters

# Pass parameters to the request
params = {
    "series_id": "CPIAUCSL",
    "api_key": os.environ.get("FRED_API_KEY"),
    "file_type": "json"
}

response = requests.get(
    "https://api.stlouisfed.org/fred/series/observations",
    params=params
)

# requests automatically encodes the URL
print(f"Request URL: {response.url}")



Handling Headers
Some APIs require specific headers:

headers = {
    "Content-Type": "application/json",
    "Authorization": f"Bearer {api_key}",
    "User-Agent": "UW-Salary-Analysis/1.0"
}

response = requests.get(url, headers=headers)

title: Working with APIs
text:
The FRED API
The Federal Reserve Economic Data (FRED) API provides access to thousands of economic time series.

Getting an API Key

Go to https://fred.stlouisfed.org/docs/api/api_key.html
Create a free account
Request an API key
Store it securely (environment variable or config file)



Setting Up Your API Key

In PyCharm, set environment variables via Run → Edit Configurations → Environment variables. Add FRED_API_KEY=your_key_here. This keeps your key out of your code.


import os

# Option 1: Environment variable (recommended)
api_key = os.environ.get("FRED_API_KEY")

# Option 2: Config file (add to .gitignore!)
# with open("config/secrets.json") as f:
#     config = json.load(f)
#     api_key = config["fred_api_key"]



Fetching CPI Data

def fetch_fred_series(series_id, start_date, end_date, api_key):
    """Fetch a time series from FRED API."""
    url = "https://api.stlouisfed.org/fred/series/observations"

    params = {
        "series_id": series_id,
        "api_key": api_key,
        "file_type": "json",
        "observation_start": start_date,
        "observation_end": end_date
    }

    response = requests.get(url, params=params)

    # Check for errors
    if not response.ok:
        raise Exception(f"API Error: {response.status_code} - {response.text}")

    return response.json()

# Fetch CPI data
data = fetch_fred_series(
    series_id="CPIAUCSL",
    start_date="2020-01-01",
    end_date="2024-12-01",
    api_key=os.environ.get("FRED_API_KEY")
)



Using the fredapi Library
For convenience, the fredapi library wraps the FRED API:

# pip install fredapi
from fredapi import Fred

fred = Fred(api_key=os.environ.get("FRED_API_KEY"))

# Fetch CPI series
cpi = fred.get_series("CPIAUCSL", observation_start="2020-01-01")

# Returns a pandas Series directly!
print(type(cpi))
print(cpi.head())

title: Working with APIs
text:
Parsing JSON Responses
Most APIs return JSON (JavaScript Object Notation). Here’s how to parse it into pandas:

# Simulated API response (what FRED returns)
sample_response = {
    "realtime_start": "2024-01-01",
    "realtime_end": "2024-01-01",
    "observation_start": "2020-01-01",
    "observation_end": "2024-01-01",
    "units": "lin",
    "output_type": 1,
    "file_type": "json",
    "order_by": "observation_date",
    "sort_order": "asc",
    "count": 5,
    "offset": 0,
    "limit": 100000,
    "observations": [
        {"realtime_start": "2024-01-01", "realtime_end": "2024-01-01",
         "date": "2020-01-01", "value": "257.971"},
        {"realtime_start": "2024-01-01", "realtime_end": "2024-01-01",
         "date": "2020-02-01", "value": "258.678"},
        {"realtime_start": "2024-01-01", "realtime_end": "2024-01-01",
         "date": "2020-03-01", "value": "258.115"},
        {"realtime_start": "2024-01-01", "realtime_end": "2024-01-01",
         "date": "2020-04-01", "value": "256.389"},
        {"realtime_start": "2024-01-01", "realtime_end": "2024-01-01",
         "date": "2020-05-01", "value": "256.394"}
    ]
}


Extracting the Data

# The data we need is nested in "observations"
observations = sample_response["observations"]

# Convert to DataFrame
df = pd.DataFrame(observations)
print("Raw DataFrame:")
print(df)



Cleaning the Data

# Convert types
df["date"] = pd.to_datetime(df["date"])
df["value"] = pd.to_numeric(df["value"], errors="coerce")

# Select only needed columns
cpi_df = df[["date", "value"]].copy()
cpi_df.columns = ["date", "cpi"]

print("\nCleaned DataFrame:")
print(cpi_df)



Handling Missing Values

# FRED uses "." for missing values
sample_with_missing = {
    "observations": [
        {"date": "2020-01-01", "value": "257.971"},
        {"date": "2020-02-01", "value": "."},  # Missing!
        {"date": "2020-03-01", "value": "258.115"}
    ]
}

df = pd.DataFrame(sample_with_missing["observations"])
df["value"] = pd.to_numeric(df["value"], errors="coerce")  # "." becomes NaN

print("After handling missing:")
print(df)
print(f"\nMissing values: {df['value'].isna().sum()}")

title: Working with APIs
text:
Authentication and Rate Limiting

API Key Authentication

# Method 1: Query parameter (FRED style)
params = {"api_key": api_key, ...}
response = requests.get(url, params=params)

# Method 2: Header (Bearer token)
headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {api_key}"}
response = requests.get(url, headers=headers)

# Method 3: Basic auth
response = requests.get(url, auth=(username, password))



Rate Limiting
Most APIs limit how many requests you can make. Handle this gracefully:

import time

def fetch_with_retry(url, params, max_retries=3, delay=1):
    """Fetch data with automatic retry on rate limit."""
    for attempt in range(max_retries):
        response = requests.get(url, params=params)

        if response.ok:
            return response.json()

        # Rate limited (429) or server error (5xx)
        if response.status_code == 429 or response.status_code >= 500:
            wait_time = delay * (2 ** attempt)  # Exponential backoff
            print(f"Rate limited. Waiting {wait_time}s before retry...")
            time.sleep(wait_time)
            continue

        # Other error - don't retry
        response.raise_for_status()

    raise Exception(f"Failed after {max_retries} attempts")



Respecting Rate Limits

def fetch_multiple_series(series_ids, api_key, delay_between=0.5):
    """Fetch multiple series with delay to respect rate limits."""
    results = {}

    for series_id in series_ids:
        print(f"Fetching {series_id}...")
        # ... fetch logic here ...
        results[series_id] = None  # placeholder

        # Be a good API citizen
        time.sleep(delay_between)

    return results

# Example usage
series_to_fetch = ["CPIAUCSL", "UNRATE", "GDP"]
# data = fetch_multiple_series(series_to_fetch, api_key)

title: Working with APIs
text:
Implementing Caching
Caching saves API responses locally to avoid repeated calls.

Simple File-Based Cache

import hashlib
from pathlib import Path

def get_cache_path(url, params, cache_dir="data/cache"):
    """Generate a unique cache filename for a request."""
    # Create a hash of the request
    cache_key = hashlib.md5(f"{url}{sorted(params.items())}".encode()).hexdigest()
    return Path(cache_dir) / f"{cache_key}.json"

def fetch_with_cache(url, params, cache_dir="data/cache", max_age_days=7):
    """Fetch data with file-based caching."""
    cache_path = get_cache_path(url, params, cache_dir)

    # Check if cache exists and is fresh
    if cache_path.exists():
        cache_age = datetime.now() - datetime.fromtimestamp(cache_path.stat().st_mtime)
        if cache_age < timedelta(days=max_age_days):
            print(f"Using cached data (age: {cache_age.days} days)")
            with open(cache_path) as f:
                return json.load(f)

    # Fetch fresh data
    print("Fetching fresh data from API...")
    response = requests.get(url, params=params)
    response.raise_for_status()
    data = response.json()

    # Save to cache
    cache_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    with open(cache_path, "w") as f:
        json.dump(data, f)

    return data


PyCharm’s debugger is invaluable when working with APIs. Set breakpoints to inspect response objects and understand the data structure before writing parsing code.



Complete CPI Pipeline with Caching

class FREDClient:
    """Client for FRED API with built-in caching."""

    def __init__(self, api_key, cache_dir="data/cache"):
        self.api_key = api_key
        self.cache_dir = Path(cache_dir)
        self.base_url = "https://api.stlouisfed.org/fred"

    def _get_cache_path(self, series_id, start_date, end_date):
        """Generate cache filename."""
        key = f"{series_id}_{start_date}_{end_date}"
        return self.cache_dir / f"fred_{key}.csv"

    def get_series(self, series_id, start_date, end_date, max_cache_days=30):
        """Fetch a series, using cache if available."""
        cache_path = self._get_cache_path(series_id, start_date, end_date)

        # Check cache
        if cache_path.exists():
            cache_age = datetime.now() - datetime.fromtimestamp(cache_path.stat().st_mtime)
            if cache_age.days < max_cache_days:
                return pd.read_csv(cache_path, parse_dates=["date"])

        # Fetch from API
        url = f"{self.base_url}/series/observations"
        params = {
            "series_id": series_id,
            "api_key": self.api_key,
            "file_type": "json",
            "observation_start": start_date,
            "observation_end": end_date
        }

        response = requests.get(url, params=params)
        response.raise_for_status()

        # Parse response
        data = response.json()
        df = pd.DataFrame(data["observations"])
        df["date"] = pd.to_datetime(df["date"])
        df["value"] = pd.to_numeric(df["value"], errors="coerce")
        df = df[["date", "value"]].rename(columns={"value": series_id.lower()})

        # Save cache
        self.cache_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
        df.to_csv(cache_path, index=False)

        return df

# Usage
client = FREDClient(api_key=os.environ.get("FRED_API_KEY"))
cpi = client.get_series("CPIAUCSL", "2020-01-01", "2024-12-01")

title: Working with APIs
text:
Working with Other APIs

Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS)

import requests

def fetch_bls_data(series_ids, start_year, end_year, api_key=None):
    """Fetch data from BLS API v2."""
    url = "https://api.bls.gov/publicAPI/v2/timeseries/data/"

    payload = {
        "seriesid": series_ids,
        "startyear": str(start_year),
        "endyear": str(end_year)
    }

    if api_key:
        payload["registrationkey"] = api_key

    response = requests.post(url, json=payload)
    return response.json()

# CPI series: CUSR0000SA0 (CPI-U, All items)
# data = fetch_bls_data(["CUSR0000SA0"], 2020, 2024)



Census Bureau

def fetch_census_data(dataset, year, variables, geography, api_key):
    """Fetch data from Census API."""
    url = f"https://api.census.gov/data/{year}/{dataset}"

    params = {
        "get": ",".join(variables),
        "for": geography,
        "key": api_key
    }

    response = requests.get(url, params=params)
    data = response.json()

    # First row is headers, rest is data
    df = pd.DataFrame(data[1:], columns=data[0])
    return df

# Example: Population by state
# df = fetch_census_data("acs/acs5", 2022, ["NAME", "B01001_001E"], "state:*", api_key)

title: Working with APIs
Working with APIs
text:
Exercise

Exercise 15: Building a CPI Data Pipeline

Register for a free FRED API key
Create a function to fetch CPI data (series CPIAUCSL) for the past 5 years
Parse the JSON response into a DataFrame with date and cpi columns
Calculate the year-over-year inflation rate
Implement caching so subsequent runs don’t hit the API
Handle potential errors (network issues, invalid API key)







NoteSolution






import pandas as pd
import requests
import json
from pathlib import Path
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
import os

class CPIPipeline:
    """Pipeline for fetching and processing CPI data."""

    def __init__(self, api_key, cache_dir="data/cache"):
        self.api_key = api_key
        self.cache_dir = Path(cache_dir)
        self.cache_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

    def _get_cache_path(self):
        """Get path to cache file."""
        return self.cache_dir / "cpi_data.csv"

    def _is_cache_valid(self, max_age_days=7):
        """Check if cache exists and is recent."""
        cache_path = self._get_cache_path()
        if not cache_path.exists():
            return False

        cache_age = datetime.now() - datetime.fromtimestamp(cache_path.stat().st_mtime)
        return cache_age < timedelta(days=max_age_days)

    # 2. Fetch CPI data
    def fetch_cpi(self, years_back=5):
        """Fetch CPI data from FRED API."""
        end_date = datetime.now().strftime("%Y-%m-%d")
        start_date = (datetime.now() - timedelta(days=365 * years_back)).strftime("%Y-%m-%d")

        url = "https://api.stlouisfed.org/fred/series/observations"
        params = {
            "series_id": "CPIAUCSL",
            "api_key": self.api_key,
            "file_type": "json",
            "observation_start": start_date,
            "observation_end": end_date
        }

        # 6. Error handling
        try:
            response = requests.get(url, params=params, timeout=30)

            if response.status_code == 401:
                raise ValueError("Invalid API key")
            elif response.status_code == 429:
                raise ValueError("Rate limit exceeded - try again later")

            response.raise_for_status()
            return response.json()

        except requests.exceptions.RequestException as e:
            raise ConnectionError(f"Failed to fetch data: {e}")

    # 3. Parse JSON response
    def parse_response(self, data):
        """Parse FRED API response into DataFrame."""
        observations = data.get("observations", [])

        df = pd.DataFrame(observations)
        df["date"] = pd.to_datetime(df["date"])
        df["cpi"] = pd.to_numeric(df["value"], errors="coerce")

        return df[["date", "cpi"]].dropna()

    # 4. Calculate year-over-year inflation
    def calculate_inflation(self, df):
        """Add year-over-year inflation rate."""
        df = df.sort_values("date").copy()

        # YoY inflation: (CPI_now / CPI_12_months_ago - 1) * 100
        df["cpi_12m_ago"] = df["cpi"].shift(12)
        df["yoy_inflation"] = ((df["cpi"] / df["cpi_12m_ago"]) - 1) * 100

        return df

    # 5. Implement caching
    def get_cpi_data(self, years_back=5, use_cache=True):
        """Get CPI data with caching."""
        cache_path = self._get_cache_path()

        # Try cache first
        if use_cache and self._is_cache_valid():
            print("Loading from cache...")
            return pd.read_csv(cache_path, parse_dates=["date"])

        # Fetch fresh data
        print("Fetching fresh data from FRED...")
        raw_data = self.fetch_cpi(years_back)
        df = self.parse_response(raw_data)
        df = self.calculate_inflation(df)

        # Save to cache
        df.to_csv(cache_path, index=False)
        print(f"Saved to cache: {cache_path}")

        return df

# Usage
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Get API key from environment
    api_key = os.environ.get("FRED_API_KEY")

    if not api_key:
        print("Please set FRED_API_KEY environment variable")
    else:
        pipeline = CPIPipeline(api_key)

        try:
            cpi_data = pipeline.get_cpi_data(years_back=5)

            print("\nCPI Data (last 12 months):")
            print(cpi_data.tail(12))

            print("\nLatest YoY Inflation:")
            latest = cpi_data.dropna().iloc[-1]
            print(f"  {latest['date'].strftime('%B %Y')}: {latest['yoy_inflation']:.1f}%")

        except ValueError as e:
            print(f"API Error: {e}")
        except ConnectionError as e:
            print(f"Connection Error: {e}")

title: Working with APIs
text:
Summary
You’ve learned to work with APIs:

REST Basics: GET requests, query parameters, headers
requests Library: Making HTTP requests in Python
FRED API: Fetching economic data with fredapi or raw requests
JSON Parsing: Converting API responses to DataFrames
Authentication: API keys and bearer tokens
Rate Limiting: Respecting API limits with delays and retries
Caching: Saving responses locally to avoid repeated calls
Error Handling: Gracefully handling network and API errors

Key insights: - Always check for an API before scraping - Store API keys securely (environment variables, not in code) - Implement caching to be a good API citizen - Handle errors gracefully with informative messages

title: Static Visualization with Plotly
text:
After this lesson, you will be able to:

Create figure objects and traces in Plotly
Build line charts, bar charts, and distribution plots
Use subplots and faceting for multi-panel displays
Apply colorblind-friendly palettes
Customize axes, legends, and annotations

title: Static Visualization with Plotly
text:
Introduction
Plotly creates interactive visualizations that work well in Quarto documents. Unlike matplotlib, Plotly charts have built-in zoom, pan, and hover features.

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots

# Sample salary trend data
dates = pd.date_range("2021-01", periods=8, freq="6MS")
trends = pd.DataFrame({
    "date": list(dates) * 3,
    "category": ["Faculty"] * 8 + ["Academic Staff"] * 8 + ["University Staff"] * 8,
    "median_salary": (
        [95000, 97000, 99000, 101000, 103000, 105000, 107000, 110000] +
        [72000, 73500, 75000, 76500, 78000, 79500, 81000, 83000] +
        [55000, 56000, 57000, 58000, 59000, 60000, 61000, 62500]
    )
})

title: Static Visualization with Plotly
text:
Plotly Express: Quick Charts
Plotly Express provides high-level functions for common chart types:

Line Charts

fig = px.line(
    trends,
    x="date",
    y="median_salary",
    color="category",
    title="Median Salary Trends by Employee Category",
    labels={"median_salary": "Median Salary ($)", "date": "Year", "category": "Category"}
)

fig.update_layout(
    legend=dict(orientation="h", yanchor="bottom", y=-0.3),
    hovermode="x unified"
)

fig.show()







TipIn R





library(plotly)
library(ggplot2)

# ggplot2 approach
p <- ggplot(trends, aes(x = date, y = median_salary, color = category)) +
  geom_line() +
  labs(title = "Median Salary Trends")

ggplotly(p)





Bar Charts

# Latest salary by category
latest = trends[trends["date"] == trends["date"].max()]

fig = px.bar(
    latest,
    x="category",
    y="median_salary",
    color="category",
    title="Current Median Salary by Category"
)

fig.update_layout(showlegend=False)
fig.show()



Distribution Plots

# Generate salary distribution data
np.random.seed(42)
salary_dist = pd.DataFrame({
    "salary": np.concatenate([
        np.random.normal(100000, 15000, 100),
        np.random.normal(75000, 12000, 150),
        np.random.normal(58000, 8000, 120)
    ]),
    "category": ["Faculty"] * 100 + ["Academic Staff"] * 150 + ["University Staff"] * 120
})

fig = px.box(
    salary_dist,
    x="category",
    y="salary",
    color="category",
    title="Salary Distribution by Category"
)
fig.show()

title: Static Visualization with Plotly
text:
Graph Objects: More Control
For complex visualizations, use graph_objects:

fig = go.Figure()

# Add traces for each category
for cat in trends["category"].unique():
    cat_data = trends[trends["category"] == cat]
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=cat_data["date"],
        y=cat_data["median_salary"],
        mode="lines+markers",
        name=cat,
        hovertemplate="%{y:$,.0f}<extra></extra>"
    ))

fig.update_layout(
    title="Salary Trends (Graph Objects)",
    xaxis_title="Date",
    yaxis_title="Median Salary",
    yaxis_tickformat="$,.0f"
)

fig.show()

title: Static Visualization with Plotly
text:
Subplots
Create multi-panel displays:

fig = make_subplots(
    rows=1, cols=2,
    subplot_titles=("Nominal Salary", "Salary Growth Rate")
)

# Panel 1: Nominal salary
for cat in ["Faculty", "Academic Staff"]:
    cat_data = trends[trends["category"] == cat]
    fig.add_trace(
        go.Scatter(x=cat_data["date"], y=cat_data["median_salary"], name=cat, mode="lines"),
        row=1, col=1
    )

# Panel 2: Growth rate (simulated)
growth_data = trends.copy()
growth_data["growth"] = growth_data.groupby("category")["median_salary"].pct_change() * 100

for cat in ["Faculty", "Academic Staff"]:
    cat_data = growth_data[growth_data["category"] == cat].dropna()
    fig.add_trace(
        go.Scatter(x=cat_data["date"], y=cat_data["growth"], name=cat, mode="lines", showlegend=False),
        row=1, col=2
    )

fig.update_layout(height=400, title_text="Salary Analysis")
fig.show()

title: Static Visualization with Plotly
text:
Colorblind-Friendly Palettes

# Wong colorblind-friendly palette
wong_palette = ["#E69F00", "#56B4E9", "#009E73", "#F0E442", "#0072B2", "#D55E00", "#CC79A7"]

fig = px.line(
    trends,
    x="date",
    y="median_salary",
    color="category",
    color_discrete_sequence=wong_palette,
    title="Using Colorblind-Friendly Palette"
)
fig.show()

title: Static Visualization with Plotly
text:
Customization

fig = px.line(trends, x="date", y="median_salary", color="category")

fig.update_layout(
    title=dict(text="Customized Chart", x=0.5, font=dict(size=20)),
    xaxis=dict(title="Year", showgrid=True, gridcolor="lightgray"),
    yaxis=dict(title="Salary ($)", tickformat="$,.0f", showgrid=True),
    legend=dict(title="Category", orientation="h", y=-0.2),
    plot_bgcolor="white",
    font=dict(family="Arial", size=12)
)

fig.show()

title: Static Visualization with Plotly
text:
Exercise

Exercise 13: Salary Visualization Suite

Create a line chart showing salary trends over time
Create a bar chart comparing current salaries across divisions
Create a box plot showing salary distributions
Apply a colorblind-friendly palette
Create a two-panel subplot comparing nominal vs real salary







NoteSolution






import plotly.express as px
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots
import plotly.graph_objects as go

# Sample data
divisions = ["L&S", "Medicine", "Engineering"]
df = pd.DataFrame({
    "division": divisions * 2,
    "year": [2022] * 3 + [2024] * 3,
    "salary": [85000, 105000, 95000, 90000, 112000, 102000],
    "real_salary": [85000, 105000, 95000, 82000, 102000, 93000]
})

# 1. Line chart
fig1 = px.line(df, x="year", y="salary", color="division", title="1. Salary Trends")
fig1.show()

# 2. Bar chart (2024)
current = df[df["year"] == 2024]
fig2 = px.bar(current, x="division", y="salary", title="2. Current Salaries by Division")
fig2.show()

# 3. Box plot
np.random.seed(42)
box_data = pd.DataFrame({
    "salary": np.concatenate([np.random.normal(m, 10000, 30) for m in [85000, 105000, 95000]]),
    "division": ["L&S"] * 30 + ["Medicine"] * 30 + ["Engineering"] * 30
})
fig3 = px.box(box_data, x="division", y="salary", title="3. Salary Distributions")
fig3.show()

# 4. Colorblind palette (already applied)
wong = ["#E69F00", "#56B4E9", "#009E73"]
fig4 = px.bar(current, x="division", y="salary", color="division",
              color_discrete_sequence=wong, title="4. Colorblind-Friendly")
fig4.show()

# 5. Subplot
fig5 = make_subplots(rows=1, cols=2, subplot_titles=("Nominal", "Real (2021$)"))
for i, div in enumerate(divisions):
    d = df[df["division"] == div]
    fig5.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=d["year"], y=d["salary"], name=div), row=1, col=1)
    fig5.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=d["year"], y=d["real_salary"], name=div, showlegend=False), row=1, col=2)
fig5.update_layout(title="5. Nominal vs Real Salary")
fig5.show()

title: Workforce Analytics: Headcount and FTE
text:
After this lesson, you will be able to:

Distinguish between headcount and FTE (Full-Time Equivalent) metrics
Calculate FTE-adjusted salary and compensation totals
Analyze headcount and FTE trends over time
Measure departmental composition changes
Identify staffing pattern shifts post-reorganization

title: Workforce Analytics: Headcount and FTE
text:
Introduction

Key Workforce Concept: Headcount vs FTE
Headcount counts each person once, regardless of work hours.
FTE (Full-Time Equivalent) measures work capacity: - A full-time employee = 1.0 FTE - A half-time employee = 0.5 FTE - Two half-time employees = 1.0 FTE total
For workforce planning and cost analysis, FTE is often more meaningful than headcount.


Revenue Sources and Cost Drivers in Higher Education
To understand why workforce metrics matter, you need to understand the financial structure of a public university:
Revenue side:

State appropriations (GPR): At UW-Madison, state funding covers roughly 15-18% of the operating budget — down from over 30% in the 1990s. This decline is the single biggest driver of budget pressure.
Tuition and fees: Subject to the Board of Regents tuition freeze since 2013 (with limited recent increases), capping a major revenue source.
Federal research grants: UW-Madison is a top-5 research institution (~$1.5B/year in research expenditures), but grant funding covers specific project costs, not general operations.
Auxiliary and other: Clinical revenue (UW Health), gifts, and self-supporting operations.

Cost side:

Personnel costs are 70-80% of the operating budget at most universities. Salaries, fringe benefits (health insurance, retirement), and related costs dominate spending.
Every FTE change directly moves the budget. Adding 10 FTE at an average total compensation of $120K costs $1.2M annually — a recurring obligation, not a one-time expense.

This is why headcount and FTE trend analysis isn’t just an HR exercise — it’s the most important input to financial planning. When you calculate that a division’s FTE grew 5% while its budget grew 2%, you’ve identified a structural deficit.


import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Create multi-period workforce data
np.random.seed(42)

dates = ["2022-01", "2022-07", "2023-01", "2023-07", "2024-01"]
divisions = ["Letters & Science", "Medicine", "Engineering"]
categories = ["Faculty", "Academic Staff", "University Staff"]

records = []
for date in dates:
    for div in divisions:
        n_employees = np.random.randint(15, 30)
        for i in range(n_employees):
            records.append({
                "date": date,
                "employee_id": f"{div[:3]}{date[:4]}{i:03d}",
                "division": div,
                "employee_category": np.random.choice(categories, p=[0.3, 0.4, 0.3]),
                "fte": np.random.choice([1.0, 0.75, 0.5, 0.25], p=[0.7, 0.15, 0.1, 0.05]),
                "salary": np.random.randint(45000, 150000)
            })

df = pd.DataFrame(records)
df["date"] = pd.to_datetime(df["date"])
print(f"Total records: {len(df)}")
df.head(10)

title: Workforce Analytics: Headcount and FTE
text:
Headcount vs FTE Calculations

Basic Counts

# Headcount by division and date
headcount = df.groupby(["date", "division"]).size().reset_index(name="headcount")
headcount_pivot = headcount.pivot(index="division", columns="date", values="headcount")
print("Headcount by Division:")
headcount_pivot


# FTE by division and date
fte = df.groupby(["date", "division"])["fte"].sum().reset_index()
fte_pivot = fte.pivot(index="division", columns="date", values="fte")
print("FTE by Division:")
fte_pivot.round(1)



Average FTE per Employee

# Average FTE shows proportion of part-time work
avg_fte = df.groupby(["date", "division"]).agg(
    headcount=("employee_id", "count"),
    total_fte=("fte", "sum")
).reset_index()

avg_fte["avg_fte_per_employee"] = (avg_fte["total_fte"] / avg_fte["headcount"]).round(2)
avg_fte_pivot = avg_fte.pivot(index="division", columns="date", values="avg_fte_per_employee")
print("Average FTE per Employee:")
avg_fte_pivot







TipIn R





# dplyr approach
df %>%
  group_by(date, division) %>%
  summarize(
    headcount = n(),
    total_fte = sum(fte),
    avg_fte = mean(fte)
  )

title: Workforce Analytics: Headcount and FTE
text:
Analyzing Workforce Trends

Change Over Time

# Calculate change from first to last period
first_date = df["date"].min()
last_date = df["date"].max()

first_period = df[df["date"] == first_date].groupby("division")["fte"].sum()
last_period = df[df["date"] == last_date].groupby("division")["fte"].sum()

fte_change = pd.DataFrame({
    "fte_start": first_period,
    "fte_end": last_period
})
fte_change["change"] = fte_change["fte_end"] - fte_change["fte_start"]
fte_change["pct_change"] = ((fte_change["fte_end"] / fte_change["fte_start"]) - 1) * 100

fte_change.round(1)



Composition Analysis

# Employee category composition by division
composition = df.groupby(["date", "division", "employee_category"])["fte"].sum().reset_index()

# Calculate percentage within each division-date
composition["total_fte"] = composition.groupby(["date", "division"])["fte"].transform("sum")
composition["pct_of_division"] = (composition["fte"] / composition["total_fte"] * 100).round(1)

# Show latest period composition
latest = composition[composition["date"] == last_date]
composition_pivot = latest.pivot(index="division", columns="employee_category", values="pct_of_division")
print("Employee Category Mix (% of FTE):")
composition_pivot

title: Workforce Analytics: Headcount and FTE
text:
Cost Analysis

Total Salary Expenditure

# FTE-adjusted salary represents actual cost
df["fte_adjusted_salary"] = df["salary"] * df["fte"]

cost_by_division = df.groupby(["date", "division"]).agg(
    headcount=("employee_id", "count"),
    total_fte=("fte", "sum"),
    total_nominal_salary=("salary", "sum"),
    total_fte_adjusted_salary=("fte_adjusted_salary", "sum")
).reset_index()

cost_by_division["avg_cost_per_fte"] = (
    cost_by_division["total_fte_adjusted_salary"] / cost_by_division["total_fte"]
).round(0)

# Latest period
latest_cost = cost_by_division[cost_by_division["date"] == last_date]
latest_cost[["division", "total_fte", "total_fte_adjusted_salary", "avg_cost_per_fte"]]

title: Workforce Analytics: Headcount and FTE
text:
Detecting Organizational Changes

Division Appearance/Disappearance

# Which divisions appear in which periods?
div_by_date = df.groupby(["date", "division"]).size().unstack(fill_value=0)

# Divisions present in first but not last period (or vice versa)
first_divisions = set(df[df["date"] == first_date]["division"].unique())
last_divisions = set(df[df["date"] == last_date]["division"].unique())

print("Divisions in first period only:", first_divisions - last_divisions)
print("Divisions in last period only:", last_divisions - first_divisions)
print("Divisions in both:", first_divisions & last_divisions)



Significant FTE Changes

# Find divisions with large FTE changes
significant_threshold = 0.20  # 20% change

fte_by_div_date = df.groupby(["date", "division"])["fte"].sum().reset_index()
fte_wide = fte_by_div_date.pivot(index="division", columns="date", values="fte")

# Calculate overall change
fte_wide["change_pct"] = (
    (fte_wide[last_date] / fte_wide[first_date]) - 1
) * 100

significant_changes = fte_wide[abs(fte_wide["change_pct"]) > significant_threshold * 100]
print(f"Divisions with >{significant_threshold:.0%} FTE change:")
significant_changes[["change_pct"]].round(1)

title: Workforce Analytics: Headcount and FTE
text:
Full-Time vs Part-Time Analysis

# Categorize employees by FTE level
df["fte_category"] = pd.cut(
    df["fte"],
    bins=[0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0],
    labels=["<25%", "25-50%", "50-75%", "75-100%"]
)

# Distribution of FTE categories
fte_dist = df.groupby(["date", "fte_category"]).size().unstack(fill_value=0)
fte_dist["total"] = fte_dist.sum(axis=1)

# Calculate percentages
for col in fte_dist.columns[:-1]:
    fte_dist[f"{col}_pct"] = (fte_dist[col] / fte_dist["total"] * 100).round(1)

fte_dist[[c for c in fte_dist.columns if "_pct" in c]]

title: Workforce Analytics: Headcount and FTE
text:
Exercise

Exercise 11: Workforce Analysis

Create workforce data with 3 divisions, 2 time periods, and varying FTE levels
Calculate headcount and FTE totals by division for each period
Identify which division had the largest FTE growth
Calculate the percentage of employees who are part-time (<1.0 FTE)
Compute the total salary cost per FTE by division
Find divisions where the employee category mix changed significantly







NoteSolution






import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# 1. Create workforce data
np.random.seed(99)
data = []
divisions = ["L&S", "Medicine", "Engineering"]
dates = ["2023-01", "2024-01"]

for date in dates:
    for div in divisions:
        n = np.random.randint(20, 35)
        for i in range(n):
            data.append({
                "date": date,
                "employee_id": f"{div[:1]}{date[-2:]}{i:02d}",
                "division": div,
                "category": np.random.choice(["Faculty", "Staff"], p=[0.35, 0.65]),
                "fte": np.random.choice([1.0, 0.75, 0.5], p=[0.65, 0.20, 0.15]),
                "salary": np.random.randint(50000, 130000)
            })

df = pd.DataFrame(data)
df["date"] = pd.to_datetime(df["date"])
print("1. Sample data:")
print(df.head())

# 2. Headcount and FTE by division and period
summary = df.groupby(["date", "division"]).agg(
    headcount=("employee_id", "count"),
    total_fte=("fte", "sum")
).reset_index()
print("\n2. Headcount and FTE:")
print(summary)

# 3. Division with largest FTE growth
fte_wide = summary.pivot(index="division", columns="date", values="total_fte")
fte_wide["growth"] = fte_wide.iloc[:, 1] - fte_wide.iloc[:, 0]
print("\n3. FTE growth by division:")
print(fte_wide)
print(f"Largest growth: {fte_wide['growth'].idxmax()}")

# 4. Percentage part-time
df["is_part_time"] = df["fte"] < 1.0
part_time_pct = df.groupby("date")["is_part_time"].mean() * 100
print("\n4. Percentage part-time:")
print(part_time_pct.round(1))

# 5. Cost per FTE
df["fte_salary"] = df["salary"] * df["fte"]
cost = df.groupby(["date", "division"]).agg(
    total_fte=("fte", "sum"),
    total_cost=("fte_salary", "sum")
)
cost["cost_per_fte"] = (cost["total_cost"] / cost["total_fte"]).round(0)
print("\n5. Cost per FTE:")
print(cost["cost_per_fte"])

# 6. Category mix changes
mix = df.groupby(["date", "division", "category"])["fte"].sum()
mix_pct = mix / mix.groupby(["date", "division"]).transform("sum") * 100
print("\n6. Category mix by division:")
print(mix_pct.round(1).unstack())

title: Workforce Analytics: Headcount and FTE
text:
Summary
You’ve learned workforce analytics fundamentals:

Headcount vs FTE: Use FTE for capacity and cost analysis
Trend analysis: Calculate changes over time with pivot tables
Composition: Analyze category mix within divisions
Cost metrics: FTE-adjusted salary for true cost analysis
Change detection: Identify significant organizational shifts

Key insights: - FTE is more useful than headcount for most analyses - Look at both absolute and percentage changes - Monitor composition shifts that may indicate strategy changes

title: Inflation Adjustment and Real vs Nominal Values
text:
After this lesson, you will be able to:

Explain the difference between nominal and real (inflation-adjusted) values
Understand the Consumer Price Index (CPI) and how it measures inflation
Fetch CPI data from the FRED API
Calculate real salaries in constant dollars
Interpret real vs nominal salary changes
crumbs: Inflation Adjustment and Real vs Nominal Values

title: Inflation Adjustment and Real vs Nominal Values
text:
Introduction

Key Financial Concept: Real vs Nominal Values
Nominal values are raw dollar amounts at their face value. A $50,000 salary in 2021 is simply $50,000.
Real values adjust for inflation to enable meaningful comparisons across time. That $50,000 in 2021 might have the purchasing power of $55,000 in 2024 dollars due to inflation.
Without inflation adjustment, a 3% raise during 5% inflation is actually a pay cut in purchasing power.


HE Budget Pressure and Baumol’s Cost Disease
Higher education suffers from what economist William Baumol called the “cost disease” of labor-intensive industries. A university can’t increase faculty productivity the way a factory automates production — a class still needs an instructor, a lab still needs a PI. Yet universities compete for talent in markets where salaries rise with the broader economy.
The result: salary costs grow faster than many public revenue sources. Wisconsin state appropriations to UW have been essentially flat in real terms since 2008, while health insurance costs and salary market rates have risen steadily. This structural gap means that even maintaining current salary levels requires finding new revenue or cutting elsewhere.
When you inflation-adjust salary data, you’re not just doing arithmetic — you’re measuring how well the institution has kept pace with economic reality. A finding that “median faculty salaries grew 12% over 10 years” sounds positive until you show that CPI rose 25% over the same period, revealing a 13% real decline in purchasing power. This is the kind of analysis that drives budget requests and policy discussions.

Understanding real vs nominal values is essential for salary analysis. Between 2021 and 2024, cumulative inflation was roughly 18%. An employee whose nominal salary stayed flat actually experienced an 18% real pay cut.

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Sample salary data across time
salary_data = pd.DataFrame({
    "employee_id": ["E001", "E001", "E001", "E001"],
    "date": pd.to_datetime(["2021-11-01", "2022-08-01", "2023-10-01", "2024-09-01"]),
    "salary": [80000, 82000, 84000, 86000]
})

salary_data
crumbs: Inflation Adjustment and Real vs Nominal Values

title: Inflation Adjustment and Real vs Nominal Values
text:
Understanding the Consumer Price Index
The Consumer Price Index (CPI) measures the average change over time in prices paid by consumers for a basket of goods and services.
Key points: - Published monthly by the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) - The most common series is CPI-U (All Urban Consumers) - Higher CPI = higher prices = less purchasing power per dollar

# Sample CPI data (CPIAUCSL - All Urban Consumers, Seasonally Adjusted)
cpi_data = pd.DataFrame({
    "date": pd.to_datetime(["2021-11-01", "2022-08-01", "2023-10-01", "2024-09-01"]),
    "cpi": [277.948, 296.171, 307.671, 314.796]
})

# Calculate year-over-year inflation
cpi_data["yoy_inflation"] = cpi_data["cpi"].pct_change(periods=1) * 100

cpi_data


CPI Interpretation

# How much did prices increase from 2021-11 to 2024-09?
start_cpi = 277.948
end_cpi = 314.796

total_inflation = (end_cpi / start_cpi - 1) * 100
print(f"Total inflation from Nov 2021 to Sep 2024: {total_inflation:.1f}%")
print(f"A dollar in 2021 is worth ${start_cpi/end_cpi:.2f} in 2024 purchasing power")
crumbs: Inflation Adjustment and Real vs Nominal Values

title: Inflation Adjustment and Real vs Nominal Values
text:
Calculating Real Salaries
The formula for converting to constant dollars:
\[\text{Real Salary} = \text{Nominal Salary} \times \frac{\text{CPI}_{\text{base period}}}{\text{CPI}_{\text{current period}}}\]
We’ll express all salaries in 2021 dollars (our base period):

# Define base CPI (November 2021)
base_cpi = 277.948

# Calculate CPI index (multiplier to convert to 2021 dollars)
cpi_data["cpi_2021_index"] = base_cpi / cpi_data["cpi"]
cpi_data


# Merge CPI data with salary data
salary_with_cpi = pd.merge(salary_data, cpi_data[["date", "cpi", "cpi_2021_index"]], on="date")

# Calculate real salary
salary_with_cpi["real_salary_2021"] = salary_with_cpi["salary"] * salary_with_cpi["cpi_2021_index"]

salary_with_cpi


Interpreting the Results

# Compare nominal vs real changes
first_row = salary_with_cpi.iloc[0]
last_row = salary_with_cpi.iloc[-1]

nominal_change = last_row["salary"] - first_row["salary"]
nominal_pct = (nominal_change / first_row["salary"]) * 100

real_change = last_row["real_salary_2021"] - first_row["real_salary_2021"]
real_pct = (real_change / first_row["real_salary_2021"]) * 100

print(f"Nominal salary change: ${nominal_change:,.0f} ({nominal_pct:+.1f}%)")
print(f"Real salary change:    ${real_change:,.0f} ({real_pct:+.1f}%)")
print(f"\nDespite a ${nominal_change:,.0f} raise, purchasing power {'increased' if real_change > 0 else 'decreased'} by ${abs(real_change):,.0f}")







TipIn R





# Using dplyr
salary_data %>%
  left_join(cpi_data, by = "date") %>%
  mutate(
    cpi_2021_index = base_cpi / cpi,
    real_salary_2021 = salary * cpi_2021_index
  )
crumbs: Inflation Adjustment and Real vs Nominal Values

title: Inflation Adjustment and Real vs Nominal Values
text:
Fetching CPI Data from FRED
The Federal Reserve Economic Data (FRED) provides free access to CPI data via API.

Using the fredapi Library

from fredapi import Fred
import os

# Initialize with API key (get free key at https://fred.stlouisfed.org/docs/api/api_key.html)
fred = Fred(api_key=os.environ.get("FRED_API_KEY"))

# Fetch CPI data
cpi_series = fred.get_series("CPIAUCSL", observation_start="2020-01-01")

# Convert to DataFrame
cpi_df = cpi_series.reset_index()
cpi_df.columns = ["date", "cpi"]
cpi_df.head()



Caching CPI Data
To avoid repeated API calls, save the data locally:

def fetch_and_cache_cpi(api_key, cache_path="data/cpi_data.csv"):
    """Fetch CPI data from FRED and cache locally."""
    from fredapi import Fred
    import os

    # Check if cache exists and is recent
    if os.path.exists(cache_path):
        cached = pd.read_csv(cache_path, parse_dates=["date"])
        # If cache is less than 30 days old, use it
        if (pd.Timestamp.today() - cached["date"].max()).days < 30:
            return cached

    # Fetch fresh data
    fred = Fred(api_key=api_key)
    cpi_series = fred.get_series("CPIAUCSL")

    cpi_df = cpi_series.reset_index()
    cpi_df.columns = ["date", "cpi"]

    # Calculate 2021 index
    base_cpi = cpi_df[cpi_df["date"] == "2021-11-01"]["cpi"].values[0]
    cpi_df["cpi_2021_index"] = base_cpi / cpi_df["cpi"]

    # Save cache
    cpi_df.to_csv(cache_path, index=False)

    return cpi_df
crumbs: Inflation Adjustment and Real vs Nominal Values

title: Inflation Adjustment and Real vs Nominal Values
text:
Complete Inflation Adjustment Pipeline
Let’s put it all together with a realistic example:

# Simulate multiple employees across multiple periods
np.random.seed(42)

employees = pd.DataFrame({
    "employee_id": ["E001", "E002", "E003", "E004", "E005"] * 4,
    "date": (["2021-11-01"] * 5 + ["2022-08-01"] * 5 +
             ["2023-10-01"] * 5 + ["2024-09-01"] * 5),
    "last_name": ["SMITH", "JOHNSON", "WILLIAMS", "BROWN", "JONES"] * 4,
    "salary": [
        80000, 95000, 72000, 110000, 65000,  # 2021-11
        82000, 98000, 74000, 113000, 67000,  # 2022-08 (+2.5%)
        84000, 101000, 76000, 116000, 69000,  # 2023-10 (+2.5%)
        86000, 104000, 78000, 119000, 71000   # 2024-09 (+2.5%)
    ]
})
employees["date"] = pd.to_datetime(employees["date"])

# CPI data
cpi = pd.DataFrame({
    "date": pd.to_datetime(["2021-11-01", "2022-08-01", "2023-10-01", "2024-09-01"]),
    "cpi": [277.948, 296.171, 307.671, 314.796]
})

# Calculate index with 2021-11 as base
base_cpi = cpi[cpi["date"] == "2021-11-01"]["cpi"].values[0]
cpi["cpi_2021_index"] = base_cpi / cpi["cpi"]

# Merge and calculate real salaries
df = pd.merge(employees, cpi, on="date")
df["real_salary_2021"] = (df["salary"] * df["cpi_2021_index"]).round(0)

df[["last_name", "date", "salary", "cpi", "cpi_2021_index", "real_salary_2021"]].head(12)


Analyzing Real Salary Changes

# Calculate change from first to last period per employee
first_period = df[df["date"] == "2021-11-01"][["employee_id", "salary", "real_salary_2021"]]
first_period.columns = ["employee_id", "salary_2021", "real_salary_2021_base"]

last_period = df[df["date"] == "2024-09-01"][["employee_id", "last_name", "salary", "real_salary_2021"]]
last_period.columns = ["employee_id", "last_name", "salary_2024", "real_salary_2024"]

comparison = pd.merge(first_period, last_period, on="employee_id")

# Calculate changes
comparison["nominal_change"] = comparison["salary_2024"] - comparison["salary_2021"]
comparison["nominal_pct"] = (comparison["nominal_change"] / comparison["salary_2021"] * 100).round(1)
comparison["real_change"] = comparison["real_salary_2024"] - comparison["real_salary_2021_base"]
comparison["real_pct"] = (comparison["real_change"] / comparison["real_salary_2021_base"] * 100).round(1)

comparison[["last_name", "salary_2021", "salary_2024", "nominal_pct", "real_pct"]]



Visualization of Trends

# Summary by date
trend = df.groupby("date").agg({
    "salary": "median",
    "real_salary_2021": "median"
}).reset_index()

trend.columns = ["date", "median_nominal", "median_real_2021"]
trend["date_str"] = trend["date"].dt.strftime("%Y-%m")

print("Median Salary Trends (Nominal vs Real 2021 Dollars):")
print(trend)
crumbs: Inflation Adjustment and Real vs Nominal Values

title: Inflation Adjustment and Real vs Nominal Values
section: Interpreting Real vs Nominal Salary Growth
text:
Interpreting Real vs Nominal Salary Growth

What Does Negative Real Growth Mean?
If an employee’s nominal salary increased but their real salary decreased, it means:

Their raise was smaller than inflation
They can buy less with their new salary than before
They effectively took a pay cut in purchasing power

Example: A 2.5% annual raise during 5% annual inflation = 2.5% real pay cut
crumbs: Inflation Adjustment and Real vs Nominal Values

title: Inflation Adjustment and Real vs Nominal Values
text:
FTE-Adjusted Real Salary
For part-time employees, we often want the FTE-adjusted real salary:

# Add FTE to our data
df["fte"] = [1.0, 1.0, 0.8, 1.0, 0.5] * 4  # Some are part-time

# Calculate FTE-adjusted salaries (both nominal and real)
df["fte_adjusted_salary"] = df["salary"] * df["fte"]
df["fte_adjusted_real_2021"] = df["real_salary_2021"] * df["fte"]

df[["last_name", "date", "salary", "fte", "fte_adjusted_salary",
    "real_salary_2021", "fte_adjusted_real_2021"]].head(8)
crumbs: Inflation Adjustment and Real vs Nominal Values

title: Inflation Adjustment and Real vs Nominal Values
text:
Quick Reference



Concept
Formula




CPI Index (to base year)
base_cpi / current_cpi


Real Salary
nominal_salary × cpi_index


Nominal Change
salary_end - salary_start


Real Change
real_salary_end - real_salary_start


Total Inflation
(cpi_end / cpi_start - 1) × 100
crumbs: Inflation Adjustment and Real vs Nominal Values

title: Inflation Adjustment and Real vs Nominal Values
text:
Exercise

Exercise 9: Inflation-Adjusted Salary Analysis

Create a DataFrame with 4 employees and their salaries at two points (2022 and 2024)
Create CPI data for those dates (2022-01: 281.148, 2024-01: 308.417)
Calculate the CPI index using 2022 as the base year
Calculate real salaries in 2022 dollars
Determine which employees had real salary increases vs decreases
Calculate the total inflation between the two periods







NoteSolution






import pandas as pd

# 1. Employee salary data
employees = pd.DataFrame({
    "employee_id": ["E001", "E002", "E003", "E004"] * 2,
    "date": pd.to_datetime(["2022-01-01"] * 4 + ["2024-01-01"] * 4),
    "last_name": ["SMITH", "JOHNSON", "WILLIAMS", "BROWN"] * 2,
    "salary": [
        80000, 95000, 72000, 110000,  # 2022
        84000, 98000, 75000, 120000   # 2024 (various raise levels)
    ]
})

print("1. Employee salary data:")
print(employees)

# 2. CPI data
cpi = pd.DataFrame({
    "date": pd.to_datetime(["2022-01-01", "2024-01-01"]),
    "cpi": [281.148, 308.417]
})

print("\n2. CPI data:")
print(cpi)

# 3. Calculate CPI index with 2022 as base
base_cpi = cpi[cpi["date"] == "2022-01-01"]["cpi"].values[0]
cpi["cpi_2022_index"] = base_cpi / cpi["cpi"]

print("\n3. CPI index (2022 base):")
print(cpi)

# 4. Merge and calculate real salaries
df = pd.merge(employees, cpi, on="date")
df["real_salary_2022"] = (df["salary"] * df["cpi_2022_index"]).round(0)

print("\n4. Real salaries in 2022 dollars:")
print(df[["last_name", "date", "salary", "real_salary_2022"]])

# 5. Determine winners and losers
start = df[df["date"] == "2022-01-01"][["employee_id", "last_name", "salary", "real_salary_2022"]]
start.columns = ["employee_id", "last_name", "salary_2022", "real_2022"]

end = df[df["date"] == "2024-01-01"][["employee_id", "salary", "real_salary_2022"]]
end.columns = ["employee_id", "salary_2024", "real_2024"]

comparison = pd.merge(start, end, on="employee_id")
comparison["real_change"] = comparison["real_2024"] - comparison["real_2022"]
comparison["real_increase"] = comparison["real_change"] > 0

print("\n5. Real salary changes:")
print(comparison[["last_name", "salary_2022", "salary_2024", "real_change", "real_increase"]])

# 6. Total inflation
total_inflation = (cpi[cpi["date"] == "2024-01-01"]["cpi"].values[0] /
                   cpi[cpi["date"] == "2022-01-01"]["cpi"].values[0] - 1) * 100

print(f"\n6. Total inflation 2022-01 to 2024-01: {total_inflation:.1f}%")
print(f"   Employees needed at least a {total_inflation:.1f}% raise to maintain purchasing power")
crumbs: Inflation Adjustment and Real vs Nominal Values

title: Inflation Adjustment and Real vs Nominal Values
text:
Summary
You’ve learned to work with inflation-adjusted values:

Nominal vs Real: Nominal is face value; real adjusts for purchasing power
CPI: Consumer Price Index measures inflation
CPI Index: base_cpi / current_cpi gives the multiplier for constant dollars
Real Salary: nominal_salary × cpi_index
Interpretation: Negative real change = purchasing power loss despite nominal gain

Key insight: Between 2021-2024, employees needed roughly 13% cumulative raises just to maintain purchasing power. Those with smaller raises experienced real pay cuts.

title: Working with Dates and Time Series
text:
After this lesson, you will be able to:

Convert strings to datetime with pd.to_datetime()
Extract date components (year, month, day)
Perform date arithmetic and calculate durations
Work with periods and date ranges
Calculate rolling statistics over time
crumbs: Working with Dates and Time Series

title: Working with Dates and Time Series
text:
Introduction
Salary data is inherently time-based: we have snapshots at different dates, hire dates, and need to calculate tenure and salary changes over time. pandas has powerful datetime handling through the datetime64 type and the .dt accessor.

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

# Create sample data with various date formats
df = pd.DataFrame({
    "employee_id": ["E001", "E002", "E003", "E004", "E005"],
    "last_name": ["SMITH", "JOHNSON", "WILLIAMS", "BROWN", "JONES"],
    "hire_date_str": ["2010-08-15", "2005-01-10", "2020-08-20", "2015-06-01", "2018-09-01"],
    "snapshot_date": ["2024-01", "2024-01", "2024-01", "2024-01", "2024-01"],
    "salary": [125000, 185000, 95000, 175000, 65000]
})
df
crumbs: Working with Dates and Time Series

title: Working with Dates and Time Series
text:
Converting to Datetime

Using pd.to_datetime()

# Convert string to datetime
df["hire_date"] = pd.to_datetime(df["hire_date_str"])
print(df["hire_date"])
print(f"\nData type: {df['hire_date'].dtype}")







TipIn R





# lubridate approach
library(lubridate)
df$hire_date <- ymd(df$hire_date_str)
df$hire_date <- as.Date(df$hire_date_str)





Handling Different Date Formats
pd.to_datetime() is smart about parsing, but you can specify format explicitly:

# Various formats pandas can parse automatically
dates = pd.Series([
    "2024-01-15",      # ISO format
    "January 15, 2024", # Month name
    "01/15/2024",      # US format
    "15-01-2024",      # Day first
])

# Automatic parsing (pandas guesses)
parsed = pd.to_datetime(dates)
print(parsed)


# Explicit format for better performance and control
df["snapshot"] = pd.to_datetime(df["snapshot_date"], format="%Y-%m")
df["snapshot"]



Common Format Codes



Code
Meaning
Example




%Y
4-digit year
2024


%y
2-digit year
24


%m
Month (01-12)
01


%d
Day (01-31)
15


%B
Full month name
January


%b
Abbreviated month
Jan


%H
Hour (00-23)
14


%M
Minute (00-59)
30
crumbs: Working with Dates and Time Series

title: Working with Dates and Time Series
text:
Extracting Date Components
Use the .dt accessor to extract parts of dates:

# Extract components
df["hire_year"] = df["hire_date"].dt.year
df["hire_month"] = df["hire_date"].dt.month
df["hire_day"] = df["hire_date"].dt.day
df["hire_weekday"] = df["hire_date"].dt.day_name()

df[["last_name", "hire_date", "hire_year", "hire_month", "hire_weekday"]]







TipIn R





# lubridate
year(df$hire_date)
month(df$hire_date)
day(df$hire_date)
wday(df$hire_date, label = TRUE)




Useful .dt Attributes

# More dt attributes
print("Quarter:", df["hire_date"].dt.quarter.values)
print("Day of year:", df["hire_date"].dt.dayofyear.values)
print("Week number:", df["hire_date"].dt.isocalendar().week.values)
print("Is month end:", df["hire_date"].dt.is_month_end.values)
crumbs: Working with Dates and Time Series

title: Working with Dates and Time Series
text:
Date Arithmetic

Calculating Durations

# Calculate tenure in days
today = pd.Timestamp("2024-06-01")
df["tenure_days"] = (today - df["hire_date"]).dt.days

# Convert to years (approximate)
df["tenure_years"] = df["tenure_days"] / 365.25

df[["last_name", "hire_date", "tenure_days", "tenure_years"]]







TipIn R





# lubridate
today <- as.Date("2024-06-01")
df$tenure_days <- as.numeric(today - df$hire_date)
df$tenure_years <- interval(df$hire_date, today) / years(1)





Adding/Subtracting Time

# Add time using timedelta or DateOffset
df["one_year_later"] = df["hire_date"] + pd.DateOffset(years=1)
df["six_months_later"] = df["hire_date"] + pd.DateOffset(months=6)
df["30_days_later"] = df["hire_date"] + pd.Timedelta(days=30)

df[["last_name", "hire_date", "one_year_later", "six_months_later"]]



Calculating Time Between Snapshots

# Create multi-snapshot data
snapshots = pd.DataFrame({
    "employee_id": ["E001", "E001", "E001"],
    "snapshot": pd.to_datetime(["2023-01-01", "2023-06-01", "2024-01-01"]),
    "salary": [85000, 87000, 90000]
})

# Calculate days between snapshots
snapshots["prev_snapshot"] = snapshots.groupby("employee_id")["snapshot"].shift(1)
snapshots["days_between"] = (snapshots["snapshot"] - snapshots["prev_snapshot"]).dt.days
snapshots
crumbs: Working with Dates and Time Series

title: Working with Dates and Time Series
text:
Period Objects
For data like “2024-01” that represents a month (not a specific day), use Period:

# Create period from string
period = pd.Period("2024-01", freq="M")
print(f"Period: {period}")
print(f"Start: {period.start_time}")
print(f"End: {period.end_time}")


# Convert column to period
df["snapshot_period"] = df["snapshot_date"].apply(lambda x: pd.Period(x, freq="M"))
df["snapshot_period"]


Period Arithmetic

# Add months to period
period = pd.Period("2024-01", freq="M")
print(f"Current: {period}")
print(f"Next month: {period + 1}")
print(f"6 months later: {period + 6}")
print(f"Previous quarter: {period.asfreq('Q') - 1}")
crumbs: Working with Dates and Time Series

title: Working with Dates and Time Series
text:
Date Ranges
Generate sequences of dates:

# Daily range
daily = pd.date_range(start="2024-01-01", end="2024-01-10", freq="D")
print("Daily:", daily.tolist())


# Monthly range (first of month)
monthly = pd.date_range(start="2024-01", periods=6, freq="MS")
print("Monthly starts:", monthly.tolist())


# Business days
business = pd.date_range(start="2024-01-01", periods=5, freq="B")
print("Business days:", business.tolist())







TipIn R





# Base R
seq(as.Date("2024-01-01"), as.Date("2024-01-10"), by = "day")

# lubridate
seq(ymd("2024-01-01"), ymd("2024-01-10"), by = "days")
crumbs: Working with Dates and Time Series

title: Working with Dates and Time Series
text:
Rolling Calculations
Calculate statistics over moving windows:

# Time series of salary snapshots
salary_history = pd.DataFrame({
    "date": pd.date_range("2021-01", periods=8, freq="6MS"),
    "median_salary": [75000, 76500, 78000, 79200, 80500, 82000, 84000, 86000]
})

# Rolling mean (3-period)
salary_history["rolling_mean_3"] = salary_history["median_salary"].rolling(window=3).mean()

# Rolling percent change
salary_history["pct_change"] = salary_history["median_salary"].pct_change() * 100

salary_history







TipIn R





# dplyr/zoo
library(zoo)
df %>%
  mutate(
    rolling_mean = rollmean(salary, k = 3, fill = NA, align = "right"),
    pct_change = (salary - lag(salary)) / lag(salary) * 100
  )
crumbs: Working with Dates and Time Series

title: Working with Dates and Time Series
text:
Filtering by Date

# Sample data with dates
data = pd.DataFrame({
    "date": pd.date_range("2021-01-01", periods=20, freq="3MS"),
    "value": range(20)
})

# Filter to 2023 and later
recent = data[data["date"] >= "2023-01-01"]
print("2023 and later:")
print(recent)


# Filter to specific year
year_2022 = data[data["date"].dt.year == 2022]
print("\n2022 only:")
print(year_2022)


# Filter to date range
range_filter = data[(data["date"] >= "2022-06-01") & (data["date"] < "2023-06-01")]
print("\nJun 2022 to Jun 2023:")
print(range_filter)
crumbs: Working with Dates and Time Series

title: Working with Dates and Time Series
text:
Practical Example: Salary Tenure Analysis

# Comprehensive date analysis
employees = pd.DataFrame({
    "employee_id": ["E001", "E002", "E003", "E004"],
    "last_name": ["SMITH", "JOHNSON", "WILLIAMS", "BROWN"],
    "hire_date": pd.to_datetime(["2010-08-15", "2005-01-10", "2020-08-20", "2015-06-01"]),
    "salary": [125000, 185000, 95000, 175000]
})

# Current date for calculations
analysis_date = pd.Timestamp("2024-06-01")

# Calculate tenure metrics
employees["tenure_years"] = ((analysis_date - employees["hire_date"]).dt.days / 365.25).round(1)
employees["hire_decade"] = (employees["hire_date"].dt.year // 10) * 10
employees["anniversary_this_year"] = employees["hire_date"].apply(
    lambda x: x.replace(year=2024) if x.month <= 6 else x.replace(year=2025)
)

# Salary per year of tenure
employees["salary_per_tenure_year"] = (employees["salary"] / employees["tenure_years"]).round(0)

employees
crumbs: Working with Dates and Time Series

title: Working with Dates and Time Series
text:
Quick Reference



Task
pandas
lubridate




Parse string
pd.to_datetime(s)
ymd(s), mdy(s)


Extract year
dt.year
year()


Extract month
dt.month
month()


Extract day
dt.day
day()


Day of week
dt.day_name()
wday(label=TRUE)


Add days
+ pd.Timedelta(days=n)
+ days(n)


Add months
+ pd.DateOffset(months=n)
+ months(n)


Difference in days
(d1 - d2).dt.days
as.numeric(d1 - d2)


Date range
pd.date_range()

Rolling mean

crumbs: Working with Dates and Time Series

title: Working with Dates and Time Series
text:
Exercise

Exercise 7: Analyzing Salary Trends Over Time

Create a DataFrame with 5 employees and their hire dates (various years)
Calculate each employee’s tenure in years as of today
Group employees by the decade they were hired (2000s, 2010s, 2020s)
Create a monthly date range from Jan 2023 to Dec 2024
For a sample salary time series, calculate the rolling 3-month average
Find employees who have their work anniversary this month







NoteSolution






import pandas as pd
from datetime import date

# 1. Create employee data with hire dates
employees = pd.DataFrame({
    "employee_id": ["E001", "E002", "E003", "E004", "E005"],
    "last_name": ["SMITH", "JOHNSON", "WILLIAMS", "BROWN", "JONES"],
    "hire_date": pd.to_datetime([
        "2005-03-15", "2012-07-20", "2019-11-01", "2008-06-10", "2021-02-28"
    ]),
    "salary": [125000, 95000, 78000, 110000, 72000]
})

print("1. Employee data:")
print(employees)

# 2. Calculate tenure in years
today = pd.Timestamp.today()
employees["tenure_years"] = ((today - employees["hire_date"]).dt.days / 365.25).round(1)
print("\n2. Tenure in years:")
print(employees[["last_name", "hire_date", "tenure_years"]])

# 3. Group by hire decade
employees["hire_decade"] = (employees["hire_date"].dt.year // 10) * 10
decade_summary = employees.groupby("hire_decade").agg({
    "employee_id": "count",
    "salary": "mean"
}).rename(columns={"employee_id": "count", "salary": "avg_salary"})
print("\n3. By decade hired:")
print(decade_summary)

# 4. Monthly date range
monthly_range = pd.date_range(start="2023-01", end="2024-12", freq="MS")
print("\n4. Monthly date range (first 6):")
print(monthly_range[:6].tolist())

# 5. Rolling average for salary time series
salary_trend = pd.DataFrame({
    "date": pd.date_range("2023-01", periods=12, freq="MS"),
    "median_salary": [75000, 75500, 76000, 76200, 76800, 77500,
                      78000, 78500, 79000, 79500, 80000, 80500]
})
salary_trend["rolling_3mo"] = salary_trend["median_salary"].rolling(3).mean()
print("\n5. Rolling 3-month average:")
print(salary_trend)

# 6. Employees with anniversary this month
current_month = today.month
employees["anniversary_month"] = employees["hire_date"].dt.month
anniversary_this_month = employees[employees["anniversary_month"] == current_month]
print(f"\n6. Employees with anniversary in month {current_month}:")
print(anniversary_this_month[["last_name", "hire_date"]])
crumbs: Working with Dates and Time Series

title: Working with Dates and Time Series
text:
Summary
You’ve learned to work with dates in pandas:

pd.to_datetime(): Parse strings to datetime
.dt accessor: Extract year, month, day, and other components
Date arithmetic: Calculate durations, add/subtract time
pd.Period: Represent time periods (months, quarters)
pd.date_range(): Generate date sequences
Rolling calculations: Moving averages and statistics

Key differences from R: - pandas uses .dt accessor instead of lubridate functions - pd.DateOffset for month/year arithmetic vs lubridate’s months()/years() - Period objects for representing time periods (no direct R equivalent)

Continue to Lesson 8: String Operations to learn text manipulation and cleaning.
