title: Compensation Analysis and Distributions
text:
After this lesson, you will be able to:

Calculate salary percentiles and distributions by group
Perform peer comparison analysis (how does an employee compare to similar roles?)
Identify salary outliers and anomalies
Understand compensation equity analysis basics
Create meaningful salary comparison visualizations

title: Compensation Analysis and Distributions
text:
Introduction

Key Financial Concept: Peer Comparison
Compensation analysis often requires comparing an individual to their peer group. Key questions include:

Is this salary competitive for this job classification?
Where does this employee fall in the distribution?
Are there unexplained disparities between similar roles?

Peer groups can be defined by job code, division, tenure, or combinations of factors.


Equal Pay Act and Title IX Compliance
Compensation analysis in higher education operates within a legal framework that every analyst should understand:

Equal Pay Act (1963): Requires equal pay for equal work regardless of sex. The four defensible factors for pay differences are seniority, merit, quantity/quality of production, and “any factor other than sex.”
Title VII of the Civil Rights Act (1964): Prohibits employment discrimination based on race, color, religion, sex, or national origin — including compensation decisions.
Title IX (1972): While known for athletics, Title IX also covers employment in educational institutions. Sex-based salary disparities can trigger Title IX investigations.
Executive Order 11246: Federal contractors (which includes most research universities) must comply with OFCCP regulations, including affirmative action in compensation.

When performing peer group comparisons, you are doing the same type of analysis that regulators use to detect disparate impact. If women in a job group earn systematically less than men after controlling for legitimate factors (rank, years, department), that’s a potential compliance issue — not just an interesting finding.
Practically, this means your choice of comparison variables matters legally as well as analytically. Including a variable like “negotiated starting salary” may mask discrimination if the negotiation process itself was biased. These are the kinds of nuances that distinguish routine reporting from actionable equity analysis.


import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Create realistic salary data with variation
np.random.seed(42)

n_employees = 50
job_codes = ["FA001", "FA002", "AS001", "AS002", "CL001"]
divisions = ["Letters & Science", "Medicine", "Engineering", "Business"]

df = pd.DataFrame({
    "employee_id": [f"E{i:03d}" for i in range(n_employees)],
    "last_name": [f"Employee{i}" for i in range(n_employees)],
    "jobcode": np.random.choice(job_codes, n_employees),
    "division": np.random.choice(divisions, n_employees),
    "years_employed": np.random.randint(1, 25, n_employees),
})

# Generate salaries based on job code with some variation
salary_base = {"FA001": 85000, "FA002": 110000, "AS001": 65000, "AS002": 72000, "CL001": 55000}
df["salary"] = df.apply(
    lambda row: salary_base[row["jobcode"]] * (1 + row["years_employed"] * 0.02) +
                np.random.normal(0, 5000),
    axis=1
).round(0)

df.head(10)

title: Compensation Analysis and Distributions
text:
Calculating Percentiles

Basic Percentile Calculation

# Overall salary percentiles
percentiles = df["salary"].quantile([0.10, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 0.90])
print("Salary Percentiles:")
for p, val in percentiles.items():
    print(f"  {int(p*100)}th percentile: ${val:,.0f}")



Percentile Rank for Each Employee

# Calculate each employee's percentile rank
df["salary_percentile"] = df["salary"].rank(pct=True).round(2)
df["salary_quartile"] = pd.qcut(df["salary"], q=4, labels=["Q1", "Q2", "Q3", "Q4"])

df[["last_name", "jobcode", "salary", "salary_percentile", "salary_quartile"]].head(10)







TipIn R





# dplyr approach
df %>%
  mutate(
    salary_percentile = percent_rank(salary),
    salary_quartile = ntile(salary, 4)
  )

title: Compensation Analysis and Distributions
text:
Peer Group Analysis
The most useful comparison is against peers in the same job classification:

# Calculate percentile within job code (peer group)
df["peer_percentile"] = df.groupby("jobcode")["salary"].transform(
    lambda x: x.rank(pct=True)
).round(2)

# Calculate difference from peer median
df["peer_median"] = df.groupby("jobcode")["salary"].transform("median")
df["vs_peer_median"] = df["salary"] - df["peer_median"]
df["vs_peer_median_pct"] = (df["vs_peer_median"] / df["peer_median"] * 100).round(1)

df[["last_name", "jobcode", "salary", "peer_percentile", "peer_median", "vs_peer_median_pct"]].head(10)


Peer Group Summary

# Summary statistics by job code
peer_summary = df.groupby("jobcode").agg(
    count=("salary", "count"),
    min_salary=("salary", "min"),
    median_salary=("salary", "median"),
    max_salary=("salary", "max"),
    std_salary=("salary", "std")
).round(0)

peer_summary

title: Compensation Analysis and Distributions
text:
Identifying Outliers

Using IQR Method

def identify_outliers_iqr(series, multiplier=1.5):
    """Identify outliers using the IQR method."""
    q1 = series.quantile(0.25)
    q3 = series.quantile(0.75)
    iqr = q3 - q1
    lower_bound = q1 - multiplier * iqr
    upper_bound = q3 + multiplier * iqr
    return (series < lower_bound) | (series > upper_bound)

# Identify overall outliers
df["is_outlier"] = identify_outliers_iqr(df["salary"])

print(f"Number of outliers: {df['is_outlier'].sum()}")
print("\nOutliers:")
print(df[df["is_outlier"]][["last_name", "jobcode", "salary"]])



Outliers Within Peer Groups

# Identify outliers within each job code
df["is_peer_outlier"] = df.groupby("jobcode")["salary"].transform(
    lambda x: identify_outliers_iqr(x)
)

print("Peer group outliers:")
outliers = df[df["is_peer_outlier"]][["last_name", "jobcode", "salary", "peer_percentile"]]
print(outliers)

title: Compensation Analysis and Distributions
text:
Compensation Equity Analysis
Equity analysis looks for unexplained salary differences between groups:

# Compare median salaries by division within same job code
equity = df.groupby(["jobcode", "division"]).agg(
    count=("salary", "count"),
    median_salary=("salary", "median")
).reset_index()

# Pivot for easier comparison
equity_pivot = equity.pivot(index="jobcode", columns="division", values="median_salary")
equity_pivot


Salary Gap Analysis

# Calculate the range (max - min) of median salaries across divisions
# A large range suggests potential equity issues
equity_range = equity.groupby("jobcode")["median_salary"].agg(["min", "max"])
equity_range["gap"] = equity_range["max"] - equity_range["min"]
equity_range["gap_pct"] = (equity_range["gap"] / equity_range["min"] * 100).round(1)

equity_range

title: Compensation Analysis and Distributions
text:
Building a Peer Comparison Report
Let’s create a comprehensive report for a single employee:

def peer_comparison_report(df, employee_id):
    """Generate a peer comparison report for an employee."""
    emp = df[df["employee_id"] == employee_id].iloc[0]

    # Get peer group
    peers = df[df["jobcode"] == emp["jobcode"]]

    report = {
        "employee": emp["last_name"],
        "jobcode": emp["jobcode"],
        "salary": emp["salary"],
        "peer_count": len(peers),
        "peer_min": peers["salary"].min(),
        "peer_median": peers["salary"].median(),
        "peer_max": peers["salary"].max(),
        "percentile_rank": emp["peer_percentile"],
        "vs_median": emp["salary"] - peers["salary"].median(),
        "vs_median_pct": (emp["salary"] / peers["salary"].median() - 1) * 100
    }

    return report

# Generate report for first employee
report = peer_comparison_report(df, "E000")

print("Peer Comparison Report")
print("=" * 40)
for key, value in report.items():
    if isinstance(value, float):
        print(f"{key:20}: {value:,.1f}")
    else:
        print(f"{key:20}: {value}")

title: Compensation Analysis and Distributions
text:
Salary Distribution Analysis

# Distribution statistics by job code
distribution = df.groupby("jobcode")["salary"].describe()
distribution


Skewness Analysis

# Check for skewed distributions
skewness = df.groupby("jobcode")["salary"].agg(
    lambda x: x.skew()
).reset_index()
skewness.columns = ["jobcode", "skewness"]

# Interpretation: positive skew = long right tail (few very high earners)
skewness["interpretation"] = skewness["skewness"].apply(
    lambda x: "Right-skewed (high earners)" if x > 0.5
              else "Left-skewed (low earners)" if x < -0.5
              else "Approximately normal"
)
skewness

title: Compensation Analysis and Distributions
text:
Exercise

Exercise 10: Building a Peer Comparison Report

Create a salary DataFrame with at least 30 employees across 4 job codes
Calculate each employee’s percentile rank within their job code
Identify the top 10% earners in each job code
Find employees who are below the 25th percentile of their peer group
Calculate the salary gap between divisions for each job code
Create a peer comparison report function that returns key metrics







NoteSolution






import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# 1. Create salary data
np.random.seed(123)
n = 40
job_codes = ["FA001", "FA002", "AS001", "AS002"]
divisions = ["L&S", "Medicine", "Engineering"]

df = pd.DataFrame({
    "employee_id": [f"E{i:03d}" for i in range(n)],
    "last_name": [f"Emp{i}" for i in range(n)],
    "jobcode": np.random.choice(job_codes, n),
    "division": np.random.choice(divisions, n),
})

base = {"FA001": 85000, "FA002": 105000, "AS001": 62000, "AS002": 70000}
df["salary"] = df["jobcode"].map(base) + np.random.normal(0, 8000, n)
df["salary"] = df["salary"].round(0)

print("1. Sample data (first 10 rows):")
print(df.head(10))

# 2. Percentile rank within job code
df["peer_percentile"] = df.groupby("jobcode")["salary"].transform(
    lambda x: x.rank(pct=True)
).round(2)

print("\n2. With peer percentiles:")
print(df[["last_name", "jobcode", "salary", "peer_percentile"]].head(10))

# 3. Top 10% earners per job code
top_10_pct = df[df["peer_percentile"] >= 0.90]
print("\n3. Top 10% earners by job code:")
print(top_10_pct[["last_name", "jobcode", "salary", "peer_percentile"]])

# 4. Below 25th percentile
bottom_25_pct = df[df["peer_percentile"] <= 0.25]
print("\n4. Below 25th percentile:")
print(bottom_25_pct[["last_name", "jobcode", "salary", "peer_percentile"]])

# 5. Salary gap between divisions
gap_analysis = df.groupby(["jobcode", "division"])["salary"].median().reset_index()
gap_pivot = gap_analysis.pivot(index="jobcode", columns="division", values="salary")
gap_pivot["max_gap"] = gap_pivot.max(axis=1) - gap_pivot.min(axis=1)
print("\n5. Division salary gaps:")
print(gap_pivot)

# 6. Peer comparison function
def peer_report(data, emp_id):
    emp = data[data["employee_id"] == emp_id].iloc[0]
    peers = data[data["jobcode"] == emp["jobcode"]]

    return {
        "Employee": emp["last_name"],
        "Job Code": emp["jobcode"],
        "Salary": f"${emp['salary']:,.0f}",
        "Peer Count": len(peers),
        "Peer Median": f"${peers['salary'].median():,.0f}",
        "Percentile": f"{emp['peer_percentile']:.0%}",
        "Position": "Above median" if emp["salary"] > peers["salary"].median() else "Below median"
    }

print("\n6. Sample peer comparison report:")
report = peer_report(df, "E005")
for k, v in report.items():
    print(f"  {k}: {v}")

Summary
You’ve learned key compensation analysis techniques:

Percentile ranks: Where an employee falls in the distribution
Peer comparisons: Comparing to others in the same job classification
Outlier detection: Identifying unusual salaries using IQR method
Equity analysis: Looking for unexplained gaps between groups
Distribution analysis: Understanding salary spread and skewness

Key insights: - Always compare salaries within appropriate peer groups - Use percentiles rather than raw values for fair comparisons - Look for systematic patterns that might indicate equity issues

title: Tracking Individuals Over Time
text:
After this lesson, you will be able to:

Create stable employee identifiers across data snapshots
Handle name format changes between systems (UFAS → Workday)
Detect employee transfers between departments
Distinguish real transfers from organizational restructuring
Calculate individual salary growth trajectories

title: Tracking Individuals Over Time
text:
Introduction

Key Data Challenge: Identity Resolution
When tracking individuals across time, you need a stable identifier. The UW salary data doesn’t have a persistent employee ID, so we create one by hashing:
Last name + First name + Hire date → Unique ID
This handles name case differences (UFAS: “SMITH” vs Workday: “Smith”) and is stable across system transitions.


Longitudinal Equity Monitoring
Tracking individuals over time isn’t just a data management exercise — it’s the foundation of equity monitoring. Cross-sectional snapshots can show that men and women in the same job code earn similar salaries today, but longitudinal tracking may reveal very different stories:

Salary growth disparities: Do women receive smaller annual raises than men in the same department, even when starting at the same salary?
Promotion velocity: How long does it take to move from Assistant to Associate to Full Professor, broken down by gender or race? Longer time-in-rank means lower lifetime earnings even if salaries at each rank are equitable.
Retention patterns: Are underrepresented faculty leaving at higher rates? If so, the institution may look equitable in snapshots while systematically losing diverse talent.
Cohort effects: Employees hired during budget freezes may be permanently disadvantaged if subsequent raises are percentage-based (3% of a low starting salary compounds differently than 3% of a market-rate start).

The OFCCP (Office of Federal Contract Compliance Programs) uses exactly this kind of longitudinal analysis when auditing federal contractors. Their methodology examines cohort progression over time, not just current-year snapshots. Building robust individual tracking into your data pipeline prepares you to answer these questions proactively rather than reactively during an audit.


import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import hashlib

# Create sample multi-snapshot data
data = {
    "snapshot": ["2023-01", "2023-01", "2023-06", "2023-06", "2024-01", "2024-01"],
    "last_name": ["SMITH", "JOHNSON", "SMITH", "JOHNSON", "Smith", "Johnson"],  # Case change
    "first_name": ["JOHN", "MARY", "JOHN", "MARY", "John", "Mary"],
    "hire_date": ["2015-08-01", "2018-03-15", "2015-08-01", "2018-03-15",
                  "2015-08-01", "2018-03-15"],
    "division": ["L&S", "Medicine", "L&S", "Medicine", "Engineering", "Medicine"],  # SMITH transferred
    "salary": [85000, 95000, 87000, 98000, 92000, 101000]
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)
df["snapshot"] = pd.to_datetime(df["snapshot"])
df["hire_date"] = pd.to_datetime(df["hire_date"])
df

title: Tracking Individuals Over Time
text:
Creating Stable Employee IDs

def generate_employee_id(row):
    """Generate stable ID from name + hire date."""
    # Normalize to handle case differences
    last = str(row["last_name"]).upper().strip()
    first = str(row["first_name"]).upper().strip()
    hire = str(row["hire_date"])[:10]  # Just the date part

    # Create hash
    key = f"{last}|{first}|{hire}"
    return hashlib.md5(key.encode()).hexdigest()[:12]

df["employee_id"] = df.apply(generate_employee_id, axis=1)
df[["last_name", "first_name", "hire_date", "employee_id"]]







TipIn R





library(digest)
df %>%
  mutate(
    key = paste(toupper(last_name), toupper(first_name), hire_date, sep = "|"),
    employee_id = substr(sapply(key, digest, algo = "md5"), 1, 12)
  )

title: Tracking Individuals Over Time
text:
Tracking Department Changes (Transfers)

# Sort by employee and time
df = df.sort_values(["employee_id", "snapshot"])

# Get previous division for each employee
df["prev_division"] = df.groupby("employee_id")["division"].shift(1)

# Flag transfers
df["transferred"] = (df["division"] != df["prev_division"]) & df["prev_division"].notna()

df[["last_name", "snapshot", "division", "prev_division", "transferred"]]


Analyzing Transfers

# Find all transfers
transfers = df[df["transferred"]]
print("Transfers detected:")
print(transfers[["last_name", "snapshot", "prev_division", "division"]])

title: Tracking Individuals Over Time
text:
Calculating Salary Trajectories

# Calculate salary change from previous snapshot
df["prev_salary"] = df.groupby("employee_id")["salary"].shift(1)
df["salary_change"] = df["salary"] - df["prev_salary"]
df["salary_change_pct"] = (df["salary_change"] / df["prev_salary"] * 100).round(1)

df[["last_name", "snapshot", "salary", "prev_salary", "salary_change_pct"]]


Individual Salary Trajectory

# Complete trajectory for one employee
smith = df[df["last_name"].str.upper() == "SMITH"]
print("SMITH's salary trajectory:")
print(smith[["snapshot", "division", "salary", "salary_change_pct"]])

# Total growth
first = smith.iloc[0]["salary"]
last = smith.iloc[-1]["salary"]
total_growth = (last / first - 1) * 100
print(f"\nTotal salary growth: {total_growth:.1f}%")

title: Tracking Individuals Over Time
text:
Distinguishing Transfers from Reorganizations
When many employees “transfer” between two departments with similar names, it’s likely a reorganization, not real transfers.

from difflib import SequenceMatcher

def name_similarity(name1, name2):
    """Calculate similarity between two department names."""
    return SequenceMatcher(None, name1.lower(), name2.lower()).ratio()

# Example
print(name_similarity("College of Letters & Science", "College of Letters and Science"))
print(name_similarity("College of Letters & Science", "School of Medicine"))


def is_likely_reorganization(old_div, new_div, similarity_threshold=0.8):
    """Check if a 'transfer' is likely just a name change."""
    similarity = name_similarity(old_div, new_div)
    return similarity >= similarity_threshold

# Test
print(is_likely_reorganization("Coll of Ltrs & Sci", "College of Letters and Science"))
print(is_likely_reorganization("L&S", "Medicine"))

title: Tracking Individuals Over Time
text:
Cohort Analysis
Analyze employees hired in the same period:

# Create larger sample
np.random.seed(42)
n = 50
cohort_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "employee_id": [f"E{i:03d}" for i in range(n)],
    "hire_year": np.random.choice([2018, 2019, 2020, 2021, 2022], n),
    "starting_salary": np.random.randint(50000, 100000, n),
    "current_salary": np.random.randint(55000, 120000, n)
})

cohort_df["salary_growth"] = (
    (cohort_df["current_salary"] / cohort_df["starting_salary"]) - 1
) * 100

# Analyze by hire year cohort
cohort_analysis = cohort_df.groupby("hire_year").agg(
    count=("employee_id", "count"),
    avg_starting=("starting_salary", "mean"),
    avg_current=("current_salary", "mean"),
    avg_growth_pct=("salary_growth", "mean")
).round(0)

cohort_analysis

title: Tracking Individuals Over Time
text:
Exercise

Exercise 12: Employee Journey Analysis

Create employee data with at least 5 employees across 3 time periods
Generate stable employee IDs using name + hire date hashing
Identify which employees transferred between divisions
Calculate each employee’s total salary growth from first to last period
Find the employee with the highest salary growth percentage







NoteSolution






import pandas as pd
import hashlib

# 1. Create employee data
data = []
employees = [
    ("SMITH", "JOHN", "2015-01-15"),
    ("JOHNSON", "MARY", "2017-06-01"),
    ("WILLIAMS", "JAMES", "2019-09-20"),
    ("BROWN", "SARAH", "2016-03-10"),
    ("DAVIS", "MICHAEL", "2020-01-05")
]

snapshots = ["2022-01", "2023-01", "2024-01"]
divisions = ["L&S", "Medicine", "Engineering"]

for last, first, hire in employees:
    current_div = np.random.choice(divisions)
    base_salary = np.random.randint(60000, 100000)
    for i, snap in enumerate(snapshots):
        # Maybe transfer in last period
        if i == 2 and last == "SMITH":
            current_div = "Engineering"  # Transfer
        data.append({
            "snapshot": snap,
            "last_name": last,
            "first_name": first,
            "hire_date": hire,
            "division": current_div,
            "salary": base_salary + i * np.random.randint(2000, 5000)
        })

df = pd.DataFrame(data)
df["snapshot"] = pd.to_datetime(df["snapshot"])
print("1. Sample data:")
print(df.head(10))

# 2. Generate employee IDs
def make_id(row):
    key = f"{row['last_name'].upper()}|{row['first_name'].upper()}|{row['hire_date']}"
    return hashlib.md5(key.encode()).hexdigest()[:8]

df["emp_id"] = df.apply(make_id, axis=1)
print("\n2. With employee IDs:")
print(df[["last_name", "hire_date", "emp_id"]].drop_duplicates())

# 3. Identify transfers
df = df.sort_values(["emp_id", "snapshot"])
df["prev_div"] = df.groupby("emp_id")["division"].shift(1)
df["transferred"] = (df["division"] != df["prev_div"]) & df["prev_div"].notna()

transfers = df[df["transferred"]]
print("\n3. Transfers:")
print(transfers[["last_name", "snapshot", "prev_div", "division"]])

# 4 & 5. Salary growth
first_snap = df.groupby("emp_id").first()[["last_name", "salary"]].rename(columns={"salary": "start_salary"})
last_snap = df.groupby("emp_id").last()[["salary"]].rename(columns={"salary": "end_salary"})

growth = first_snap.join(last_snap)
growth["growth_pct"] = ((growth["end_salary"] / growth["start_salary"]) - 1) * 100

print("\n4. Salary growth:")
print(growth.round(1))

print(f"\n5. Highest growth: {growth['growth_pct'].idxmax()} - {growth.loc[growth['growth_pct'].idxmax(), 'last_name']}")

title: Interactive Dashboards in Quarto
text:
After this lesson, you will be able to:

Leverage Plotly’s built-in interactivity (hover, zoom, selection)
Create Quarto dashboard layouts
Use tabsets and columns for organized presentation
Add dropdown filters with Observable JS (optional)
Publish interactive reports to GitHub Pages

title: Interactive Dashboards in Quarto
text:
Introduction
Quarto combines the best of literate programming (like R Markdown) with modern web interactivity. When you embed Plotly figures in a Quarto document, they retain full interactivity in the rendered HTML.

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots

# Create sample salary data
np.random.seed(42)
dates = pd.date_range("2021-01", periods=8, freq="6MS")
divisions = ["L&S", "Medicine", "Engineering", "Business"]

records = []
for div in divisions:
    base = {"L&S": 85000, "Medicine": 105000, "Engineering": 95000, "Business": 92000}[div]
    for i, date in enumerate(dates):
        records.append({
            "date": date,
            "division": div,
            "median_salary": base * (1 + i * 0.025) + np.random.normal(0, 2000),
            "headcount": np.random.randint(150, 300),
            "total_fte": np.random.randint(120, 280)
        })

df = pd.DataFrame(records)
df["median_salary"] = df["median_salary"].round(0)
df.head()







TipIn R





# R Markdown / Quarto with plotly
library(plotly)
p <- ggplot(df, aes(x = date, y = salary)) + geom_line()
ggplotly(p)  # Makes the ggplot interactive

title: Interactive Dashboards in Quarto
text:
Plotly Interactivity Features
Plotly charts come with built-in interactivity that requires no additional code.

Hover Information

fig = px.line(
    df,
    x="date",
    y="median_salary",
    color="division",
    title="Median Salary Trends by Division",
    labels={"median_salary": "Median Salary ($)", "date": "Date", "division": "Division"}
)

# Customize hover template
fig.update_traces(
    hovertemplate="<b>%{fullData.name}</b><br>" +
                  "Date: %{x|%b %Y}<br>" +
                  "Salary: $%{y:,.0f}<extra></extra>"
)

fig.update_layout(hovermode="x unified")
fig.show()



Zoom and Pan
Users can: - Box zoom: Click and drag to select a region - Pan: Hold shift and drag - Reset: Double-click to reset view - Autoscale: Click the autoscale button in the toolbar

# Enable range slider for time series
fig = px.line(df, x="date", y="median_salary", color="division")

fig.update_layout(
    xaxis=dict(
        rangeslider=dict(visible=True),
        type="date"
    ),
    title="Interactive Time Series with Range Slider"
)

fig.show()



Click Events and Selection

# Scatter plot with selection capabilities
scatter_data = df.copy()
scatter_data["size"] = scatter_data["headcount"] / 10

fig = px.scatter(
    scatter_data,
    x="total_fte",
    y="median_salary",
    color="division",
    size="size",
    hover_data=["date", "headcount"],
    title="FTE vs Median Salary (click to select points)"
)

fig.update_layout(
    dragmode="select"  # Enable box selection
)

fig.show()

Quarto Dashboard Layouts

fig = make_subplots(rows=2, cols=2, subplot_titles=divisions)

title: Interactive Dashboards in Quarto
text:
Publishing to GitHub Pages

Step 1: Initialize Git Repository
git init
git add .
git commit -m "Initial dashboard commit"


Step 2: Create GitHub Repository
gh repo create my-salary-dashboard --public --source=. --push


Step 3: Publish with Quarto
quarto publish gh-pages
This command: 1. Renders your Quarto project 2. Creates/updates the gh-pages branch 3. Pushes to GitHub 4. Configures GitHub Pages automatically


Step 4: Access Your Dashboard
Your dashboard will be available at: https://yourusername.github.io/my-salary-dashboard/

Add a PyCharm run configuration for publishing: 1. Run → Edit Configurations → Add New → Shell Script 2. Script: quarto publish gh-pages --no-prompt 3. Now you can publish with one click!



Automating with GitHub Actions
Create .github/workflows/publish.yml:
name: Publish Dashboard

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  build-deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.11'

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pip install pandas numpy plotly

      - name: Setup Quarto
        uses: quarto-dev/quarto-actions/setup@v2

      - name: Publish
        uses: quarto-dev/quarto-actions/publish@v2
        with:
          target: gh-pages
        env:
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

title: Interactive Dashboards in Quarto
text:
Exercise

Exercise 14: Build a Salary Explorer Dashboard
Create a Quarto dashboard that includes:

A tabset with “Overview” and “By Division” tabs
Interactive line chart of salary trends with hover details
A summary table showing key metrics by division
Two-column layout comparing headcount vs FTE trends
At least one value box showing a key metric







NoteSolution





Create a file salary-explorer.qmd:
---
title: "UW Salary Explorer"
format:
  dashboard:
    orientation: rows
    theme: cosmo
---

```{python}
#| include: false
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go

# Generate sample data
np.random.seed(42)
dates = pd.date_range("2021-01", periods=8, freq="6MS")
divisions = ["L&S", "Medicine", "Engineering", "Business"]

records = []
for div in divisions:
    base = {"L&S": 85000, "Medicine": 105000, "Engineering": 95000, "Business": 92000}[div]
    for i, date in enumerate(dates):
        records.append({
            "date": date,
            "division": div,
            "median_salary": base * (1 + i * 0.025) + np.random.normal(0, 2000),
            "headcount": np.random.randint(150, 300),
            "total_fte": np.random.randint(120, 280)
        })

df = pd.DataFrame(records)
df["median_salary"] = df["median_salary"].round(0)
```

## Row {height=15%}

```{python}
#| content: valuebox
#| title: "Total Headcount (Latest)"
latest = df[df["date"] == df["date"].max()]
dict(value=f"{latest['headcount'].sum():,}", icon="people", color="primary")
```

```{python}
#| content: valuebox
#| title: "Average Median Salary"
dict(value=f"${latest['median_salary'].mean():,.0f}", icon="currency-dollar", color="success")
```

```{python}
#| content: valuebox
#| title: "Total FTE"
dict(value=f"{latest['total_fte'].sum():,}", icon="briefcase", color="info")
```

## Row {height=85%}

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Overview

```{python}
fig = px.line(
    df, x="date", y="median_salary", color="division",
    title="Median Salary Trends by Division",
    labels={"median_salary": "Median Salary ($)", "date": "Date"}
)
fig.update_traces(hovertemplate="%{y:$,.0f}")
fig.update_layout(
    legend=dict(orientation="h", y=-0.15),
    hovermode="x unified"
)
fig.show()
```

### By Division

```{python}
#| layout-ncol: 2

# Headcount trends
fig1 = px.line(df, x="date", y="headcount", color="division", title="Headcount Trends")
fig1.update_layout(showlegend=False)
fig1.show()

# FTE trends
fig2 = px.line(df, x="date", y="total_fte", color="division", title="FTE Trends")
fig2.update_layout(showlegend=True, legend=dict(orientation="h", y=-0.2))
fig2.show()
```

### Summary Table

```{python}
summary = df.groupby("division").agg(
    latest_salary=("median_salary", "last"),
    salary_change=("median_salary", lambda x: x.iloc[-1] - x.iloc[0]),
    avg_headcount=("headcount", "mean"),
    avg_fte=("total_fte", "mean")
).round(0)

summary["salary_change_pct"] = (summary["salary_change"] / (summary["latest_salary"] - summary["salary_change"]) * 100).round(1)

summary
```

title: Web Scraping Basics
text:
After this lesson, you will be able to:

Understand when web scraping is appropriate (vs. APIs)
Use BeautifulSoup to parse HTML documents
Navigate the DOM to extract specific elements
Handle nested structures and tables
Practice ethical scraping (robots.txt, rate limiting)

title: Web Scraping Basics
text:
Introduction
Web scraping extracts data from web pages by parsing their HTML. While APIs are preferred, scraping is sometimes necessary when:

No API exists
The API is too limited
You need historical snapshots of web content







WarningEthical Scraping



Before scraping any website:

Check for an API first (it’s almost always better)
Review the site’s robots.txt file (e.g., example.com/robots.txt)
Respect rate limits to avoid overwhelming servers
Check terms of service for scraping policies
Cache responses to minimize requests
Identify yourself with a User-Agent header




import pandas as pd
import requests
from bs4 import BeautifulSoup
import time
from pathlib import Path

title: Web Scraping Basics
Web Scraping Basics
text:
Navigating the DOM
The DOM (Document Object Model) is a tree structure. Navigate it using parent, child, and sibling relationships.

# Get a job element
job = soup.find("div", class_="job")

# Navigate to children
for child in job.children:
    if child.name:  # Skip text nodes
        print(f"Child: {child.name} - {child.text.strip()[:30]}")


# Navigate to parent
title = soup.find("h2", class_="title")
parent = title.parent
print(f"Title's parent: {parent.name}, id={parent.get('id')}")

# Navigate to siblings
code = soup.find("span", class_="code")
next_elem = code.find_next_sibling()
print(f"After code: {next_elem.text}")

title: Web Scraping Basics
text:
Extracting Structured Data
Let’s extract job data into a DataFrame:

def extract_job_data(soup):
    """Extract all job listings into a list of dictionaries."""
    jobs = []

    for job_div in soup.find_all("div", class_="job"):
        job = {
            "id": job_div.get("id"),
            "title": job_div.find("h2", class_="title").text.strip(),
            "code": job_div.find("span", class_="code").text.strip(),
            "salary_range": job_div.find("span", class_="salary-range").text.strip(),
            "description": job_div.find("p", class_="description").text.strip()
        }
        jobs.append(job)

    return jobs

jobs = extract_job_data(soup)
df = pd.DataFrame(jobs)
print(df)


Parsing Salary Ranges

import re

def parse_salary_range(salary_str):
    """Extract min and max salary from a range string."""
    # Remove $ and commas, find all numbers
    numbers = re.findall(r"[\d,]+", salary_str)
    numbers = [int(n.replace(",", "")) for n in numbers]

    if len(numbers) >= 2:
        return numbers[0], numbers[1]
    elif len(numbers) == 1:
        return numbers[0], numbers[0]
    return None, None

# Apply to our data
df["salary_min"], df["salary_max"] = zip(*df["salary_range"].apply(parse_salary_range))
print(df[["title", "salary_min", "salary_max"]])

title: Web Scraping Basics
Web Scraping Basics
text:
Fetching Web Pages
Now let’s fetch real web pages:

def fetch_page(url, headers=None, delay=1):
    """Fetch a web page with polite delay."""
    default_headers = {
        "User-Agent": "UW-Salary-Analysis/1.0 (Educational project; contact@example.com)"
    }
    headers = headers or default_headers

    time.sleep(delay)  # Be polite
    response = requests.get(url, headers=headers, timeout=30)
    response.raise_for_status()

    return BeautifulSoup(response.text, "html.parser")

# Example usage
# soup = fetch_page("https://example.com/jobs")


Checking robots.txt

from urllib.parse import urlparse

def check_robots_txt(url):
    """Check if scraping is allowed by robots.txt."""
    parsed = urlparse(url)
    robots_url = f"{parsed.scheme}://{parsed.netloc}/robots.txt"

    try:
        response = requests.get(robots_url, timeout=10)
        print(f"robots.txt for {parsed.netloc}:")
        print(response.text[:500])  # First 500 chars
    except requests.RequestException as e:
        print(f"Could not fetch robots.txt: {e}")

# check_robots_txt("https://www.wisconsin.edu")

title: Web Scraping Basics
Web Scraping Basics
text:
Pagination
Many sites split data across multiple pages:

def scrape_paginated(base_url, max_pages=10, delay=1):
    """Scrape multiple pages of results."""
    all_data = []

    for page in range(1, max_pages + 1):
        url = f"{base_url}?page={page}"
        print(f"Fetching page {page}...")

        try:
            soup = fetch_page(url, delay=delay)

            # Extract data from this page
            page_data = extract_job_data(soup)

            if not page_data:
                print("No more data, stopping.")
                break

            all_data.extend(page_data)

        except requests.RequestException as e:
            print(f"Error on page {page}: {e}")
            break

    return all_data

# Example usage
# all_jobs = scrape_paginated("https://example.com/jobs", max_pages=5)

title: Web Scraping Basics
text:
Caching Scraped Data
Cache pages locally to avoid re-fetching:

import hashlib

def fetch_with_cache(url, cache_dir="data/scrape_cache"):
    """Fetch page with local file caching."""
    cache_path = Path(cache_dir)
    cache_path.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

    # Create unique filename from URL
    url_hash = hashlib.md5(url.encode()).hexdigest()
    cache_file = cache_path / f"{url_hash}.html"

    # Check cache
    if cache_file.exists():
        print(f"Using cached: {url[:50]}...")
        return BeautifulSoup(cache_file.read_text(), "html.parser")

    # Fetch and cache
    print(f"Fetching: {url[:50]}...")
    response = requests.get(url, timeout=30)
    response.raise_for_status()

    cache_file.write_text(response.text)
    return BeautifulSoup(response.text, "html.parser")

title: Web Scraping Basics
Web Scraping Basics
Web Scraping Basics
text:
Summary
You’ve learned web scraping fundamentals:

BeautifulSoup: Parse HTML with find(), find_all(), and select()
CSS Selectors: Powerful element selection with .class, #id, parent child
DOM Navigation: Move through parent, child, and sibling elements
Table Extraction: Parse HTML tables into DataFrames
Error Handling: Deal with missing elements gracefully
Pagination: Scrape multiple pages systematically
Caching: Store pages locally to avoid re-fetching
Ethics: Check robots.txt, use delays, identify yourself

Key principles: - Always prefer APIs over scraping - Be respectful: delays, caching, proper User-Agent - Handle missing data gracefully - Test on cached data during development

title: Capstone: Complete Analysis Report
text:
Introduction
This capstone project brings together everything you’ve learned throughout the tutorial. You’ll create a complete salary equity analysis report that could be shared with stakeholders, demonstrating mastery of:

Data loading and cleaning (Lessons 3-5)
Joining and reshaping data (Lessons 5-6)
Time series and date handling (Lesson 7)
String operations (Lesson 8)
Inflation adjustment (Lesson 9)
Compensation analysis (Lesson 10)
Workforce analytics (Lesson 11)
Employee tracking (Lesson 12)
Visualization with Plotly (Lessons 13-14)
API integration (Lesson 15)
Web scraping (Lesson 16)
Production code patterns (Lesson 17)


Framing Analysis for Decision-Makers
The difference between a good analysis and an influential one is framing. University leaders don’t have time to interpret raw statistics — they need findings connected to decisions they can make.
Executive summary format that works:

Lead with the problem: “Faculty salary competitiveness has declined 12% relative to peers since 2018, contributing to a 15% increase in voluntary departures among mid-career faculty.”
Quantify the stakes: “At current attrition rates, replacement costs (search, startup packages, lost productivity) exceed $2.5M annually — more than the cost of targeted retention raises.”
Present options, not just findings: “Three scenarios: (A) 2% across-the-board + 1% merit pool ($4.2M), (B) targeted market adjustments for highest-risk positions ($2.8M), (C) status quo with projected 18% attrition.”
Recommend and justify: “We recommend Option B based on cost-effectiveness and retention modeling.”

Connecting to institutional strategy: Every university has a strategic plan with goals around research excellence, student success, diversity, and financial sustainability. Frame your findings in those terms: “Salary compression in STEM departments undermines our strategic goal of research competitiveness” is more actionable than “STEM compression ratios average 0.87.”

title: Capstone: Complete Analysis Report
text:
The Assignment
Create a Quarto document analyzing salary trends at UW-Madison. Your report should address these key questions:

Research Questions

How have salaries changed in real terms since 2021?

Compare nominal vs inflation-adjusted trends
Identify which employee categories gained or lost purchasing power

How do salaries vary across divisions?

Compare median salaries and salary ranges
Identify the highest and lowest paying divisions

What do the salary distributions look like?

Analyze by employee category (Faculty, Academic Staff, University Staff)
Identify outliers and explain possible reasons

What workforce changes have occurred?

Track headcount and FTE trends
Identify divisions that grew or shrank significantly

Are there equity concerns in compensation?

Examine salary differences by employee category within similar job groups
Look for evidence of salary compression among long-tenured employees
Consider whether any observed disparities align with protected class patterns (gender, race) if demographic data is available
Frame findings in terms of institutional risk and compliance obligations

title: Capstone: Complete Analysis Report
text:
Guidelines

Writing for Your Audience

Write for a reader who understands finance but not Python

Explain what the analysis does, not how the code works
Use visualizations to support every major claim
Define technical terms when first used

Keep narrative focused

Lead with the insight, then show the evidence
“Salaries grew 3% in nominal terms but fell 2% in real terms” is better than “The mean salary in 2024 was $X and in 2021 was $Y”




Code Presentation

Use code folding (code-fold: true) to hide code by default
Include code in collapsible sections for reproducibility
Add comments that explain why, not what
Use consistent styling throughout



Visualizations

Every chart needs a title and proper labels
Use colorblind-friendly palettes (Wong palette from Lesson 13)
Include hover information for interactivity
Don’t overload charts - one message per chart



Length and Scope

Total length: 2,000-3,000 words plus code/figures
Visualizations: 5-8 charts minimum
Focus depth over breadth: Better to deeply analyze 3 questions than superficially cover 10

title: Capstone: Complete Analysis Report
text:
Sample Code Patterns

Loading and Preparing Data

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Load salary data
df = pd.read_csv("data/salaries.csv")
df.columns = df.columns.str.lower().str.replace(" ", "_")
df["date"] = pd.to_datetime(df["date"])

# Load CPI data for inflation adjustment
cpi = pd.read_csv("data/cpi_data.csv")
base_cpi = cpi[cpi["date"] == "2021-11-01"]["cpi"].values[0]
cpi["cpi_index"] = base_cpi / cpi["cpi"]

# Merge and calculate real salary
df = df.merge(cpi[["date", "cpi_index"]], on="date")
df["real_salary"] = (df["salary"] * df["cpi_index"]).round(0)



Creating Summary Statistics

# Salary summary by division
summary = df.groupby("division").agg(
    count=("employee_id", "count"),
    median_salary=("salary", "median"),
    median_real=("real_salary", "median"),
    min_salary=("salary", "min"),
    max_salary=("salary", "max")
).round(0)

# Calculate salary range
summary["salary_range"] = summary["max_salary"] - summary["min_salary"]

summary.sort_values("median_salary", ascending=False)



Creating Effective Visualizations

# Colorblind-friendly palette
wong_palette = ["#E69F00", "#56B4E9", "#009E73", "#F0E442",
                "#0072B2", "#D55E00", "#CC79A7"]

# Trend comparison chart
fig = make_subplots(
    rows=1, cols=2,
    subplot_titles=("Nominal Salary", "Real Salary (2021$)")
)

for i, col in enumerate(["median_salary", "median_real"]):
    for j, div in enumerate(divisions):
        div_data = trend_data[trend_data["division"] == div]
        fig.add_trace(
            go.Scatter(
                x=div_data["date"],
                y=div_data[col],
                name=div,
                line_color=wong_palette[j],
                showlegend=(i == 0)
            ),
            row=1, col=i+1
        )

fig.update_layout(
    title="Nominal vs Real Salary Trends by Division",
    height=400,
    hovermode="x unified"
)
fig.update_yaxes(tickformat="$,.0f")
fig.show()
