title: Salary Compression Analysis
text:
After this lesson, you will be able to:

Define and calculate salary compression metrics
Compute compa-ratios (actual salary vs range midpoint)
Identify where new hires earn close to or above senior staff
Analyze compression across job families and divisions
Visualize salary range utilization

title: Salary Compression Analysis
text:
Introduction

What is Salary Compression?
Salary compression occurs when there is little difference in pay between employees regardless of experience, skills, or seniority. Common causes:

Market-driven compression: New hires command higher starting salaries due to market demand, closing the gap with long-tenured staff
Minimum salary increases: Across-the-board raises that disproportionately lift the bottom
Inversion: When new hires actually earn more than experienced employees in the same role

Compression erodes the reward for experience and loyalty, increasing turnover risk among senior staff.


Wisconsin Public-Sector Compensation Context
Salary compression at UW is not accidental — it’s a predictable consequence of how public-sector compensation works in Wisconsin:
Pay plan history: The state legislature sets the “pay plan” — the authorized percentage increase for state employees in each biennium. From 2011 to 2021, these increases were minimal (often 0-2%), while the private sector and peer institutions offered 3-5%. The result: new hires needed market-rate offers to accept positions, but incumbents received only the modest pay plan increase. Over a decade, this compresses salary ranges dramatically.
Available tools for addressing compression:

Across-the-board (ATB) raises: Equal percentage increases for all. These maintain the status quo — they don’t fix compression because 3% of $80K ($2,400) is less than 3% of $120K ($3,600), meaning dollar gaps persist and ratios stay compressed.
Merit raises: Differentiated based on performance. Can help if directed toward compressed positions, but politically difficult and requires robust evaluation systems.
Market/equity adjustments: Targeted increases to bring specific positions to competitive levels. The most effective tool for compression, but requires justification and data — exactly what this lesson teaches you to produce.
Title changes: Reclassifying positions to higher salary ranges. Common in academic staff and university staff, but not available for ranked faculty.
Retention offers: Reactive adjustments when an employee has an outside offer. Effective but expensive (disrupts internal equity) and only helps those with external opportunities.

When you calculate compa-ratios and identify inversion, you’re building the evidence base that HR and the Provost’s office need to justify targeted market adjustments in their budget requests.


import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots

# Generate salary data with compression characteristics
np.random.seed(42)

job_families = {
    "Assistant Professor": {"min": 75000, "mid": 95000, "max": 115000, "n": 200},
    "Associate Professor": {"min": 85000, "mid": 110000, "max": 135000, "n": 150},
    "Full Professor": {"min": 100000, "mid": 140000, "max": 180000, "n": 120},
    "Research Scientist": {"min": 55000, "mid": 72000, "max": 89000, "n": 180},
    "Academic Staff": {"min": 45000, "mid": 60000, "max": 75000, "n": 250},
    "IT Professional": {"min": 60000, "mid": 80000, "max": 100000, "n": 100},
}

records = []
for title, params in job_families.items():
    for i in range(params["n"]):
        years_service = np.random.exponential(8)
        years_service = min(years_service, 35)

        # Base salary with some compression:
        # New hires cluster near midpoint, senior staff don't go much higher
        base = params["min"] + (params["mid"] - params["min"]) * min(years_service / 5, 1.0)
        noise = np.random.normal(0, (params["max"] - params["min"]) * 0.08)
        salary = base + noise

        # Some market-driven new hires come in high
        if years_service < 2 and np.random.random() < 0.3:
            salary = params["mid"] + np.random.uniform(0, (params["max"] - params["mid"]) * 0.3)

        salary = np.clip(salary, params["min"] * 0.95, params["max"] * 1.05)

        records.append({
            "title": title,
            "salary": round(salary, 0),
            "years_service": round(years_service, 1),
            "range_min": params["min"],
            "range_mid": params["mid"],
            "range_max": params["max"]
        })

df = pd.DataFrame(records)
print(f"Employees: {len(df)}")
print(df.groupby("title")["salary"].describe()[["count", "mean", "min", "max"]].round(0))







TipIn R





# Compa-ratio in R
library(dplyr)
df <- df %>%
  mutate(
    compa_ratio = salary / range_mid,
    range_position = (salary - range_min) / (range_max - range_min)
  )

title: Salary Compression Analysis
text:
Compa-Ratios
The compa-ratio (comparative ratio) measures where an employee’s salary falls relative to the midpoint of their pay range.

def calculate_compa_ratio(salary, range_midpoint):
    """Calculate compa-ratio.

    Compa-ratio = Actual Salary / Range Midpoint

    Interpretation:
        < 0.80: Significantly below midpoint
        0.80-0.90: Below midpoint
        0.90-1.10: At midpoint (target zone)
        1.10-1.20: Above midpoint
        > 1.20: Significantly above midpoint
    """
    return salary / range_midpoint

df["compa_ratio"] = calculate_compa_ratio(df["salary"], df["range_mid"])

print("Compa-Ratio Summary by Title:")
print(df.groupby("title")["compa_ratio"].describe()[["mean", "std", "min", "max"]].round(3))


Compa-Ratio Distribution

fig = px.box(
    df,
    x="title",
    y="compa_ratio",
    title="Compa-Ratio Distribution by Job Title",
    labels={"compa_ratio": "Compa-Ratio", "title": "Job Title"},
    color="title"
)

# Add target zone
fig.add_hline(y=1.0, line_dash="solid", line_color="black", line_width=1)
fig.add_hline(y=0.9, line_dash="dot", line_color="gray",
              annotation_text="Target Zone", annotation_position="top left")
fig.add_hline(y=1.1, line_dash="dot", line_color="gray")

fig.update_layout(showlegend=False, xaxis_tickangle=-30)
fig.show()

title: Salary Compression Analysis
text:
Range Position (Range Penetration)
While compa-ratio measures distance from midpoint, range position measures where someone falls within the full range.

def range_position(salary, range_min, range_max):
    """Calculate range position (0-1 scale).

    0.0 = at minimum
    0.5 = at midpoint
    1.0 = at maximum
    """
    if range_max == range_min:
        return 0.5
    return (salary - range_min) / (range_max - range_min)

df["range_position"] = df.apply(
    lambda row: range_position(row["salary"], row["range_min"], row["range_max"]),
    axis=1
)

# Summary
print("Range Position Summary:")
print(df.groupby("title")["range_position"].describe()[["mean", "25%", "50%", "75%"]].round(3))


Range Position by Seniority

# Create seniority buckets
df["seniority"] = pd.cut(
    df["years_service"],
    bins=[0, 2, 5, 10, 20, 40],
    labels=["0-2 yrs", "2-5 yrs", "5-10 yrs", "10-20 yrs", "20+ yrs"]
)

fig = px.box(
    df,
    x="seniority",
    y="range_position",
    color="seniority",
    title="Range Position by Years of Service",
    labels={"range_position": "Range Position", "seniority": "Years of Service"}
)

fig.add_hline(y=0.5, line_dash="dash", line_color="black",
              annotation_text="Midpoint")

fig.update_layout(showlegend=False)
fig.show()

title: Salary Compression Analysis
text:
Detecting Compression

Compression Ratio
The compression ratio compares salaries of new hires to senior employees.

def compression_ratio(df, salary_col, seniority_col, junior_max=2, senior_min=10):
    """Calculate compression ratio.

    Compression Ratio = Median Junior Salary / Median Senior Salary

    Values close to 1.0 indicate severe compression.
    """
    junior = df[df[seniority_col] <= junior_max][salary_col].median()
    senior = df[df[seniority_col] >= senior_min][salary_col].median()

    return junior / senior if senior > 0 else None

# Calculate by job title
compression_data = []
for title in job_families:
    title_df = df[df["title"] == title]
    cr = compression_ratio(title_df, "salary", "years_service")

    junior_med = title_df[title_df["years_service"] <= 2]["salary"].median()
    senior_med = title_df[title_df["years_service"] >= 10]["salary"].median()

    compression_data.append({
        "title": title,
        "compression_ratio": cr,
        "junior_median": junior_med,
        "senior_median": senior_med,
        "gap": senior_med - junior_med
    })

comp_df = pd.DataFrame(compression_data).sort_values("compression_ratio", ascending=False)

print("Compression Analysis:")
print("=" * 70)
for _, row in comp_df.iterrows():
    status = "SEVERE" if row["compression_ratio"] > 0.95 else \
             "HIGH" if row["compression_ratio"] > 0.90 else \
             "MODERATE" if row["compression_ratio"] > 0.85 else "LOW"
    print(f"  {row['title']:22s} CR={row['compression_ratio']:.3f}  "
          f"Gap=${row['gap']:,.0f}  [{status}]")



Visualizing Compression

fig = px.scatter(
    df,
    x="years_service",
    y="salary",
    color="title",
    title="Salary vs Years of Service (Compression Visible)",
    labels={"years_service": "Years of Service", "salary": "Salary ($)"},
    opacity=0.5,
    trendline="lowess"
)

fig.update_layout(height=500)
fig.show()



Salary-Seniority Slope
A flat slope indicates compression - seniority doesn’t pay off.

from numpy.polynomial import polynomial as P

slope_data = []
for title in job_families:
    title_df = df[df["title"] == title]
    x = title_df["years_service"].values
    y = title_df["salary"].values

    # Linear fit
    coeffs = np.polyfit(x, y, 1)
    slope = coeffs[0]  # Dollars per year of service

    slope_data.append({
        "title": title,
        "slope_per_year": slope,
        "midpoint": job_families[title]["mid"],
        "slope_pct": slope / job_families[title]["mid"] * 100
    })

slope_df = pd.DataFrame(slope_data).sort_values("slope_pct")

fig = px.bar(
    slope_df,
    x="slope_pct",
    y="title",
    orientation="h",
    title="Salary Growth per Year of Service (% of Midpoint)",
    labels={"slope_pct": "Annual Growth (% of midpoint)", "title": "Job Title"},
    color="slope_pct",
    color_continuous_scale="RdYlGn"
)

fig.update_layout(showlegend=False)
fig.show()

print("\nSalary-Seniority Slopes:")
for _, row in slope_df.iterrows():
    print(f"  {row['title']:22s}: ${row['slope_per_year']:,.0f}/year "
          f"({row['slope_pct']:.2f}% of midpoint)")

title: Salary Compression Analysis
text:
Inversion Analysis
Salary inversion occurs when newer employees earn more than their senior counterparts.

def find_inversions(df, title, salary_col="salary", seniority_col="years_service"):
    """Find cases where junior employees earn more than senior ones."""
    title_df = df[df["title"] == title].copy()

    junior = title_df[title_df[seniority_col] <= 3]
    senior = title_df[title_df[seniority_col] >= 10]

    if len(junior) == 0 or len(senior) == 0:
        return 0, 0

    # How many juniors earn above the senior median?
    senior_median = senior[salary_col].median()
    inversions = (junior[salary_col] > senior_median).sum()
    inversion_rate = inversions / len(junior) * 100

    return inversions, inversion_rate

print("Inversion Analysis (junior employees earning above senior median):")
print("=" * 60)
for title in job_families:
    count, rate = find_inversions(df, title)
    flag = " ⚠" if rate > 20 else ""
    print(f"  {title:22s}: {count:3d} inversions ({rate:.1f}%){flag}")

title: Salary Compression Analysis
text:
Range Spread Analysis

def range_spread(range_min, range_max):
    """Calculate range spread percentage.

    Range Spread = (Max - Min) / Min * 100
    """
    return (range_max - range_min) / range_min * 100

# Analyze range utilization
range_analysis = []
for title, params in job_families.items():
    title_df = df[df["title"] == title]
    spread = range_spread(params["min"], params["max"])

    # What percentage of employees are in each third?
    third_1 = params["min"] + (params["max"] - params["min"]) / 3
    third_2 = params["min"] + 2 * (params["max"] - params["min"]) / 3

    in_lower = (title_df["salary"] < third_1).mean() * 100
    in_middle = ((title_df["salary"] >= third_1) & (title_df["salary"] < third_2)).mean() * 100
    in_upper = (title_df["salary"] >= third_2).mean() * 100

    range_analysis.append({
        "title": title,
        "range_spread": spread,
        "lower_third": in_lower,
        "middle_third": in_middle,
        "upper_third": in_upper
    })

range_df = pd.DataFrame(range_analysis)

print("Range Utilization (% of employees in each third of range):")
print(range_df.to_string(index=False, float_format=lambda x: f"{x:.1f}"))


# Stacked bar chart of range utilization
fig = go.Figure()

for third, color, label in [
    ("lower_third", "#D55E00", "Lower Third"),
    ("middle_third", "#E69F00", "Middle Third"),
    ("upper_third", "#009E73", "Upper Third")
]:
    fig.add_trace(go.Bar(
        x=range_df["title"],
        y=range_df[third],
        name=label,
        marker_color=color
    ))

fig.update_layout(
    barmode="stack",
    title="Salary Range Utilization by Job Title",
    yaxis_title="Percentage of Employees",
    xaxis_tickangle=-30
)

fig.show()

title: Salary Compression Analysis
text:
Exercise

Exercise 20: Compression Dashboard

Calculate compa-ratios for all employees and identify those below 0.85 or above 1.15
Create a scatter plot of compa-ratio vs years of service, colored by job title
Calculate the compression ratio for each job title and flag those above 0.90
Find the job title with the most salary inversions
Create a summary table showing: title, median compa-ratio, compression ratio, inversion rate, and range position







NoteSolution






# 1. Identify outlier compa-ratios
df["cr_flag"] = pd.cut(
    df["compa_ratio"],
    bins=[0, 0.85, 1.15, 999],
    labels=["Below Range", "On Target", "Above Range"]
)

print("1. Compa-Ratio Distribution:")
print(df["cr_flag"].value_counts())
print(f"\nBelow 0.85: {(df['compa_ratio'] < 0.85).sum()} employees")
print(f"Above 1.15: {(df['compa_ratio'] > 1.15).sum()} employees")

# 2. Scatter plot
fig = px.scatter(
    df, x="years_service", y="compa_ratio", color="title",
    title="Compa-Ratio vs Seniority",
    labels={"compa_ratio": "Compa-Ratio", "years_service": "Years of Service"},
    opacity=0.5
)
fig.add_hline(y=1.0, line_dash="dash", line_color="black")
fig.show()

# 3. Compression ratios
print("\n3. Compression Ratios:")
for title in job_families:
    cr = compression_ratio(df[df["title"] == title], "salary", "years_service")
    flag = "FLAGGED" if cr and cr > 0.90 else ""
    print(f"  {title:22s}: {cr:.3f} {flag}")

# 4. Most inversions
print("\n4. Inversion counts:")
max_inv_title = None
max_inv_rate = 0
for title in job_families:
    count, rate = find_inversions(df, title)
    if rate > max_inv_rate:
        max_inv_rate = rate
        max_inv_title = title
    print(f"  {title:22s}: {rate:.1f}%")
print(f"\n  Highest: {max_inv_title} at {max_inv_rate:.1f}%")

# 5. Summary table
summary_rows = []
for title in job_families:
    title_df = df[df["title"] == title]
    cr = compression_ratio(title_df, "salary", "years_service")
    _, inv_rate = find_inversions(df, title)

    summary_rows.append({
        "Title": title,
        "Median CR": title_df["compa_ratio"].median(),
        "Compression": cr,
        "Inversion %": inv_rate,
        "Median Range Pos": title_df["range_position"].median()
    })

summary = pd.DataFrame(summary_rows)
print("\n5. Summary Table:")
print(summary.to_string(index=False, float_format=lambda x: f"{x:.3f}"))

title: Salary Compression Analysis
text:
Summary
You’ve learned to analyze salary compression:

Compa-Ratio: Where employees sit relative to range midpoint
Range Position: Placement within the full salary range
Compression Ratio: How close junior and senior salaries are
Inversion Detection: Finding where new hires out-earn veterans
Range Utilization: How the salary range is actually used

Key takeaways: - Compression ratios above 0.90 indicate serious compression - Flat salary-seniority slopes mean experience isn’t rewarded - Range utilization reveals whether salary structures are functional - Inversions damage morale and increase senior staff turnover risk

title: Salary Compression Analysis
text:
Next Steps
Continue to Lesson 21: Statistical Testing for Equity to learn how to rigorously test for salary differences across groups.

title: Forecasting and Trend Modeling
text:
After this lesson, you will be able to:

Fit linear and polynomial trend models to historical salary data
Project future salary costs using numpy and statsmodels
Build Monte Carlo simulations for budget planning under uncertainty
Create scenario models for policy decisions (inflation, across-the-board raises)
Calculate and interpret confidence intervals on projections
Visualize forecast uncertainty with fan charts
Recognize the limitations and risks of extrapolation

title: Forecasting and Trend Modeling
text:
Introduction

Why Forecast Salary Costs?
University budget offices must plan 2-5 years ahead. Salary and fringe benefits typically account for 70-80% of operating budgets. Forecasting helps answer critical questions:

Budget requests: “How much will we need for salaries next fiscal year?”
Policy modeling: “What does a 3% across-the-board raise cost over 5 years?”
Risk assessment: “What is the range of plausible salary expenditures if inflation varies?”

Forecasting is not prediction — it is structured reasoning about possible futures.


Budget Requests and Scenario Planning
In public higher education, forecasts don’t just inform internal planning — they’re the foundation of budget requests to the state:
The biennial budget process (Wisconsin):

UW System submits a biennial budget request to the Governor ~18 months before the biennium begins
The request includes a base budget (continuing current operations) and decision items (new funding requests)
Salary and fringe benefit projections are the largest component of the base budget
The Governor’s budget office and Joint Finance Committee scrutinize these projections — credible forecasting methodology matters

Why multiple scenarios are essential:

Legislative audiences distrust single-point estimates. Presenting a range (conservative, baseline, aggressive) demonstrates analytical rigor and lets decision-makers choose their risk tolerance.
Base scenario: Assumes current pay plan continues, no new positions, historical attrition rates
Investment scenario: Models the cost of competitive raises, strategic hires, or equity adjustments — this is your “ask”
Austerity scenario: Models budget cuts, hiring freezes, or furloughs — this is your contingency plan

Connecting forecasts to decisions: A forecast showing that “salary costs will grow 4% annually while state appropriations grow 1%” is powerful because it quantifies the structural deficit. Adding “under the investment scenario, a one-time $5M equity pool would close 60% of the peer salary gap” turns a forecast into a budget request with a specific dollar amount and measurable outcome.
The Monte Carlo simulation techniques in this lesson let you attach probability ranges to each scenario, making your projections more credible and useful to decision-makers.


import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots

# Wong colorblind-safe palette
WONG = ["#E69F00", "#56B4E9", "#009E73", "#F0E442", "#0072B2", "#D55E00", "#CC79A7"]

title: Forecasting and Trend Modeling
text:
Generating Historical Salary Trend Data
We will build a realistic multi-year dataset of aggregate salary costs for several divisions, reflecting the kinds of trends you would see in UW-Madison data.

np.random.seed(42)

years = np.arange(2015, 2026)
n_years = len(years)

# Base costs in millions for each division
divisions = {
    "L&S": {"base": 320, "annual_growth": 0.028, "volatility": 0.008},
    "Medicine": {"base": 280, "annual_growth": 0.035, "volatility": 0.012},
    "Engineering": {"base": 150, "annual_growth": 0.032, "volatility": 0.010},
    "Business": {"base": 85, "annual_growth": 0.030, "volatility": 0.009},
    "Education": {"base": 65, "annual_growth": 0.022, "volatility": 0.007},
}

records = []
for div, params in divisions.items():
    cost = params["base"]
    for i, year in enumerate(years):
        # Growth with some noise
        if i > 0:
            growth = params["annual_growth"] + np.random.normal(0, params["volatility"])
            cost = cost * (1 + growth)
        records.append({
            "division": div,
            "year": year,
            "total_salary_millions": round(cost, 2)
        })

df = pd.DataFrame(records)
print(f"Historical salary data: {len(df)} rows, {df['year'].nunique()} years")
print(df.pivot_table(index="year", columns="division", values="total_salary_millions").round(1))







TipIn R





# Generating similar trend data in R
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)

set.seed(42)
years <- 2015:2025
divisions <- list(
  "L&S" = list(base = 320, growth = 0.028, vol = 0.008),
  "Medicine" = list(base = 280, growth = 0.035, vol = 0.012)
)

# R approach using purrr or a for loop
df <- purrr::map_dfr(names(divisions), function(div) {
  params <- divisions[[div]]
  cost <- params$base
  tibble(year = years) %>%
    mutate(
      growth = c(0, rnorm(length(years) - 1, params$growth, params$vol)),
      total = params$base * cumprod(1 + growth),
      division = div
    )
})

title: Forecasting and Trend Modeling
text:
Linear Trend Modeling with NumPy
The simplest forecast fits a line through historical data and extends it. np.polyfit estimates the slope and intercept using ordinary least squares.

# Focus on university-wide total
totals = df.groupby("year")["total_salary_millions"].sum().reset_index()
totals.columns = ["year", "total"]

print("University-wide total salary costs (millions):")
print(totals.to_string(index=False))

# Fit a linear trend
coeffs = np.polyfit(totals["year"], totals["total"], 1)
slope, intercept = coeffs
print(f"\nLinear trend: {slope:.2f}M per year")
print(f"Intercept: {intercept:.2f}")

# Project forward 5 years
future_years = np.arange(2026, 2031)
projected = np.polyval(coeffs, future_years)

# Combine for plotting
fig = go.Figure()

fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=totals["year"], y=totals["total"],
    mode="markers+lines", name="Historical",
    marker=dict(color=WONG[4], size=8),
    line=dict(color=WONG[4], width=2)
))

fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=future_years, y=projected,
    mode="markers+lines", name="Linear Projection",
    marker=dict(color=WONG[5], size=8, symbol="diamond"),
    line=dict(color=WONG[5], width=2, dash="dash")
))

# Trend line over full range
all_years = np.arange(2015, 2031)
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=all_years, y=np.polyval(coeffs, all_years),
    mode="lines", name="Trend Line",
    line=dict(color=WONG[0], width=1, dash="dot")
))

fig.update_layout(
    title="University-Wide Salary Costs: Linear Projection",
    xaxis_title="Year",
    yaxis_title="Total Salary Cost ($M)",
    height=450
)
fig.show()


Polynomial and Exponential Trends
Salary growth is typically compounding, not linear. A quadratic or exponential model may fit better.

# Quadratic fit
coeffs_quad = np.polyfit(totals["year"], totals["total"], 2)
projected_quad = np.polyval(coeffs_quad, future_years)

# Exponential fit via log-linear regression
log_totals = np.log(totals["total"])
coeffs_exp = np.polyfit(totals["year"], log_totals, 1)
projected_exp = np.exp(np.polyval(coeffs_exp, future_years))

fig = go.Figure()

fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=totals["year"], y=totals["total"],
    mode="markers+lines", name="Historical",
    marker=dict(color=WONG[4], size=8),
    line=dict(color=WONG[4], width=2)
))

for name, proj, color in [
    ("Linear", projected, WONG[0]),
    ("Quadratic", projected_quad, WONG[2]),
    ("Exponential", projected_exp, WONG[5])
]:
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=future_years, y=proj,
        mode="lines+markers", name=name,
        line=dict(color=color, width=2, dash="dash"),
        marker=dict(size=6)
    ))

fig.update_layout(
    title="Comparing Trend Models for Salary Projections",
    xaxis_title="Year", yaxis_title="Total Salary Cost ($M)",
    height=450
)
fig.show()

print("Projected 2030 costs by model:")
print(f"  Linear:      ${projected[-1]:,.1f}M")
print(f"  Quadratic:   ${projected_quad[-1]:,.1f}M")
print(f"  Exponential: ${projected_exp[-1]:,.1f}M")







TipIn R





# Linear model in R
model <- lm(total ~ year, data = totals)
predict(model, newdata = data.frame(year = 2026:2030))

# Exponential model
model_exp <- lm(log(total) ~ year, data = totals)
exp(predict(model_exp, newdata = data.frame(year = 2026:2030)))

title: Forecasting and Trend Modeling
text:
Regression with statsmodels
For richer diagnostics — R-squared, p-values, confidence intervals — use statsmodels, the Python equivalent of R’s lm().

import statsmodels.api as sm

X = sm.add_constant(totals["year"])
model = sm.OLS(totals["total"], X).fit()

print(model.summary())


import statsmodels.api as sm

X = sm.add_constant(totals["year"])
model = sm.OLS(totals["total"], X).fit()

# Key diagnostics
print(f"R-squared: {model.rsquared:.4f}")
print(f"Slope:     {model.params.iloc[1]:.2f}M per year (p={model.pvalues.iloc[1]:.4f})")
print(f"Intercept: {model.params.iloc[0]:.2f}")

# Prediction with confidence intervals
X_future = sm.add_constant(future_years)
predictions = model.get_prediction(X_future)
pred_summary = predictions.summary_frame(alpha=0.05)

print("\nProjections with 95% confidence intervals:")
for i, year in enumerate(future_years):
    row = pred_summary.iloc[i]
    print(f"  {year}: ${row['mean']:,.1f}M  "
          f"[${row['mean_ci_lower']:,.1f}M - ${row['mean_ci_upper']:,.1f}M]")


Confidence vs Prediction Intervals

Confidence interval (mean_ci): Range for the average outcome. Narrow — captures uncertainty in the regression line itself.
Prediction interval (obs_ci): Range for a single future observation. Wider — also captures year-to-year randomness.

For budget planning, prediction intervals are more realistic because they account for the variability you actually experience each year.


# Visualize confidence and prediction intervals
fig = go.Figure()

# Historical
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=totals["year"], y=totals["total"],
    mode="markers+lines", name="Historical",
    marker=dict(color=WONG[4], size=8),
    line=dict(color=WONG[4], width=2)
))

# Prediction interval (wider)
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=np.concatenate([future_years, future_years[::-1]]),
    y=np.concatenate([pred_summary["obs_ci_upper"], pred_summary["obs_ci_lower"][::-1]]),
    fill="toself", fillcolor="rgba(86,180,233,0.15)",
    line=dict(color="rgba(0,0,0,0)"),
    name="95% Prediction Interval"
))

# Confidence interval (narrower)
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=np.concatenate([future_years, future_years[::-1]]),
    y=np.concatenate([pred_summary["mean_ci_upper"], pred_summary["mean_ci_lower"][::-1]]),
    fill="toself", fillcolor="rgba(86,180,233,0.35)",
    line=dict(color="rgba(0,0,0,0)"),
    name="95% Confidence Interval"
))

# Point forecast
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=future_years, y=pred_summary["mean"],
    mode="lines+markers", name="Forecast",
    line=dict(color=WONG[5], width=2, dash="dash"),
    marker=dict(color=WONG[5], size=7)
))

fig.update_layout(
    title="Salary Projection with Confidence and Prediction Intervals",
    xaxis_title="Year", yaxis_title="Total Salary Cost ($M)",
    height=475
)
fig.show()

title: Forecasting and Trend Modeling
section: Monte Carlo Simulation for Budget Planning
text:
Monte Carlo Simulation for Budget Planning

What is Monte Carlo Simulation?
Monte Carlo simulation generates thousands of possible futures by randomly sampling from assumed distributions. Instead of one projection, you get a distribution of outcomes. This reveals:

The range of plausible costs
The probability that costs exceed a threshold
Which assumptions matter most



np.random.seed(123)

n_simulations = 5000
n_forecast_years = 5
current_cost = totals["total"].iloc[-1]

# Historical growth rate statistics
hist_growth = totals["total"].pct_change().dropna()
mean_growth = hist_growth.mean()
std_growth = hist_growth.std()

print(f"Historical annual growth: {mean_growth:.3f} ({mean_growth*100:.1f}%)")
print(f"Growth std deviation:     {std_growth:.3f} ({std_growth*100:.1f}%)")
print(f"Current cost (2025):      ${current_cost:,.1f}M")

# Run simulations
simulations = np.zeros((n_simulations, n_forecast_years))
for sim in range(n_simulations):
    cost = current_cost
    for yr in range(n_forecast_years):
        growth = np.random.normal(mean_growth, std_growth)
        cost = cost * (1 + growth)
        simulations[sim, yr] = cost

# Summary statistics
sim_df = pd.DataFrame(simulations, columns=[str(y) for y in range(2026, 2031)])
print("\nMonte Carlo Results (Total Salary Cost, $M):")
print(sim_df.describe(percentiles=[0.05, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 0.95]).round(1))







TipIn R





# Monte Carlo in R
set.seed(123)
n_sims <- 5000
current_cost <- tail(totals$total, 1)
mean_g <- mean(diff(totals$total) / head(totals$total, -1))
sd_g <- sd(diff(totals$total) / head(totals$total, -1))

sims <- matrix(NA, n_sims, 5)
for (s in 1:n_sims) {
  cost <- current_cost
  for (yr in 1:5) {
    cost <- cost * (1 + rnorm(1, mean_g, sd_g))
    sims[s, yr] <- cost
  }
}
apply(sims, 2, quantile, probs = c(0.05, 0.5, 0.95))




Visualizing Monte Carlo Results

# Plot a sample of simulation paths plus percentile bands
fig = go.Figure()

# Historical line
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=totals["year"].tolist(), y=totals["total"].tolist(),
    mode="lines+markers", name="Historical",
    line=dict(color=WONG[4], width=2),
    marker=dict(size=6)
))

forecast_years = list(range(2026, 2031))

# Plot 100 sample paths (semi-transparent)
for i in range(100):
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=forecast_years, y=simulations[i, :],
        mode="lines", line=dict(color="rgba(204,121,167,0.08)", width=1),
        showlegend=False, hoverinfo="skip"
    ))

# Percentile bands
p05 = np.percentile(simulations, 5, axis=0)
p25 = np.percentile(simulations, 25, axis=0)
p50 = np.percentile(simulations, 50, axis=0)
p75 = np.percentile(simulations, 75, axis=0)
p95 = np.percentile(simulations, 95, axis=0)

# 90% band (5th-95th)
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=forecast_years + forecast_years[::-1],
    y=p95.tolist() + p05[::-1].tolist(),
    fill="toself", fillcolor="rgba(230,159,0,0.15)",
    line=dict(color="rgba(0,0,0,0)"),
    name="90% Range"
))

# 50% band (25th-75th)
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=forecast_years + forecast_years[::-1],
    y=p75.tolist() + p25[::-1].tolist(),
    fill="toself", fillcolor="rgba(230,159,0,0.35)",
    line=dict(color="rgba(0,0,0,0)"),
    name="50% Range"
))

# Median projection
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=forecast_years, y=p50.tolist(),
    mode="lines", name="Median Projection",
    line=dict(color=WONG[5], width=2.5, dash="dash")
))

fig.update_layout(
    title="Monte Carlo Salary Cost Forecast (5,000 Simulations)",
    xaxis_title="Year", yaxis_title="Total Salary Cost ($M)",
    height=500
)
fig.show()



Budget Threshold Analysis

# What is the probability that 2028 costs exceed $1,100M?
threshold = 1100
year_idx = 2  # 2028 is index 2 (2026=0, 2027=1, 2028=2)
prob_exceed = (simulations[:, year_idx] > threshold).mean() * 100

print(f"P(2028 costs > ${threshold:,}M) = {prob_exceed:.1f}%")

# Probability table for multiple thresholds
print("\nProbability of exceeding cost thresholds:")
print(f"{'Year':<8}", end="")
for t in [1050, 1100, 1150, 1200]:
    print(f"  >${t}M", end="")
print()

for yr_idx, yr in enumerate(forecast_years):
    print(f"{yr:<8}", end="")
    for t in [1050, 1100, 1150, 1200]:
        prob = (simulations[:, yr_idx] > t).mean() * 100
        print(f"  {prob:5.1f}%", end="")
    print()

title: Forecasting and Trend Modeling
text:
Scenario Modeling
Scenario modeling answers “what if” questions by changing assumptions explicitly rather than sampling randomly.

# Define scenarios
scenarios = {
    "Baseline (historical avg)": {"growth": mean_growth},
    "Low inflation (2%)": {"growth": 0.020},
    "High inflation (4%)": {"growth": 0.040},
    "3% ATB raise": {"growth": 0.030},
    "Wage freeze": {"growth": 0.005},
    "Aggressive hiring (5%)": {"growth": 0.050},
}

fig = go.Figure()

# Historical
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=totals["year"].tolist(), y=totals["total"].tolist(),
    mode="lines+markers", name="Historical",
    line=dict(color="black", width=2),
    marker=dict(size=6, color="black")
))

for i, (name, params) in enumerate(scenarios.items()):
    projected = [current_cost]
    for yr in range(n_forecast_years):
        projected.append(projected[-1] * (1 + params["growth"]))

    color = WONG[i % len(WONG)]
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=[2025] + forecast_years, y=projected,
        mode="lines+markers", name=name,
        line=dict(color=color, width=2, dash="dash"),
        marker=dict(size=5)
    ))

fig.update_layout(
    title="Scenario Analysis: Salary Cost Trajectories",
    xaxis_title="Year", yaxis_title="Total Salary Cost ($M)",
    height=500, legend=dict(x=0.02, y=0.98)
)
fig.show()

# Summary table
print("Projected 2030 Total Salary Cost by Scenario:")
print("=" * 55)
for name, params in scenarios.items():
    cost_2030 = current_cost * (1 + params["growth"]) ** 5
    increase = cost_2030 - current_cost
    print(f"  {name:30s}: ${cost_2030:,.1f}M  (+${increase:,.1f}M)")


PyCharm: Comparing Scenario Runs
Use PyCharm’s Scientific Mode to run scenario analyses interactively. You can modify growth assumptions in the Python console and immediately see updated projections in the built-in plot viewer — without rerunning the entire notebook.

title: Forecasting and Trend Modeling
text:
Fan Charts for Communicating Uncertainty

Fan Charts
A fan chart displays a central projection surrounded by shaded bands representing different probability levels. The Bank of England popularized this format for inflation forecasts. Each band covers a progressively wider interval (e.g., 10%, 30%, 50%, 70%, 90%), creating a “fan” shape that widens into the future.
Fan charts honestly communicate that uncertainty grows over time.


# Build fan chart from Monte Carlo results
fig = go.Figure()

# Historical
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=totals["year"].tolist(), y=totals["total"].tolist(),
    mode="lines+markers", name="Historical",
    line=dict(color="black", width=2),
    marker=dict(size=6, color="black")
))

# Fan bands: 90%, 70%, 50%, 30%, 10%
bands = [
    (5, 95, "rgba(0,114,178,0.10)", "90% interval"),
    (15, 85, "rgba(0,114,178,0.18)", "70% interval"),
    (25, 75, "rgba(0,114,178,0.28)", "50% interval"),
    (35, 65, "rgba(0,114,178,0.40)", "30% interval"),
    (45, 55, "rgba(0,114,178,0.55)", "10% interval"),
]

for lo_pct, hi_pct, color, label in bands:
    lo = np.percentile(simulations, lo_pct, axis=0)
    hi = np.percentile(simulations, hi_pct, axis=0)

    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=forecast_years + forecast_years[::-1],
        y=hi.tolist() + lo[::-1].tolist(),
        fill="toself", fillcolor=color,
        line=dict(color="rgba(0,0,0,0)"),
        name=label
    ))

# Median line
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=forecast_years, y=p50.tolist(),
    mode="lines", name="Median",
    line=dict(color=WONG[5], width=2.5)
))

fig.update_layout(
    title="Fan Chart: Salary Cost Forecast with Uncertainty Bands",
    xaxis_title="Year", yaxis_title="Total Salary Cost ($M)",
    height=500
)
fig.show()

title: Forecasting and Trend Modeling
text:
Division-Level Forecasts
Forecasting at the division level allows budget managers to plan unit-specific allocations.

np.random.seed(456)

fig = make_subplots(
    rows=2, cols=3,
    subplot_titles=list(divisions.keys()) + ["University Total"],
    shared_yaxes=False, vertical_spacing=0.12, horizontal_spacing=0.08
)

for idx, div in enumerate(divisions.keys()):
    row = idx // 3 + 1
    col = idx % 3 + 1

    div_data = df[df["division"] == div]
    hist_costs = div_data.groupby("year")["total_salary_millions"].sum().values

    # Division-specific Monte Carlo
    div_growth = pd.Series(hist_costs).pct_change().dropna()
    div_mean = div_growth.mean()
    div_std = div_growth.std()

    div_sims = np.zeros((2000, 5))
    last_cost = hist_costs[-1]
    for s in range(2000):
        cost = last_cost
        for yr in range(5):
            cost = cost * (1 + np.random.normal(div_mean, div_std))
            div_sims[s, yr] = cost

    # Historical
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=years.tolist(), y=hist_costs.tolist(),
        mode="lines+markers", name=div, showlegend=False,
        line=dict(color=WONG[idx], width=2),
        marker=dict(size=4)
    ), row=row, col=col)

    # Forecast band
    lo = np.percentile(div_sims, 10, axis=0)
    hi = np.percentile(div_sims, 90, axis=0)
    med = np.percentile(div_sims, 50, axis=0)

    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=forecast_years + forecast_years[::-1],
        y=hi.tolist() + lo[::-1].tolist(),
        fill="toself", fillcolor=f"rgba(0,114,178,0.15)",
        line=dict(color="rgba(0,0,0,0)"),
        showlegend=False
    ), row=row, col=col)

    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=forecast_years, y=med.tolist(),
        mode="lines", showlegend=False,
        line=dict(color=WONG[idx], width=2, dash="dash")
    ), row=row, col=col)

# University total in bottom-right
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=totals["year"].tolist(), y=totals["total"].tolist(),
    mode="lines+markers", name="Total", showlegend=False,
    line=dict(color="black", width=2), marker=dict(size=4)
), row=2, col=3)

fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=forecast_years + forecast_years[::-1],
    y=p95.tolist() + p05[::-1].tolist(),
    fill="toself", fillcolor="rgba(0,0,0,0.1)",
    line=dict(color="rgba(0,0,0,0)"), showlegend=False
), row=2, col=3)

fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=forecast_years, y=p50.tolist(),
    mode="lines", showlegend=False,
    line=dict(color="black", width=2, dash="dash")
), row=2, col=3)

fig.update_layout(title="Division-Level Salary Forecasts with 80% Bands", height=550)
fig.show()

title: Forecasting and Trend Modeling
text:
Limitations of Extrapolation

The Extrapolation Trap
All projection methods assume the future resembles the past. This assumption breaks when:

Structural changes occur: new state funding formulas, hiring freezes, reorganizations
Policy shifts: a new chancellor reverses salary priorities
External shocks: pandemics, recessions, legislative actions
Nonlinear dynamics: salary compression accelerating, retirement waves

Best practices:

Never project beyond 2x your data window — 10 years of data supports at most 5 years of projection
Always show uncertainty — point forecasts without intervals are misleading
Validate on holdout data — fit on 2015-2022, test on 2023-2025
Combine with qualitative judgment — models inform, they do not decide
Update frequently — a forecast made in July is stale by December



Cross-Validation Example

# Fit on 2015-2022, test on 2023-2025
train = totals[totals["year"] <= 2022]
test = totals[totals["year"] > 2022]

# Fit linear model on training data
coeffs_train = np.polyfit(train["year"], train["total"], 1)
test_pred = np.polyval(coeffs_train, test["year"])

# Calculate forecast errors
errors = test["total"].values - test_pred
mape = np.mean(np.abs(errors) / test["total"].values) * 100

print("Cross-Validation: Train on 2015-2022, Test on 2023-2025")
print("-" * 50)
for i, year in enumerate(test["year"]):
    print(f"  {year}: Actual=${test['total'].values[i]:,.1f}M  "
          f"Predicted=${test_pred[i]:,.1f}M  "
          f"Error={errors[i]:+,.1f}M")
print(f"\nMean Absolute % Error (MAPE): {mape:.1f}%")

title: Forecasting and Trend Modeling
text:
Exercise

Exercise 22: Budget Forecast for the College of Engineering
Using the generated data:

Extract the Engineering division’s historical salary costs (2015-2025)
Fit both a linear and exponential trend model
Run 3,000 Monte Carlo simulations projecting 2026-2030
Calculate the probability that Engineering’s costs exceed $200M by 2030
Create a fan chart with 50% and 90% bands
Build a scenario comparison: 2% growth vs 4% growth vs historical average
Print a summary table showing each scenario’s 2030 projected cost







NoteSolution






# 1. Extract Engineering data
eng = df[df["division"] == "Engineering"].copy()
eng_yearly = eng.groupby("year")["total_salary_millions"].sum().reset_index()
eng_yearly.columns = ["year", "cost"]

print("Engineering Historical Costs:")
print(eng_yearly.to_string(index=False))

# 2. Linear and exponential trend models
coeffs_lin = np.polyfit(eng_yearly["year"], eng_yearly["cost"], 1)
coeffs_exp = np.polyfit(eng_yearly["year"], np.log(eng_yearly["cost"]), 1)

future = np.arange(2026, 2031)
proj_lin = np.polyval(coeffs_lin, future)
proj_exp = np.exp(np.polyval(coeffs_exp, future))

print(f"\nLinear slope: {coeffs_lin[0]:.2f}M/year")
print(f"Exponential growth rate: {coeffs_exp[0]*100:.2f}%/year")

# 3. Monte Carlo simulation
np.random.seed(789)
eng_growth = eng_yearly["cost"].pct_change().dropna()
eng_mean_g = eng_growth.mean()
eng_std_g = eng_growth.std()
eng_current = eng_yearly["cost"].iloc[-1]

n_sims = 3000
eng_sims = np.zeros((n_sims, 5))
for s in range(n_sims):
    cost = eng_current
    for yr in range(5):
        cost = cost * (1 + np.random.normal(eng_mean_g, eng_std_g))
        eng_sims[s, yr] = cost

print(f"\nMonte Carlo median 2030: ${np.median(eng_sims[:, -1]):,.1f}M")

# 4. Probability of exceeding $200M by 2030
prob_200 = (eng_sims[:, -1] > 200).mean() * 100
print(f"P(Engineering > $200M by 2030): {prob_200:.1f}%")

# 5. Fan chart
fig = go.Figure()

fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=eng_yearly["year"].tolist(), y=eng_yearly["cost"].tolist(),
    mode="lines+markers", name="Historical",
    line=dict(color="black", width=2), marker=dict(size=6, color="black")
))

for lo_pct, hi_pct, color, label in [
    (5, 95, "rgba(0,158,115,0.15)", "90% interval"),
    (25, 75, "rgba(0,158,115,0.35)", "50% interval"),
]:
    lo = np.percentile(eng_sims, lo_pct, axis=0)
    hi = np.percentile(eng_sims, hi_pct, axis=0)
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=future.tolist() + future[::-1].tolist(),
        y=hi.tolist() + lo[::-1].tolist(),
        fill="toself", fillcolor=color,
        line=dict(color="rgba(0,0,0,0)"), name=label
    ))

eng_median = np.median(eng_sims, axis=0)
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=future.tolist(), y=eng_median.tolist(),
    mode="lines", name="Median Forecast",
    line=dict(color=WONG[2], width=2.5, dash="dash")
))

fig.add_hline(y=200, line_dash="dot", line_color=WONG[5],
              annotation_text="$200M Threshold")

fig.update_layout(
    title="Engineering Division: Salary Cost Fan Chart",
    xaxis_title="Year", yaxis_title="Salary Cost ($M)",
    height=475
)
fig.show()

# 6. Scenario comparison
eng_scenarios = {
    "Low growth (2%)": 0.020,
    "Historical avg": eng_mean_g,
    "High growth (4%)": 0.040,
}

fig2 = go.Figure()

fig2.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=eng_yearly["year"].tolist(), y=eng_yearly["cost"].tolist(),
    mode="lines+markers", name="Historical",
    line=dict(color="black", width=2), marker=dict(size=6, color="black")
))

for i, (name, rate) in enumerate(eng_scenarios.items()):
    proj = [eng_current]
    for yr in range(5):
        proj.append(proj[-1] * (1 + rate))
    fig2.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=[2025] + future.tolist(), y=proj,
        mode="lines+markers", name=f"{name} ({rate*100:.1f}%)",
        line=dict(color=WONG[i], width=2, dash="dash"),
        marker=dict(size=5)
    ))

fig2.update_layout(
    title="Engineering Salary Cost: Scenario Comparison",
    xaxis_title="Year", yaxis_title="Salary Cost ($M)",
    height=450
)
fig2.show()

# 7. Summary table
print("\nScenario Summary - Engineering 2030 Projected Cost:")
print("=" * 50)
for name, rate in eng_scenarios.items():
    cost_2030 = eng_current * (1 + rate) ** 5
    increase = cost_2030 - eng_current
    print(f"  {name:25s}: ${cost_2030:,.1f}M  (+${increase:,.1f}M)")

title: Forecasting and Trend Modeling
text:
Summary
You have learned to build and communicate salary cost forecasts:

Linear and exponential trends: Quick projections using np.polyfit and statsmodels
Confidence intervals: Quantifying uncertainty in the trend line and individual predictions
Monte Carlo simulation: Generating thousands of scenarios to map the full distribution of outcomes
Scenario modeling: Testing specific “what if” assumptions for policy decisions
Fan charts: The gold standard for visualizing forecast uncertainty
Cross-validation: Testing how well your model would have performed on recent data

Key takeaways:

A single-number forecast is almost always wrong — always present ranges
Monte Carlo simulation is straightforward in Python and provides rich probability statements
Scenario modeling translates policy questions into quantitative projections
Extrapolation beyond your data window is dangerous — validate and update frequently
Fan charts communicate uncertainty honestly to decision-makers

Next Steps

title: Markov Chain Workforce Modeling
text:
After this lesson, you will be able to:

Explain Markov chain concepts (states, transitions, stationarity) in a workforce context
Build transition probability matrices from employee movement data
Estimate transition probabilities from historical observations
Predict future workforce composition using matrix multiplication with NumPy
Compute steady-state distributions and interpret long-run workforce structure
Model absorbing states such as departure and retirement
Assess sensitivity of workforce projections to changes in promotion rates
Visualize transition matrices with heatmaps and Sankey diagrams using Plotly

title: Markov Chain Workforce Modeling
text:
Introduction

What is a Markov Chain?
A Markov chain is a mathematical model for systems that transition between discrete states over time. The key assumption is the Markov property: the probability of moving to any future state depends only on the current state, not on the history of how we got here.
In workforce modeling, the states are employee categories (e.g., rank, division, or employment status), and the transition probabilities describe how employees move between states from one period to the next.
Key terminology:

State space: The set of all possible states (e.g., Assistant Prof, Associate Prof, Full Prof, Departed)
Transition probability matrix (P): A square matrix where entry \(P_{ij}\) is the probability of moving from state \(i\) to state \(j\). Each row sums to 1.
Stationarity: The assumption that transition probabilities do not change over time (time-homogeneous chain)
Steady state: The long-run distribution the workforce converges to, regardless of starting composition
Absorbing state: A state that, once entered, cannot be left (e.g., “Departed”)



The Demographic Cliff and Strategic Workforce Planning
Workforce modeling isn’t happening in a vacuum — higher education faces a structural demographic shift that makes these tools urgently relevant:
The enrollment cliff (2025-2037): Birth rates dropped sharply after 2008, meaning the traditional college-age population (18-year-olds) will decline significantly starting around 2025 and bottoming out around 2037. For many institutions, this means fewer students, less tuition revenue, and ultimately fewer positions to fill.
Implications for workforce modeling:

Hiring freezes vs. strategic growth: A Markov model can project what happens if you freeze hiring in some areas while strategically growing others (e.g., growing data science while allowing retirements in declining departments to go unfilled). The steady-state analysis shows whether this strategy converges to a viable workforce composition.
Retirement wave: Many faculty hired during the 1990s expansion are approaching retirement. Your transition matrix captures retirement rates by rank and seniority — projecting the retirement wave helps plan for knowledge transfer and succession.
Composition shifts: As senior faculty retire and are replaced (or not) by junior hires, the faculty rank distribution shifts. Markov projections can show how long it takes for the workforce to reach a new equilibrium after a policy change.

Connecting to institutional strategy: UW’s strategic plan includes goals for research growth, teaching innovation, and diversity. Workforce models can test whether current hiring and promotion patterns will achieve diversity targets, maintain research capacity, or create unsustainable cost trajectories. A Markov model showing that “at current promotion rates, the share of Full Professors will exceed 60% by 2030, creating a top-heavy salary structure” is the kind of insight that changes policy.


import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots

# Wong color palette for accessibility
wong = ["#E69F00", "#56B4E9", "#009E73", "#F0E442", "#0072B2", "#D55E00", "#CC79A7"]

title: Markov Chain Workforce Modeling
section: Building Transition Data from Employee Movements
text:
Building Transition Data from Employee Movements
We begin by generating a realistic dataset of faculty who move between ranks over several annual observation periods. Each row records one employee’s state in consecutive years.

np.random.seed(42)

states = ["Assistant Prof", "Associate Prof", "Full Prof", "Departed"]
n_employees = 500
n_years = 8

# True transition probabilities (rows = from, cols = to)
# Order: Assistant, Associate, Full, Departed
true_P = np.array([
    [0.70, 0.18, 0.00, 0.12],  # Assistant Prof
    [0.00, 0.72, 0.18, 0.10],  # Associate Prof
    [0.00, 0.00, 0.88, 0.12],  # Full Prof
    [0.00, 0.00, 0.00, 1.00],  # Departed (absorbing)
])

# Generate employee histories
records = []
for emp_id in range(n_employees):
    # Starting state: weighted toward Assistant Prof
    current = np.random.choice(
        len(states), p=[0.50, 0.30, 0.20, 0.00]
    )
    division = np.random.choice([
        "Letters & Science", "Engineering", "Medicine", "Business"
    ])

    for year in range(2017, 2017 + n_years):
        records.append({
            "employee_id": f"EMP{emp_id:04d}",
            "year": year,
            "state": states[current],
            "division": division,
        })
        # Transition to next state
        current = np.random.choice(len(states), p=true_P[current])

df = pd.DataFrame(records)
print(f"Records: {len(df):,}")
print(f"Employees: {df['employee_id'].nunique()}")
print(f"\nState counts (all years):")
print(df["state"].value_counts())







TipIn R





library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)

# In R, you might use the markovchain package:
# install.packages("markovchain")
library(markovchain)

# Create transition matrix from observed data
transitions <- df %>%
  arrange(employee_id, year) %>%
  group_by(employee_id) %>%
  mutate(next_state = lead(state)) %>%
  filter(!is.na(next_state))

# Fit a Markov chain
mc_fit <- markovchainFit(data = transitions$state)
mc_fit$estimate

title: Markov Chain Workforce Modeling
text:
Estimating Transition Probabilities
To estimate the transition matrix, we count how many employees moved from each state to every other state, then normalize each row to get probabilities.

# Create paired observations: (state_t, state_t+1)
df_sorted = df.sort_values(["employee_id", "year"])
df_sorted["next_state"] = df_sorted.groupby("employee_id")["state"].shift(-1)
transitions = df_sorted.dropna(subset=["next_state"]).copy()

print(f"Transition observations: {len(transitions):,}")
print(transitions[["employee_id", "year", "state", "next_state"]].head(10))


# Count transitions
transition_counts = pd.crosstab(
    transitions["state"], transitions["next_state"]
)

# Reorder rows and columns to match our state ordering
transition_counts = transition_counts.reindex(
    index=states, columns=states, fill_value=0
)

print("Transition Counts:")
print(transition_counts)


# Normalize to get probabilities
P_estimated = transition_counts.div(transition_counts.sum(axis=1), axis=0)

print("\nEstimated Transition Probability Matrix:")
print(P_estimated.round(3))

print("\nRow sums (should be 1.0):")
print(P_estimated.sum(axis=1).round(6))


Reading the Transition Matrix
Each row of the matrix describes the fate of employees currently in that state. For example, if the “Assistant Prof” row reads [0.70, 0.18, 0.00, 0.12], it means:

70% of Assistant Professors remain Assistant Professors next year
18% are promoted to Associate Professor
0% jump directly to Full Professor
12% depart the university

The diagonal entries represent retention rates – the probability of staying in the same state. Higher diagonal values mean lower turnover.

title: Markov Chain Workforce Modeling
text:
Visualizing the Transition Matrix

Heatmap

fig = px.imshow(
    P_estimated.values,
    x=states,
    y=states,
    text_auto=".2f",
    color_continuous_scale=[[0, "#FFFFFF"], [0.5, "#56B4E9"], [1.0, "#0072B2"]],
    labels={"x": "To State", "y": "From State", "color": "Probability"},
    title="Estimated Transition Probability Matrix"
)

fig.update_layout(
    width=600, height=500,
    xaxis_title="To State",
    yaxis_title="From State"
)
fig.show()



Sankey Diagram
A Sankey diagram shows the flow of employees between states, making the volume of transitions immediately visible.

# Build Sankey from transition counts
source_indices = []
target_indices = []
values = []
link_colors = []

# Create labels: "State (Year t)" on left, "State (Year t+1)" on right
left_labels = [f"{s} (t)" for s in states]
right_labels = [f"{s} (t+1)" for s in states]
all_labels = left_labels + right_labels

for i, from_state in enumerate(states):
    for j, to_state in enumerate(states):
        count = transition_counts.iloc[i, j]
        if count > 0:
            source_indices.append(i)          # left side
            target_indices.append(len(states) + j)  # right side
            values.append(count)
            link_colors.append(wong[i % len(wong)])

fig = go.Figure(data=[go.Sankey(
    node=dict(
        pad=20,
        thickness=20,
        label=all_labels,
        color=[wong[i % len(wong)] for i in range(len(states))] +
              [wong[i % len(wong)] for i in range(len(states))]
    ),
    link=dict(
        source=source_indices,
        target=target_indices,
        value=values,
        color=[c.replace(")", ", 0.4)").replace("rgb", "rgba")
               if "rgb" in c else c + "66"
               for c in link_colors]
    )
)])

fig.update_layout(
    title="Employee Transitions Between States (One-Year Flow)",
    font_size=12,
    width=700, height=450
)
fig.show()


PyCharm: Viewing Plotly in Scientific Mode
In PyCharm Professional, enable Scientific Mode (View > Scientific Mode) to see Plotly charts rendered in the built-in SciView panel. In Community Edition, Plotly figures will open in your browser. You can also call fig.write_html("sankey.html") and open the file manually.

title: Markov Chain Workforce Modeling
text:
Predicting Future Workforce Composition
With the transition matrix in hand, we can project future workforce composition using matrix multiplication. If \(\mathbf{s}_t\) is the workforce distribution vector at time \(t\), then:
\[\mathbf{s}_{t+1} = \mathbf{s}_t \cdot P\]
After \(n\) steps:
\[\mathbf{s}_{t+n} = \mathbf{s}_t \cdot P^n\]

# Current workforce composition (exclude Departed for initial headcount)
active = df[df["year"] == 2017]
active_counts = active["state"].value_counts().reindex(states, fill_value=0)
total_active = active_counts[states[:3]].sum()

# Express as proportions (include Departed = 0 initially)
s0 = np.array([
    active_counts["Assistant Prof"] / total_active,
    active_counts["Associate Prof"] / total_active,
    active_counts["Full Prof"] / total_active,
    0.0  # No one starts in Departed for our projection
])

print("Initial workforce distribution (proportions):")
for state, prop in zip(states, s0):
    print(f"  {state:18s}: {prop:.3f}")

# Convert estimated P to numpy array
P = P_estimated.values.copy()


# Project forward 20 years
n_projection = 20
projections = [s0.copy()]

for year in range(n_projection):
    s_next = projections[-1] @ P
    projections.append(s_next)

proj_df = pd.DataFrame(projections, columns=states)
proj_df["year"] = range(n_projection + 1)

print(f"\nProjected composition over {n_projection} years:")
print(proj_df.round(3).to_string(index=False))


# Visualize projections
fig = go.Figure()

for i, state in enumerate(states):
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=proj_df["year"],
        y=proj_df[state],
        mode="lines+markers",
        name=state,
        line=dict(color=wong[i], width=2),
        marker=dict(size=5)
    ))

fig.update_layout(
    title="Projected Workforce Composition Over 20 Years",
    xaxis_title="Years from Now",
    yaxis_title="Proportion of Original Cohort",
    yaxis_tickformat=".0%",
    legend_title="State",
    height=450
)
fig.show()







TipIn R





library(expm)  # for matrix power

# Initial distribution
s0 <- c(0.50, 0.30, 0.20, 0.00)

# Project 10 years
P_mat <- as.matrix(P_estimated)
s10 <- s0 %*% (P_mat %^% 10)
print(s10)

# Or iteratively
s <- s0
for (i in 1:20) {
  s <- s %*% P_mat
  cat(sprintf("Year %2d: %s\n", i, paste(round(s, 3), collapse = ", ")))
}

title: Markov Chain Workforce Modeling
text:
Steady-State Analysis
The steady-state distribution is the long-run equilibrium: the workforce composition the system converges to regardless of starting conditions. It is found by solving:
\[\boldsymbol{\pi} = \boldsymbol{\pi} \cdot P\]
For chains with an absorbing state (like “Departed”), all probability eventually flows into that absorbing state. So we first analyze the steady state of the non-absorbing sub-chain (the transient states only), then separately examine time-to-absorption.

# For the absorbing chain, the long run is trivial: everyone departs.
# Let's find the steady state of the *transient* sub-chain
# by renormalizing rows after removing the Departed column.

transient_states = states[:3]  # Assistant, Associate, Full
P_transient = P[:3, :3].copy()

# Renormalize rows (redistribute departure probability)
row_sums = P_transient.sum(axis=1, keepdims=True)
P_renorm = P_transient / row_sums

print("Renormalized Transition Matrix (no departures):")
print(pd.DataFrame(P_renorm, index=transient_states, columns=transient_states).round(3))


# Find steady state by eigenvalue decomposition
eigenvalues, eigenvectors = np.linalg.eig(P_renorm.T)

# Steady state corresponds to eigenvalue = 1
idx = np.argmin(np.abs(eigenvalues - 1.0))
steady = np.real(eigenvectors[:, idx])
steady = steady / steady.sum()  # Normalize

print("Steady-State Distribution (ignoring departures):")
for state, prop in zip(transient_states, steady):
    print(f"  {state:18s}: {prop:.3f} ({prop*100:.1f}%)")


# Verify by iterating many times
s_check = np.array([1/3, 1/3, 1/3])
for _ in range(500):
    s_check = s_check @ P_renorm

print("\nVerification (500 iterations from uniform start):")
for state, prop in zip(transient_states, s_check):
    print(f"  {state:18s}: {prop:.3f}")


Interpreting the Steady State
The steady-state vector tells you the long-run equilibrium workforce mix, assuming constant transition rates and ignoring departures. For example, if the steady state is roughly [0.25, 0.30, 0.45], it means the university’s faculty would naturally tend toward 25% Assistant, 30% Associate, and 45% Full Professors.
If your current composition differs significantly from the steady state, the system is “out of equilibrium” and will drift toward it over time – unless policies change the transition rates.

title: Markov Chain Workforce Modeling
text:
Absorbing States: Time to Departure
With “Departed” as an absorbing state, we can compute the expected time to absorption (average years until departure) from each transient state using the fundamental matrix:
\[N = (I - Q)^{-1}\]
where \(Q\) is the sub-matrix of transient-to-transient transition probabilities.

# Q is the transient sub-matrix (transitions among non-departed states)
Q = P[:3, :3].copy()

print("Transient sub-matrix Q:")
print(pd.DataFrame(Q, index=transient_states, columns=transient_states).round(3))

# Fundamental matrix
I = np.eye(len(transient_states))
N = np.linalg.inv(I - Q)

print("\nFundamental Matrix N = (I - Q)^(-1):")
print(pd.DataFrame(N, index=transient_states, columns=transient_states).round(2))

# Expected time to absorption = row sums of N
expected_years = N.sum(axis=1)
print("\nExpected Years Until Departure:")
for state, yrs in zip(transient_states, expected_years):
    print(f"  Starting as {state:18s}: {yrs:.1f} years")


# Visualize expected career duration
fig = px.bar(
    x=transient_states,
    y=expected_years,
    color=transient_states,
    color_discrete_sequence=wong,
    title="Expected Years Until Departure by Starting Rank",
    labels={"x": "Starting Rank", "y": "Expected Years"}
)

fig.update_layout(showlegend=False, yaxis_title="Expected Years")
fig.show()

title: Markov Chain Workforce Modeling
text:
Sensitivity Analysis: Changing Promotion Rates
A key strength of Markov models is the ability to ask “what if?” questions. How does the long-run workforce composition change if promotion rates increase or decrease?

# Vary the Assistant -> Associate promotion rate
promo_rates = np.arange(0.05, 0.36, 0.03)
sensitivity_results = []

for promo_rate in promo_rates:
    # Adjust the transition matrix
    P_mod = P.copy()
    # Assistant Prof row: adjust promotion and retention
    departure_rate = P_mod[0, 3]  # Keep departure rate fixed
    P_mod[0, 1] = promo_rate
    P_mod[0, 0] = 1.0 - promo_rate - departure_rate  # Retention adjusts

    # Renormalize transient sub-chain
    Q_mod = P_mod[:3, :3]
    Q_renorm = Q_mod / Q_mod.sum(axis=1, keepdims=True)

    # Find steady state
    evals, evecs = np.linalg.eig(Q_renorm.T)
    idx = np.argmin(np.abs(evals - 1.0))
    ss = np.real(evecs[:, idx])
    ss = ss / ss.sum()

    for j, state in enumerate(transient_states):
        sensitivity_results.append({
            "promotion_rate": promo_rate,
            "state": state,
            "steady_state_pct": ss[j] * 100
        })

sens_df = pd.DataFrame(sensitivity_results)

fig = px.line(
    sens_df,
    x="promotion_rate",
    y="steady_state_pct",
    color="state",
    color_discrete_sequence=wong,
    markers=True,
    title="Sensitivity: Steady-State Composition vs. Assistant→Associate Promotion Rate",
    labels={
        "promotion_rate": "Assistant → Associate Promotion Rate",
        "steady_state_pct": "Steady-State Proportion (%)",
        "state": "Rank"
    }
)

fig.update_layout(
    xaxis_tickformat=".0%",
    yaxis_title="Steady-State Proportion (%)",
    height=450
)
fig.show()


# Sensitivity on departure rate for Full Professors
departure_rates = np.arange(0.04, 0.25, 0.02)
depart_results = []

for dep_rate in departure_rates:
    P_mod = P.copy()
    P_mod[2, 3] = dep_rate
    P_mod[2, 2] = 1.0 - dep_rate  # Full Prof retention adjusts

    # Expected time to absorption from each state
    Q_mod = P_mod[:3, :3]
    N_mod = np.linalg.inv(np.eye(3) - Q_mod)
    exp_years = N_mod.sum(axis=1)

    for j, state in enumerate(transient_states):
        depart_results.append({
            "full_prof_departure_rate": dep_rate,
            "starting_state": state,
            "expected_years": exp_years[j]
        })

dep_df = pd.DataFrame(depart_results)

fig = px.line(
    dep_df,
    x="full_prof_departure_rate",
    y="expected_years",
    color="starting_state",
    color_discrete_sequence=wong,
    markers=True,
    title="Sensitivity: Expected Career Duration vs. Full Prof Departure Rate",
    labels={
        "full_prof_departure_rate": "Full Professor Departure Rate",
        "expected_years": "Expected Years Until Departure",
        "starting_state": "Starting Rank"
    }
)

fig.update_layout(xaxis_tickformat=".0%", height=450)
fig.show()







TipIn R





# Sensitivity analysis in R
library(purrr)
library(ggplot2)

promo_rates <- seq(0.05, 0.35, by = 0.03)

results <- map_dfr(promo_rates, function(pr) {
  P_mod <- P_mat
  P_mod[1, 2] <- pr
  P_mod[1, 1] <- 1 - pr - P_mod[1, 4]

  Q <- P_mod[1:3, 1:3]
  Q_renorm <- Q / rowSums(Q)

  # Steady state via eigen decomposition
  ev <- eigen(t(Q_renorm))
  ss <- Re(ev$vectors[, 1])
  ss <- ss / sum(ss)

  tibble(
    promotion_rate = pr,
    state = c("Assistant", "Associate", "Full"),
    proportion = ss
  )
})

ggplot(results, aes(promotion_rate, proportion, color = state)) +
  geom_line() + geom_point() +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent)

title: Markov Chain Workforce Modeling
text:
Division-Level Transition Analysis
Markov models can also be applied to inter-division mobility. Let us estimate transition probabilities between academic divisions.

# Generate division-level movement data
np.random.seed(123)

divisions = ["Letters & Science", "Engineering", "Medicine", "Business"]
n_staff = 300
n_periods = 6

div_records = []
for emp_id in range(n_staff):
    current_div = np.random.choice(divisions)
    for year in range(2019, 2019 + n_periods):
        div_records.append({
            "employee_id": f"STAFF{emp_id:04d}",
            "year": year,
            "division": current_div
        })
        # Small probability of switching divisions
        if np.random.random() < 0.08:
            other_divs = [d for d in divisions if d != current_div]
            current_div = np.random.choice(other_divs)

div_df = pd.DataFrame(div_records)

# Estimate division transition matrix
div_df_sorted = div_df.sort_values(["employee_id", "year"])
div_df_sorted["next_division"] = div_df_sorted.groupby("employee_id")["division"].shift(-1)
div_transitions = div_df_sorted.dropna(subset=["next_division"])

div_counts = pd.crosstab(
    div_transitions["division"],
    div_transitions["next_division"]
).reindex(index=divisions, columns=divisions, fill_value=0)

div_P = div_counts.div(div_counts.sum(axis=1), axis=0)

print("Division Transition Matrix:")
print(div_P.round(3))


# Heatmap of division transitions
fig = px.imshow(
    div_P.values,
    x=divisions,
    y=divisions,
    text_auto=".3f",
    color_continuous_scale=[[0, "#FFFFFF"], [0.5, "#E69F00"], [1.0, "#D55E00"]],
    labels={"x": "To Division", "y": "From Division", "color": "Probability"},
    title="Inter-Division Transition Probabilities"
)

fig.update_layout(width=600, height=500)
fig.show()


PyCharm: Debugging Matrix Operations
When working with transition matrices, use PyCharm’s variable explorer to inspect array shapes and values. Set a breakpoint after computing P and hover over it, or add it to the Watches panel. Verify that each row sums to 1.0 – a row that does not sum to 1.0 indicates a data issue.

title: Markov Chain Workforce Modeling
text:
Exercise

Exercise 24: Workforce Projection with Policy Scenarios
Using the faculty rank transition data generated in this lesson:

Estimate the transition matrix from the generated data (already done above – use P_estimated)
Starting from a cohort of 100 Assistant Professors, project the workforce composition over 15 years
Compute the expected years until departure for each starting rank
Create a “fast-track promotion” scenario where the Assistant-to-Associate promotion rate doubles. Compare the 10-year projection to the baseline.
Visualize both scenarios side-by-side using a Plotly subplot figure
What is the steady-state rank distribution under each scenario?







NoteSolution






# 1. Transition matrix (reuse P_estimated from above)
P_base = P_estimated.values.copy()
print("Baseline Transition Matrix:")
print(pd.DataFrame(P_base, index=states, columns=states).round(3))

# 2. Project 100 Assistant Professors over 15 years
s0_cohort = np.array([1.0, 0.0, 0.0, 0.0])  # All start as Asst Prof
n_years_proj = 15

baseline_proj = [s0_cohort.copy()]
for _ in range(n_years_proj):
    baseline_proj.append(baseline_proj[-1] @ P_base)

baseline_df = pd.DataFrame(baseline_proj, columns=states)
baseline_df["year"] = range(n_years_proj + 1)
baseline_df["scenario"] = "Baseline"

# Scale to 100 people
for s in states:
    baseline_df[s] = baseline_df[s] * 100

print("\nBaseline Projection (headcount out of 100):")
print(baseline_df[["year"] + states].round(1).to_string(index=False))

# 3. Expected years until departure
Q_base = P_base[:3, :3]
N_base = np.linalg.inv(np.eye(3) - Q_base)
exp_base = N_base.sum(axis=1)

print("\nExpected Years Until Departure (Baseline):")
for state, yrs in zip(states[:3], exp_base):
    print(f"  {state:18s}: {yrs:.1f} years")

# 4. Fast-track scenario: double Asst -> Assoc promotion rate
P_fast = P_base.copy()
original_promo = P_fast[0, 1]
P_fast[0, 1] = min(original_promo * 2, 0.50)  # Cap at 50%
P_fast[0, 0] = 1.0 - P_fast[0, 1] - P_fast[0, 2] - P_fast[0, 3]

print(f"\nFast-Track: Asst→Assoc rate changed from {original_promo:.3f} to {P_fast[0,1]:.3f}")

fast_proj = [s0_cohort.copy()]
for _ in range(n_years_proj):
    fast_proj.append(fast_proj[-1] @ P_fast)

fast_df = pd.DataFrame(fast_proj, columns=states)
fast_df["year"] = range(n_years_proj + 1)
fast_df["scenario"] = "Fast-Track"

for s in states:
    fast_df[s] = fast_df[s] * 100

# 5. Side-by-side visualization
fig = make_subplots(
    rows=1, cols=2,
    subplot_titles=["Baseline Promotion Rates", "Fast-Track Promotion"],
    shared_yaxes=True
)

for i, state in enumerate(states):
    fig.add_trace(
        go.Scatter(
            x=baseline_df["year"], y=baseline_df[state],
            mode="lines+markers", name=state,
            line=dict(color=wong[i]),
            legendgroup=state, showlegend=True
        ),
        row=1, col=1
    )
    fig.add_trace(
        go.Scatter(
            x=fast_df["year"], y=fast_df[state],
            mode="lines+markers", name=state,
            line=dict(color=wong[i], dash="dash"),
            legendgroup=state, showlegend=False
        ),
        row=1, col=2
    )

fig.update_layout(
    title="Workforce Projection: Baseline vs Fast-Track (Cohort of 100)",
    height=450, width=900,
    yaxis_title="Number of Employees"
)
fig.update_xaxes(title_text="Years", row=1, col=1)
fig.update_xaxes(title_text="Years", row=1, col=2)
fig.show()

# 6. Steady-state comparison
scenarios = {"Baseline": P_base, "Fast-Track": P_fast}

print("\nSteady-State Distributions (ignoring departures):")
print("-" * 55)
for name, P_mat in scenarios.items():
    Q = P_mat[:3, :3]
    Q_renorm = Q / Q.sum(axis=1, keepdims=True)

    evals, evecs = np.linalg.eig(Q_renorm.T)
    idx = np.argmin(np.abs(evals - 1.0))
    ss = np.real(evecs[:, idx])
    ss = ss / ss.sum()

    print(f"\n  {name}:")
    for state, prop in zip(states[:3], ss):
        print(f"    {state:18s}: {prop:.1%}")

# Compare at year 10
print("\nYear-10 Comparison (out of 100):")
print(f"  {'State':<18s} {'Baseline':>10s} {'Fast-Track':>10s} {'Difference':>10s}")
for state in states:
    b = baseline_df.loc[baseline_df["year"] == 10, state].values[0]
    f = fast_df.loc[fast_df["year"] == 10, state].values[0]
    print(f"  {state:<18s} {b:10.1f} {f:10.1f} {f-b:+10.1f}")

title: Markov Chain Workforce Modeling
text:
Summary
In this lesson, you learned to apply Markov chains to workforce planning:

Transition matrices capture employee movement probabilities between states (ranks, divisions, or employment status)
Estimation from historical data uses cross-tabulation and row normalization
Matrix multiplication with NumPy projects future workforce composition: \(\mathbf{s}_{t+n} = \mathbf{s}_t \cdot P^n\)
Steady-state analysis reveals the long-run equilibrium the workforce tends toward
Absorbing states model permanent exits; the fundamental matrix \(N = (I - Q)^{-1}\) gives expected time to departure
Sensitivity analysis shows how changes in promotion or departure rates ripple through the system
Plotly heatmaps and Sankey diagrams make transition patterns immediately interpretable

Key takeaways for workforce planners:

Small changes in promotion rates can substantially shift long-run composition
The steady state may be far from the current workforce mix, signaling impending drift
Expected time-to-absorption helps forecast replacement hiring needs
Markov models assume stationary transition rates – revisit your estimates as policies change

title: Markov Chain Workforce Modeling
text:
Next Steps
Continue to Lesson 25: Peer Institution Salary Comparison to learn how to benchmark UW-Madison compensation against comparable institutions.
